syllabus_desc: Explain the basic characteristics of an electrical circuit.
concept: An electrical circuit's basically a complete path where current flows from the power source, through the load (your equipment), and back. You've got three key bits: the source (like your battery), the conductors (wiring), and the load (whatever uses the power). Current only flows when you've got a complete path—break it anywhere and nothing happens. Think of it like water flowing through pipes; if you block it, flow stops.
exam_tip: Remember: circuit must be complete for current to flow. Examiners love asking what happens when you open a switch.
key_facts: Complete conducting path required
Current flows from positive to negative terminal
Open circuit = no current flow
Series vs parallel affects total resistance

syllabus_desc: State Ohm’s law.
concept: Ohm's law is the foundation of everything electrical in your aircraft: Voltage (V) equals Current (I) times Resistance (R). Written as V = IR. Think of it like water flowing through a pipe—voltage is the pressure pushing the water, current is the flow rate, and resistance is how narrow the pipe is. If you increase the voltage (more pressure), you get more current (more flow). If you increase the resistance (narrower pipe), you get less current for the same voltage. Rearrange it three ways: V = IR, I = V/R, R = V/I. You'll use this constantly when thinking about why a circuit behaves the way it does. In your helicopter's 28-volt system, if a landing light has 14 ohms resistance, it draws 2 amps (28/14 = 2). Simple maths, but it underpins every electrical calculation you'll ever do.
exam_tip: Know all three rearrangements: V = IR, I = V/R, R = V/I. They'll give you a scenario with two values and ask for the third. Units: volts, amps, ohms.
key_facts: Ohm's law: V = IR (Voltage = Current × Resistance). Three forms: V = IR, I = V/R, R = V/I. Voltage measured in volts (V). Current measured in amperes (A). Resistance measured in ohms (Ω). Higher voltage = more current (for same resistance). Higher resistance = less current (for same voltage).

syllabus_desc: Describe the terms: (a) electromotive force (volts)
concept: Electromotive force (EMF) is the electrical 'pressure' that pushes electrons around a circuit, measured in volts. Think of it like the pressure a pump creates in a water system. Your aircraft battery produces an EMF of 12 or 24 volts, while the alternator typically outputs 14 or 28 volts to keep the battery topped up.
exam_tip: EMF is the voltage the source produces -- it's the 'push', not the flow itself.
key_facts: EMF is the electrical pressure or potential difference measured in volts
A 12V battery system uses a 14V alternator; a 24V system uses a 28V alternator
EMF is generated by chemical reaction (battery) or electromagnetic induction (alternator)

syllabus_desc: Describe the terms: (b) current (amperes)
concept: Current is the actual flow of electrons through a conductor, measured in amperes (amps). If EMF is the pressure, current is the flow rate. The more amps flowing, the more electrical energy is being delivered. Your starter motor draws a huge current (hundreds of amps for a short burst), while your nav lights draw very little.
exam_tip: Current (amps) is the rate of electron flow -- more current means more energy being consumed by the load.
key_facts: Current is measured in amperes (A) and represents electron flow
High-draw items: starter motor, landing light; low-draw items: nav lights, radios
Current flows from negative to positive (electron flow) but conventional flow is positive to negative

syllabus_desc: Describe the terms: (c) resistance (ohms)
concept: Resistance is the opposition to current flow in a circuit, measured in ohms. Every conductor has some resistance -- thicker, shorter wires have less, while thinner, longer wires have more. Resistance converts electrical energy into heat, which is why your landing light gets hot and why undersized wiring is a fire hazard.
exam_tip: Resistance (ohms) opposes current flow -- and it increases with temperature in most conductors.
key_facts: Resistance is measured in ohms and opposes electron flow
Resistance increases with wire length and decreases with wire cross-sectional area
Ohm's Law: V = I x R (voltage = current x resistance)

syllabus_desc: Describe the structure of a: (a) two-wire circuit
concept: A two-wire circuit uses separate conductors for both the supply path and the return path from the power source to the load. Each wire carries current in opposite directions, forming a complete loop. This is the simplest circuit arrangement but doubles the wiring needed.
exam_tip: Two-wire circuits use dedicated supply and return conductors -- more wiring but no reliance on the airframe.
key_facts: Two-wire circuits have separate supply and return conductors
More reliable but heavier due to additional wiring
Used where electrical isolation from the airframe is required

syllabus_desc: Describe the structure of a: (b) grounded 'earth return' electrical circuit
concept: A grounded or 'earth return' circuit uses the aircraft's metal airframe as the return path instead of a second wire. One side of each component is connected ('earthed') to the airframe, and current flows back to the source through the metal structure. This halves the wiring needed and is standard in most light aircraft.
exam_tip: Earth return systems use the metal airframe as the return conductor -- less wiring but requires good bonding.
key_facts: The airframe acts as the return conductor, reducing wiring weight and complexity
Both the battery and alternator are earthed to the airframe on one side
Requires proper bonding of all metal components to maintain a low-resistance return path

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between direct and alternating current.
concept: Direct current, or DC, flows one way only—from positive to negative—which is what your battery provides. Alternating current, or AC, constantly reverses direction, changing polarity back and forth. Your aircraft runs on DC from the battery and alternator, but some older systems or test equipment might produce AC. AC's actually generated more easily in alternators, then we convert it to DC with a rectifier because that's what the aircraft electrical system needs.
exam_tip: DC = one direction always (battery/alternator output). AC = constantly reversing (found in alternators before rectification). Know why aircraft prefer DC.
key_facts: DC = unidirectional flow (positive to negative)
AC = bidirectional, constantly reversing
Aircraft electrical systems are DC-based
Alternators generate AC, then it's rectified to DC

syllabus_desc: Explain the terms: (a) frequency (Hertz)
concept: Frequency is the number of complete cycles an alternating current (AC) completes per second, measured in Hertz (Hz). One Hz means one full cycle per second. Aircraft AC systems typically run at 400 Hz (compared to household 50 Hz in NZ), which allows lighter transformers and motors.
exam_tip: Aircraft AC frequency is 400 Hz -- much higher than domestic 50 Hz, allowing lighter electrical components.
key_facts: Frequency is measured in Hertz (Hz) = cycles per second
NZ domestic supply is 50 Hz; aircraft AC systems typically use 400 Hz
Higher frequency allows smaller, lighter transformers and motors

syllabus_desc: Explain the terms: (b) rectification
concept: Rectification is the process of converting alternating current (AC) into direct current (DC). An alternator naturally produces AC, but most aircraft systems run on DC, so the output is rectified using diodes. A diode acts as a one-way valve for electricity, only allowing current to flow in one direction.
exam_tip: Rectification converts AC to DC using diodes -- this is why your alternator can charge a DC battery.
key_facts: Rectification converts AC to DC using semiconductor diodes
Diodes allow current flow in one direction only
Aircraft alternators have built-in rectifier assemblies to produce DC output

syllabus_desc: Explain the properties of magnetism, including: (a) polarity
concept: Magnetic polarity refers to the two distinct ends of a magnet -- the north-seeking pole and the south-seeking pole. Every magnet has both poles; you can't have one without the other. Cut a magnet in half and you get two complete magnets, each with its own north and south pole.
exam_tip: Every magnet has two poles -- you cannot isolate a single pole no matter how you divide the magnet.
key_facts: All magnets have a north pole and a south pole
Cutting a magnet creates two smaller complete magnets
The Earth itself is a large magnet with magnetic poles near (but not at) the geographic poles

syllabus_desc: Explain the properties of magnetism, including: (b) attraction
concept: Magnetic attraction occurs between unlike poles -- a north pole attracts a south pole and vice versa. This is the same principle that makes a compass needle align with the Earth's magnetic field. In aircraft, magnetic attraction is used in relays, solenoids, and of course the magnetic compass.
exam_tip: Unlike poles attract -- north attracts south, and this is the basis of compass operation.
key_facts: Unlike magnetic poles (N-S) attract each other
The force of attraction increases as poles get closer together
Compass needles are attracted toward the Earth's magnetic poles

syllabus_desc: Explain the properties of magnetism, including: (c) repulsion
concept: Magnetic repulsion occurs between like poles -- two north poles or two south poles push each other away. The repulsive force follows the same inverse-square relationship as attraction: double the distance and the force drops to a quarter.
exam_tip: Like poles repel -- two norths or two souths push apart, and the force weakens rapidly with distance.
key_facts: Like magnetic poles (N-N or S-S) repel each other
Repulsive force decreases with the square of the distance
Repulsion is used in some electric motor designs

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between: (a) temporary and permanent magnets
concept: Temporary magnets lose their magnetism quickly once the magnetising force is removed, while permanent magnets retain theirs indefinitely. A screwdriver touched to a magnet becomes temporarily magnetic. Aircraft magnetos use permanent magnets to generate the spark, while electromagnets in relays and solenoids are temporary -- they only work when current flows.
exam_tip: Permanent magnets retain magnetism; temporary magnets only stay magnetic while the magnetising force is applied.
key_facts: Permanent magnets retain magnetism for years -- used in magnetos and compasses
Temporary magnets lose magnetism when the external field is removed
Electromagnets are temporary magnets controlled by switching current on and off

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between: (b) the properties of 'soft iron' and 'hard iron' magnets
concept: Soft iron is easily magnetised but loses its magnetism quickly -- ideal for temporary magnets like transformer cores and relay armatures. Hard iron (steel) is difficult to magnetise but retains magnetism strongly once magnetised -- perfect for permanent magnets used in magnetos and compasses.
exam_tip: Soft iron = easy to magnetise, loses it quickly (temporary). Hard iron = hard to magnetise, keeps it (permanent).
key_facts: Soft iron: easily magnetised and demagnetised -- used in electromagnet cores
Hard iron (steel): difficult to magnetise but retains magnetism -- used in permanent magnets
Compass needles and magneto magnets are made from hard magnetic materials

syllabus_desc: Describe the terms: (a) magnetic field
concept: A magnetic field is the region of space around a magnet where magnetic forces can be detected. Field lines run from the north pole to the south pole outside the magnet, forming closed loops. The field is strongest where the lines are closest together -- near the poles.
exam_tip: Magnetic field lines run from north to south outside the magnet and are densest near the poles.
key_facts: A magnetic field is the zone of influence around a magnet
Field lines run from north to south outside the magnet
Field strength is greatest where lines of force are closest together

syllabus_desc: Describe the terms: (b) magnetic flux
concept: Magnetic flux is the total quantity of magnetic field lines passing through a given area. Think of it like the total flow of water through a pipe -- more flux means a stronger overall magnetic effect. Flux is measured in webers (Wb) and is the product of field strength and area.
exam_tip: Flux is the total number of field lines through an area -- measured in webers.
key_facts: Magnetic flux = total field lines through an area, measured in webers (Wb)
Flux density (measured in tesla) = flux per unit area
Changing flux through a conductor induces an EMF (Faraday's law)

syllabus_desc: Describe the terms: (c) permeability
concept: Permeability is a measure of how easily a material allows magnetic flux to pass through it. High-permeability materials like soft iron concentrate magnetic field lines and make excellent electromagnet cores. Air has low permeability, which is why placing an iron core inside a coil dramatically increases its magnetic strength.
exam_tip: Permeability measures how easily flux passes through a material -- soft iron has high permeability, air has low.
key_facts: Permeability = ease with which flux passes through a material
Soft iron has very high permeability -- used in transformer and relay cores
Air and non-ferrous metals have low permeability

syllabus_desc: Explain electromagnetism.
concept: When current flows through a conductor, it creates a magnetic field around it—that's electromagnetism. You can boost this field by wrapping the conductor into a coil; more turns mean stronger field. Add a soft iron core inside and you've got an electromagnet that's switchable; electricity on, you get magnetism; switch off, magnetism's gone. This principle powers your starter motor solenoid, relays, and ignition systems. It's the bridge between electricity and magnetism that makes modern aircraft electrical systems work.
exam_tip: Current through wire = magnetic field. Coil = stronger field. Iron core = even stronger. Switchable = that's why it's useful. Know how this enables relays and solenoids.
key_facts: Current produces magnetic field around conductor
Coil strengthens magnetic field
Iron core further increases field strength
Switchable nature enables relays and solenoids

syllabus_desc: Describe the lines of magnetic force around a: (a) straight conductor
concept: When current flows through a straight conductor, it creates a circular magnetic field around the wire. The field lines form concentric circles centred on the conductor. Use the right-hand grip rule: wrap your right hand around the wire with your thumb pointing in the direction of conventional current flow, and your fingers show the direction of the magnetic field.
exam_tip: Right-hand grip rule: thumb = current direction, curled fingers = magnetic field direction around the wire.
key_facts: Current through a straight wire creates concentric circular field lines
The right-hand grip rule gives field direction
Field strength decreases with distance from the conductor

syllabus_desc: Describe the lines of magnetic force around a: (b) current carrying coil
concept: A current-carrying coil (solenoid) produces a magnetic field pattern very similar to a bar magnet, with a clear north and south pole at each end. The field inside the coil is strong and uniform. Adding an iron core dramatically increases the field strength -- this is the basis of electromagnets used in relays, solenoids, and contactors throughout your aircraft.
exam_tip: A coil with current creates a field like a bar magnet -- adding an iron core multiplies the field strength.
key_facts: A current-carrying coil produces a magnetic field similar to a bar magnet
The field inside the coil is approximately uniform
An iron core increases field strength by concentrating flux (basis of electromagnets)

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of operation of an electromagnetic switch (or relay).
concept: An electromagnetic switch or relay's essentially a remote control for electrical circuits. Current flows through a coil, creating a magnetic field that attracts an iron armature, which moves and operates contacts—opening or closing circuits without anyone touching anything. You've got a low-current control circuit that operates a high-current load circuit, which is brilliant for aircraft where you can't run heavy cables to every switch. Your starter motor uses a relay; so does your landing gear system.
exam_tip: Low current through coil creates magnetic field, attracts armature, operates contacts. Know why relays are essential in aircraft electrical systems (remote operation, circuit isolation).
key_facts: Coil carries control current
Magnetic field attracts armature
Armature movement operates contacts
Separates control from load circuit

syllabus_desc: Describe typical examples of the use of electromagnetic switches in aircraft electrical circuits.
concept: You'll find electromagnetic switches throughout your aircraft. The starter solenoid's the obvious one—single switch in the cockpit controls a heavy-duty starter motor. Landing gear relays use electromagnets to divert power to retract or extend the gear. Landing light circuits often have relays so a small cockpit switch doesn't have to handle the full current. Fuel pump circuits, flap motors, heater fans—they all typically use relays. The beauty is you've got one small switch doing remote control work.
exam_tip: Be ready with specific examples: starter solenoid, landing gear relay, fuel pump relay. Explain why we don't just run heavy cables from cockpit switches.
key_facts: Starter motor solenoid
Landing gear relays
Landing light relays
Fuel pump control relays
Flap motor relays

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of electromagnetic induction.
concept: When you move a conductor through a magnetic field, or change the field around a conductor, you generate an electrical voltage. That's electromagnetic induction—Faraday's principle. The faster the field changes or the more field lines cut, the bigger the voltage generated. This is exactly how your alternator works: rotating magnets create a changing field through stationary coils, inducing voltage. It's also how your ignition magneto generates the high voltage for spark plugs, and how instrument pickups sense engine speed.
exam_tip: Movement or field change = induced voltage. Faster change = higher voltage. Know this is the basis for alternators and magnetos.
key_facts: Conductor moving through field generates voltage
Changing magnetic field induces voltage
Rate of change determines voltage magnitude
Foundation of alternator and magneto operation

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of operation of a simple alternator.
concept: A simple alternator's got a rotating magnetic field (rotor) inside stationary coils (stator). As the rotor spins, its magnetic field cuts through the stator coils, inducing AC voltage. The faster it spins, the higher the frequency. Your engine drives the alternator directly, usually through a belt. The frequency depends entirely on rotational speed and pole count. Modern aircraft alternators generate around 400 Hz at cruise RPM, which is then rectified to DC for the electrical system.
exam_tip: Rotating magnet, stationary coils, induced voltage. Higher RPM = higher frequency. This AC gets rectified to DC before reaching your battery.
key_facts: Rotor (magnet) rotates, stator (coil) stationary
Magnetic field change induces AC
Frequency increases with RPM
Output is AC before rectification

syllabus_desc: Describe: (a) the features of a practical alternator
concept: A practical alternator spins a magnetic field (the rotor) inside stationary coils (the stator). Because the output windings don't rotate, there's no need for brushes on the high-current output side -- just small slip rings to feed DC to the rotor's field winding. This makes alternators more reliable and lighter than generators.
exam_tip: Alternators spin the field (rotor) inside the stator windings -- no brushes on the output side means better reliability.
key_facts: The rotor carries the field winding; the stator carries the output windings
Slip rings feed a small DC current to the rotating field
Alternators reach rated voltage at low RPM and are lighter than generators

syllabus_desc: Describe: (b) how the AC output is normally rectified to provide DC
concept: An alternator naturally produces AC, but aircraft electrical systems run on DC. The AC output is converted to DC by a rectifier assembly containing diodes -- semiconductor devices that only allow current to flow one way. A full-wave rectifier uses multiple diodes to convert both halves of the AC cycle into a smooth DC output.
exam_tip: Alternator AC is rectified to DC by diodes in a full-wave rectifier -- both halves of the AC cycle are used.
key_facts: Diodes in the rectifier convert AC to DC by blocking reverse current flow
Full-wave rectification uses both halves of the AC waveform for smoother DC
The rectifier diodes also prevent battery current from flowing back into the alternator

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of operation of a simple generator.
concept: A simple DC generator's similar to an alternator, but instead of rectifying AC, it uses a commutator—a split ring on the rotor that switches connections as it rotates. This switching action reverses which coil half's connected to the output at exactly the right moment, so output's always DC. You get pulsing DC rather than the smooth output of an alternator, but it's DC nonetheless. The faster you spin it, the higher the voltage. Older aircraft and some backup systems used generators; most modern ones switched to alternators for better output characteristics.
exam_tip: Generator uses commutator to switch coil connections, producing DC. Alternator + rectifier vs. generator + commutator—both end with DC but different methods.
key_facts: Rotating coil in magnetic field
Commutator switches connections
Output is DC (pulsing)
Voltage increases with rotation speed
Less efficient than modern alternators

syllabus_desc: Describe: (a) the features of a practical generator
concept: A practical generator uses a permanent magnet to create a fixed magnetic field, and the output windings rotate inside this field. Because the output coils spin, brushes and a commutator are needed to collect the current. This makes generators heavier and less reliable than alternators, which is why alternators have largely replaced them.
exam_tip: Generators rotate the output windings in a fixed field -- brushes and commutator wear out over time.
key_facts: The field is provided by permanent magnets (fixed strength)
Output windings rotate inside the field
Brushes and commutator are required to collect current from the spinning windings

syllabus_desc: Describe: (b) how the output is passed through a commutator to provide DC
concept: A commutator is a mechanical switching device on the generator shaft that reverses the connections to the output windings every half turn. This converts the AC naturally produced by the rotating coils into DC. Carbon brushes press against the commutator segments to collect the current -- they wear over time and need periodic replacement.
exam_tip: The commutator mechanically switches connections each half-revolution to convert AC to pulsating DC.
key_facts: The commutator reverses connections every half rotation, producing DC output
Carbon brushes ride on the commutator to collect current
Brush and commutator wear is the main maintenance concern with generators

syllabus_desc: Explain: (a) the need for voltage regulation for both alternators and generators
concept: Both alternators and generators must maintain a constant output voltage regardless of RPM changes and varying electrical loads. A voltage regulator does this by adjusting the field current -- strengthening the field when voltage drops and weakening it when voltage rises. Without regulation, voltage would fluctuate and damage sensitive avionics.
exam_tip: Voltage regulators maintain constant system voltage by varying the field current -- stronger field raises output, weaker lowers it.
key_facts: Voltage regulators maintain constant system voltage (typically 14V or 28V)
They work by adjusting field current strength
Both alternators and generators need voltage regulation

syllabus_desc: Explain: (b) why a generator also requires a current regulator and a reverse current relay
concept: A generator needs a current regulator in addition to a voltage regulator because its permanent magnet field means output is primarily RPM-dependent. At high RPM, excessive current could overheat the windings. A generator also needs a reverse current relay (cut-out) to prevent the battery feeding current back into the generator at low RPM, which would turn it into a motor and flatten the battery.
exam_tip: Generators need three regulators: voltage regulator, current regulator, and reverse current relay -- alternators only need one.
key_facts: Current regulator prevents generator overheating at high RPM
Reverse current relay disconnects the generator when its output drops below battery voltage
Alternators don't need these because their diodes prevent reverse current flow automatically

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of a battery.
concept: A battery works through chemical reaction. Inside, you've got two different metals (terminals) separated by chemical electrolyte. Chemical action causes electrons to build up at one terminal (negative) and leave the other (positive), creating voltage. When you connect an external circuit, electrons flow from negative to positive, powering your equipment. The chemical reaction continues supplying this electron imbalance until the chemicals are spent. In a secondary cell (rechargeable), applying external current reverses the chemical reaction, restoring the battery. Your aircraft's lead-acid battery stores energy chemically; the alternator, via the regulator, chemically recharges it.
exam_tip: Chemical reaction = voltage difference. Electrons flow negative to positive. Chemical depleted = dead battery. Recharge = reverse the chemistry. Simple concept, understand it deeply.
key_facts: Chemical reaction creates voltage
Two different terminals (positive/negative)
Electrolyte enables reaction
Electrons flow negative to positive externally
Rechargeable batteries reverse chemical reaction

syllabus_desc: With respect to batteries, distinguish between: (a) primary and secondary cells
concept: Primary cells produce electricity from a one-way chemical reaction and cannot be recharged -- like a standard torch battery. Secondary cells use a reversible chemical reaction and can be recharged by passing current back through them. Aircraft batteries are secondary cells because they need to be recharged by the alternator after each engine start.
exam_tip: Primary = single-use (non-rechargeable). Secondary = rechargeable. Aircraft batteries are always secondary cells.
key_facts: Primary cells: non-rechargeable, one-way chemical reaction
Secondary cells: rechargeable, reversible chemical reaction
All aircraft batteries are secondary cells

syllabus_desc: With respect to batteries, distinguish between: (b) wet cells and dry cells
concept: Wet cells use a liquid electrolyte (acid or alkaline solution) that can spill if the battery is damaged or inverted. Dry cells use a paste or gel electrolyte that won't spill. Most aircraft lead-acid batteries are wet cells and must be installed upright with proper ventilation for the gases produced during charging.
exam_tip: Wet cells use liquid electrolyte and can spill; dry cells use paste/gel and are spill-proof.
key_facts: Wet cells: liquid electrolyte, must remain upright, need ventilation
Dry cells: paste or gel electrolyte, spill-resistant
Most light aircraft lead-acid batteries are wet-cell types

syllabus_desc: With respect to batteries, distinguish between: (c) lead-acid and nickel-cadmium (and similar) types
concept: Lead-acid batteries use lead plates in sulphuric acid and are common in light aircraft -- they're cheaper and provide high starting current. Nickel-cadmium (NiCad) batteries use nickel and cadmium plates in an alkaline (potassium hydroxide) electrolyte. NiCads are lighter, more robust, and perform better in cold weather, but cost more and are prone to thermal runaway if abused.
exam_tip: Lead-acid: cheaper, high starting current. NiCad: lighter, better cold performance, but thermal runaway risk.
key_facts: Lead-acid: sulphuric acid electrolyte, cheaper, high cranking amps
NiCad: alkaline electrolyte, lighter, better in cold, longer life
NiCad batteries are prone to thermal runaway if overcharged or damaged

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (a) battery capacity
concept: Battery capacity is the total amount of electrical energy a fully charged battery can deliver, measured in ampere-hours (Ah). A 36 Ah battery can supply 1 amp for 36 hours, or 36 amps for 1 hour. After an alternator failure, knowing your battery capacity tells you roughly how long you have before the battery goes flat -- so shed unnecessary electrical loads immediately.
exam_tip: Battery capacity in Ah tells you how long it will last -- a 36 Ah battery at 12A draw lasts about 3 hours.
key_facts: Capacity measured in ampere-hours (Ah)
36 Ah = 1A for 36 hours, or 6A for 6 hours, etc.
After alternator failure, reduce electrical load to extend battery endurance

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (b) thermal runaway
concept: Thermal runaway is a dangerous condition where a battery overheats and the heat causes it to accept more charging current, which generates more heat, creating a vicious cycle. It's most associated with NiCad batteries but can affect lead-acid types too. Signs include excessive battery temperature, swelling, and an acrid smell. If suspected, disconnect the charging source immediately.
exam_tip: Thermal runaway is a self-accelerating overheating cycle -- most common in NiCad batteries, disconnect the charger immediately.
key_facts: Thermal runaway: heat increases charging current, which increases heat further
Most common in NiCad batteries, especially if overcharged or in hot environments
Immediate action: disconnect the charging source and ventilate the battery area

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic features and operation of a: (a) lead-acid battery
concept: A lead-acid battery uses lead dioxide (positive) and spongy lead (negative) plates immersed in dilute sulphuric acid. During discharge, both plates convert to lead sulphate and the acid weakens. Recharging reverses this reaction. They produce hydrogen and oxygen gases when charging, so the battery compartment must be ventilated. Electrolyte specific gravity indicates charge state.
exam_tip: Lead-acid batteries produce hydrogen gas during charging -- ventilation is critical to prevent explosion risk.
key_facts: Lead plates in sulphuric acid electrolyte -- specific gravity indicates charge level
Produces hydrogen and oxygen during charging -- requires ventilation
Nominal voltage: 2V per cell (6 cells = 12V, 12 cells = 24V)

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic features and operation of a: (b) nickel-cadmium (NiCad) battery
concept: A NiCad battery uses nickel hydroxide (positive) and cadmium hydroxide (negative) plates in a potassium hydroxide (alkaline) electrolyte. NiCads maintain nearly constant voltage throughout discharge -- unlike lead-acid which drops gradually. They handle high discharge rates and cold weather well, but their constant voltage makes it harder to detect a low charge state, and they're susceptible to thermal runaway.
exam_tip: NiCad voltage stays nearly constant until almost flat -- harder to detect low charge compared to lead-acid.
key_facts: Alkaline electrolyte (potassium hydroxide) -- does not weaken during discharge
Voltage remains nearly constant until almost fully discharged
Nominal voltage: 1.2V per cell (requires more cells than lead-acid for same system voltage)

syllabus_desc: Explain the advantages of NiCad batteries.
concept: NiCad batteries are sealed—no water to leak or evaporate, so zero maintenance. They're lighter than equivalent lead-acid (important in Ardmore training flights where weight matters). They accept rapid charging without damage, so if you've got a slow alternator or quick turnaround between flights, NiCad keeps up. Temperature range is wider; they work in freezing Christchurch winters and hot Queenstown summers without significant performance loss. They handle deep discharge and recharge cycles better—lead-acid sulphates up and dies if regularly discharged below 50%, but NiCad goes to zero without damage. Lifespan is longer. The main disadvantage is cost, but many operators reckon the maintenance savings and reliability make it worthwhile.
exam_tip: Advantages: sealed, lightweight, accepts fast charge, wide temperature range, handles deep discharge, longer lifespan. Why training schools might prefer them despite cost.
key_facts: Sealed—no maintenance or water checks
Lighter than lead-acid
Accepts rapid charging safely
Wide operating temperature range
Handles deep discharge/recharge cycles
Longer service life

syllabus_desc: State Pascal’s principle.
concept: Pascal's principle is fundamental to understanding hydraulic systems in your helicopter. It states that when you apply pressure to a confined fluid, that pressure transmits equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid. Imagine a sealed container of oil with a piston on one end—push that piston and the pressure spreads evenly everywhere in the fluid, not just in the direction you're pushing. This is why hydraulic systems work so reliably: you can apply force at one point and get predictable, uniform pressure throughout. In your helicopter's hydraulic systems, whether you're operating the cyclic, collective, or flight control surfaces, you're relying on Pascal's principle. The fluid in the pipes doesn't compress (it's incompressible, unlike air), so that pressure transmits instantly and uniformly. That's what makes hydraulics so precise and responsive for flight controls.
exam_tip: Pascal's principle: pressure applied to confined fluid transmits equally in all directions. This is the foundation of why hydraulic systems work in helicopters.
key_facts: Pressure applied to confined fluid transmits equally in all directions
Fluid is essentially incompressible
Pressure acts perpendicular to all surfaces in contact with the fluid
Foundation principle for all hydraulic system operation

syllabus_desc: Describe mechanical advantage, and how it can be gained hydraulically.
concept: Mechanical advantage in hydraulic systems comes down to the sizes of your pistons. If you've got a small piston pushing on one side of a confined fluid and a larger piston on the other, the larger piston will move with greater force but less distance. The mechanical advantage equals the ratio of the areas of the two pistons. For example, if your input piston is 10 square centimetres and your output piston is 50 square centimetres, you've got a 5-to-1 mechanical advantage. Push with 100 newtons on the small piston and you get 500 newtons pushing on the large one. This is exactly how your helicopter's hydraulic control systems work—small control inputs at the cyclic or collective lever generate large forces on the rotor head or flight control surfaces. The trade-off is always distance: to move that larger piston the same distance, you'd need to move the small piston five times further. Understanding this piston area ratio is crucial for grasping why hydraulic systems give pilots such precise, high-force control with relatively small inputs.
exam_tip: Mechanical advantage = ratio of piston areas. Larger output piston = greater force but less distance. This is why hydraulics let you control heavy loads easily.
key_facts: Mechanical advantage = output piston area ÷ input piston area
Greater piston area ratio = greater force multiplication
Force multiplication comes at the cost of distance (inverse relationship)
Small control inputs generate large control forces in helicopter systems

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of aircraft hydraulic services.
concept: Aircraft hydraulic systems work on Pascal's principle—pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally in all directions. You've got a small master cylinder (the input) connected via fluid lines to a larger slave cylinder (the output). Press on the small piston, the fluid transmits that pressure to the large piston. Since the large piston has more area, it produces more force—that's your mechanical advantage. In your helicopter, hydraulic systems boost the pilot's control inputs. Without hydraulic assistance, the aerodynamic loads on a helicopter's main rotor controls would be too heavy for the pilot to move comfortably, especially in larger machines. The system uses an engine-driven pump to pressurise hydraulic fluid (typically MIL-H-5606 mineral-based fluid), a reservoir to store fluid, a filter to keep it clean, an accumulator to smooth pressure pulses and provide emergency reserve, and actuators at each control—collective, cyclic, and sometimes tail rotor pedals. The R22 is simple enough to fly without hydraulics (it's actually designed that way), but the R44 and larger helicopters rely on hydraulic boost for normal control feel.
exam_tip: Pascal's principle: pressure transmitted equally through confined fluid. Hydraulic advantage = larger output piston area. Components: pump, reservoir, filter, accumulator, actuators. R22 = no hydraulic boost. R44+ = hydraulic-assisted controls.
key_facts: Pascal's principle: pressure applied to confined fluid transmitted equally in all directions. Small input piston → large output piston = mechanical advantage. Components: engine-driven pump, reservoir, filter, accumulator, actuators. Hydraulic fluid: typically MIL-H-5606 mineral-based. Helicopter use: boosts control inputs for collective, cyclic, pedals. Without hydraulics: control loads too heavy in larger helicopters. R22: designed without hydraulic boost. R44 and larger: require hydraulic assistance.

syllabus_desc: Explain the fundamental difference between piston and gas turbine (jet) engines.
concept: Piston engines work through reciprocating motion—pistons go up and down, driving a crankshaft that ultimately turns your propeller. Fuel and air burn in discrete pulses inside cylinders, each pulse pushing a piston down. Gas turbine engines work continuously—air flows through a compressor, fuel burns steadily in a combustor, and hot expanding gases drive turbines. Piston engines are efficient at lower speeds (why we use them for general aviation training at places like Ardmore), thermally efficient because of high compression ratios, and simpler mechanically. Turbines produce thrust directly, no propeller needed, but are fuel-hungry at low speeds and need expensive materials. For training aircraft with moderate power requirements, piston's the sensible choice.
exam_tip: Piston = reciprocating pistons, pulses of combustion, propeller. Turbine = continuous flow, hot gas drives turbines, direct thrust. Know why piston's used in your training aircraft.
key_facts: Piston: reciprocating motion, discrete combustion pulses
Turbine: continuous flow, continuous combustion
Piston: efficient low-speed, high compression ratio
Turbine: direct thrust, fuel-hungry at low speed
Training aircraft use piston for efficiency

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between the basic types of piston aircraft engine.
concept: Aircraft piston engines come in several flavours. You've got in-line engines where cylinders line up in a row (older design, compact lengthwise but tall). Radial engines with cylinders arranged in a circle around a central crankshaft (old warbird types, rarely seen now). Flat or horizontally-opposed engines where cylinders are opposite each other (like your modern Lycoming or Continental—what you'll see in training twin at Hamilton or Christchurch). There's also V-engines with two banks of cylinders at an angle. For modern light aircraft, horizontally-opposed dominates because it's compact, good centre of gravity, and reasonable power-to-weight.
exam_tip: In-line, radial, flat, V-type. Know which one's in your aircraft. Most modern trainers = horizontally-opposed.
key_facts: In-line: cylinders in row
Radial: cylinders in circle around crankshaft
Flat/horizontally-opposed: opposite cylinders
V-type: two banks at angle
Modern trainers use horizontally-opposed

syllabus_desc: Explain the fundamental operating principle of a piston four-stroke cycle engine.
concept: Four-stroke's the standard. First stroke (intake): piston moves down, intake valve opens, fuel-air mixture fills the cylinder. Second stroke (compression): both valves close, piston compresses the mixture, temperature and pressure rise. Third stroke (power/expansion): spark ignites the mixture, explosion pushes the piston down hard, producing useful work. Fourth stroke (exhaust): piston rises, exhaust valve opens, burnt gases exit. Then the cycle repeats. Each cylinder fires in sequence, so on a four-cylinder engine with proper timing, there's always one cylinder on the power stroke, giving continuous smooth delivery of power to the crankshaft. This is your light twin at Ardmore.
exam_tip: Intake, compression, power, exhaust. Know what happens in each. Spark timing happens during compression stroke, ignition during power stroke. Understand the thermodynamic sequence.
key_facts: Intake: valve open, mixture in
Compression: both closed, pressure rises
Power: spark ignites, explosion drives piston
Exhaust: valve open, gases out
Two complete crankshaft rotations = one cycle

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: (a) cylinders
concept: Cylinders are the closed chambers where combustion takes place. Each cylinder consists of a barrel (the cylindrical tube) and a cylinder head bolted on top. The barrel guides the piston's up-and-down movement, while the head houses the valves, spark plugs, and ports. Most light aircraft engines are air-cooled, so the cylinders have cooling fins cast into their outer surface.
exam_tip: The cylinder is where combustion occurs -- barrel guides the piston, head contains valves and spark plugs.
key_facts: Cylinder = barrel + cylinder head
Houses the combustion chamber, valves, and spark plugs
Cooling fins on the outside dissipate heat to the airflow

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: (b) cylinder heads
concept: Cylinder heads form the top of the combustion chamber and contain the inlet and exhaust valves, spark plugs, and valve ports. They're the hottest part of the engine because they absorb combustion heat directly, which is why they have the largest cooling fins. Cylinder head temperature (CHT) is a critical engine monitoring parameter.
exam_tip: Cylinder heads are the hottest engine component -- they have the largest cooling fins and CHT must be monitored.
key_facts: Contains valves, spark plugs, and ports for gas flow
Hottest part of the engine -- largest cooling fins
CHT gauge monitors cylinder head temperature to prevent overheating

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: (c) pistons
concept: Pistons are plungers that move up and down inside the cylinder barrels. Combustion pressure acts on the piston crown, pushing it down on the power stroke. Piston rings seal the gap between the piston and barrel wall -- compression rings prevent gas leakage, and the oil ring prevents oil entering the combustion chamber while keeping the barrel lubricated.
exam_tip: Pistons have compression rings (seal combustion gases) and an oil ring (controls lubrication).
key_facts: Pistons convert combustion pressure into linear motion
Compression rings seal gases above the piston
Oil ring prevents oil from entering the combustion chamber

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: (d) connecting rods
concept: Connecting rods link each piston to the crankshaft, converting the piston's straight-line motion into rotary motion. The small end attaches to the piston via a wrist pin, and the big end attaches to the crankshaft journal. The big end bearing is critical -- it must withstand enormous forces and relies on pressurised oil for lubrication.
exam_tip: The connecting rod converts linear piston motion to rotary crankshaft motion -- small end to piston, big end to crankshaft.
key_facts: Small end connects to piston via wrist pin
Big end connects to crankshaft journal
Big end bearing requires high-pressure oil lubrication

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: (e) crankshaft
concept: The crankshaft is the backbone of the engine -- it converts the reciprocating motion of all the pistons into continuous rotary motion to drive the propeller. It's supported by main bearings and must be extremely strong. The crankshaft journals (offset sections) are where the connecting rods attach. Adequate lubrication of the main and big end bearings is vital.
exam_tip: The crankshaft converts reciprocating piston motion into rotation -- it directly drives the propeller.
key_facts: Converts linear piston motion to rotary motion
Supported by main bearings, connected to pistons via journals
Must be lubricated under high pressure -- loss of oil pressure is an emergency

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: (f) valves
concept: Valves control the flow of gas into and out of each cylinder. The inlet valve opens to admit the fuel-air mixture during the induction stroke, and the exhaust valve opens to expel burnt gases during the exhaust stroke. Valves are held closed by strong springs and opened by the rocker arm mechanism. The exhaust valve runs hotter than the inlet valve.
exam_tip: Exhaust valves run hotter than inlet valves -- the incoming mixture cools the inlet valve.
key_facts: Inlet valve admits fuel-air mixture; exhaust valve expels burnt gases
Valve springs hold valves closed; rocker arms push them open
Exhaust valve operates at higher temperature than inlet valve

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: (g) valve operating mechanism
concept: The valve operating mechanism translates camshaft rotation into valve opening and closing. It consists of cam lobes, push rods (or tappets), and rocker arms. As the camshaft turns, each cam lobe lifts its push rod, which pivots the rocker arm to push the valve open against its spring. When the lobe passes, the spring closes the valve.
exam_tip: The valve train sequence is: camshaft lobe -> push rod -> rocker arm -> valve opens against spring.
key_facts: Cam lobe lifts push rod, which pivots rocker arm to open valve
Valve spring returns valve to closed position
Valve timing is mechanically fixed by the camshaft profile

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: (h) camshaft
concept: The camshaft controls valve timing by rotating at half the crankshaft speed (in a four-stroke engine, each valve only needs to open once every two crankshaft revolutions). The shape of each cam lobe determines when the valve opens, how far it opens, and how long it stays open. The camshaft is gear-driven from the crankshaft.
exam_tip: The camshaft turns at half crankshaft speed -- each valve opens once per complete four-stroke cycle.
key_facts: Camshaft rotates at half crankshaft RPM
Cam lobe profile determines valve timing, lift, and duration
Gear-driven from the crankshaft to maintain precise timing

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: (i) spark plugs
concept: Spark plugs provide the electrical spark that ignites the compressed fuel-air mixture in each cylinder. Aircraft engines use dual ignition -- two spark plugs per cylinder fired by independent magnetos. This provides redundancy (if one system fails, the engine keeps running) and more complete combustion by igniting the mixture from two points.
exam_tip: Two spark plugs per cylinder for redundancy and better combustion -- each fired by a separate magneto.
key_facts: Two spark plugs per cylinder, each fired by an independent magneto
Dual ignition provides redundancy and more efficient combustion
Spark plug fouling (carbon or lead deposits) causes misfiring

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of the main components of a four-stroke cycle piston engine including: (j) injectors
concept: Fuel injectors spray metered fuel directly into the cylinder intake port or combustion chamber. In fuel-injected engines, injectors replace the carburettor. Each cylinder has its own injector, ensuring more even fuel distribution than a carburettor can achieve. This gives better fuel economy, smoother power delivery, and eliminates carburettor icing risk.
exam_tip: Injectors deliver metered fuel directly to each cylinder -- more even distribution than a carburettor.
key_facts: One injector per cylinder for precise fuel metering
Eliminates carburettor icing risk
Provides more even fuel distribution across all cylinders

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (a) cycle
concept: A cycle is one complete sequence of the four strokes -- induction, compression, power, and exhaust. It takes two full crankshaft revolutions (720 degrees) to complete one cycle. Named the Otto cycle after August Otto who developed it.
exam_tip: One complete four-stroke cycle = 4 strokes = 2 crankshaft revolutions (720 degrees).
key_facts: One cycle = induction + compression + power + exhaust
Requires two full crankshaft revolutions (720 degrees)
Named the Otto cycle after inventor August Otto

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (b) stroke
concept: A stroke is one complete movement of the piston from one end of its travel to the other -- either from TDC to BDC or BDC to TDC. There are four strokes per cycle: induction (down), compression (up), power (down), and exhaust (up). Only the power stroke produces useful work.
exam_tip: One stroke = piston moving from one end to the other. Only the power stroke produces work -- the other three consume it.
key_facts: A stroke is one full piston movement (TDC to BDC or BDC to TDC)
Four strokes per cycle: induction, compression, power, exhaust
Only the power stroke delivers energy; the others are 'maintained' by the flywheel

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (c) top dead centre (TDC)
concept: Top dead centre (TDC) is the position of the piston at the very top of its travel in the cylinder. At TDC, the combustion chamber volume is at its smallest. Ignition timing is referenced to TDC -- the spark fires a set number of degrees before TDC (BTDC) to allow time for combustion.
exam_tip: TDC is the top of piston travel -- ignition occurs before TDC (typically 20-28 degrees BTDC).
key_facts: TDC = piston at the top of its stroke
Combustion chamber volume is minimum at TDC
Ignition timing is expressed as degrees before TDC (BTDC)

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (d) bottom dead centre (BDC)
concept: Bottom dead centre (BDC) is the position of the piston at the very bottom of its travel. At BDC, the cylinder volume is at its maximum. The exhaust valve typically opens before BDC on the power stroke (about 55 degrees BBDC) because little useful work is being done in the final portion of the power stroke.
exam_tip: BDC is the bottom of piston travel -- the exhaust valve opens well before BDC to start clearing burnt gases.
key_facts: BDC = piston at the bottom of its stroke
Cylinder volume is maximum at BDC
Exhaust valve opens approximately 55 degrees before BDC

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (e) bore
concept: Bore is the internal diameter of the cylinder barrel. Along with stroke length, bore determines the swept volume (displacement) of the engine. Larger bore means a bigger piston area for combustion pressure to act on, producing more force per power stroke.
exam_tip: Bore is the cylinder's internal diameter -- it determines piston area and contributes to engine displacement.
key_facts: Bore = internal diameter of the cylinder
Larger bore = larger piston area = more force per power stroke
Bore x stroke determines swept volume

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (f) clearance volume
concept: Clearance volume is the space remaining above the piston when it's at TDC -- the volume of the combustion chamber itself. The charge is compressed into this small space before ignition. Clearance volume directly affects the compression ratio.
exam_tip: Clearance volume is the combustion chamber space at TDC -- smaller clearance volume means higher compression ratio.
key_facts: Clearance volume = space above piston at TDC
This is where the compressed charge is ignited
Determines the compression ratio along with swept volume

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (g) swept volume
concept: Swept volume (displacement) is the volume the piston sweeps through as it moves from TDC to BDC in one stroke. It's calculated from the bore and stroke: swept volume = piston area x stroke length. Total engine displacement is the swept volume of one cylinder multiplied by the number of cylinders.
exam_tip: Swept volume = bore area x stroke length. Total displacement = swept volume x number of cylinders.
key_facts: Swept volume = volume displaced by piston from TDC to BDC
Calculated as piston area x stroke length
Total engine displacement = single cylinder swept volume x number of cylinders

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (h) compression ratio
concept: Compression ratio is the ratio of total cylinder volume (swept + clearance) to clearance volume alone. A typical aircraft engine has a compression ratio around 7:1 to 8.5:1. Higher compression ratios extract more energy from the fuel but increase the risk of detonation, which is why aircraft engines must use the correct fuel grade.
exam_tip: Compression ratio = (swept volume + clearance volume) / clearance volume. Typical aircraft engines: 7:1 to 8.5:1.
key_facts: CR = total volume / clearance volume
Typical aircraft piston engines: 7:1 to 8.5:1
Higher CR means more power but greater detonation risk -- correct fuel grade is essential

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (i) firing interval
concept: Firing interval is the number of degrees of crankshaft rotation between successive power strokes across the engine. For a four-cylinder engine with a four-stroke cycle: 720 degrees / 4 cylinders = 180 degrees between power strokes, giving evenly spaced firing. A six-cylinder engine fires every 120 degrees for even smoother power delivery.
exam_tip: Firing interval = 720 degrees / number of cylinders. Four-cylinder = 180 degrees; six-cylinder = 120 degrees.
key_facts: Firing interval = 720 degrees / number of cylinders
Four-cylinder engine: 180-degree firing interval
Even firing intervals produce smoother power delivery

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (j) firing order
concept: Firing order is the specific sequence in which the cylinders fire. It's designed to distribute power strokes evenly around the crankshaft and minimise vibration. A typical four-cylinder Lycoming fires 1-3-2-4 or 1-4-2-3. The firing order is fixed by the engine design and cannot be changed.
exam_tip: Firing order is fixed by engine design -- it distributes power impulses evenly to minimise vibration.
key_facts: Firing order determines the sequence of power strokes
Designed to distribute loads evenly and minimise vibration
Common four-cylinder Lycoming order: 1-3-2-4

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (k) manifolds
concept: Manifolds are the ducting that distributes gases to and from the cylinders. The inlet (or induction) manifold carries the fuel-air mixture from the carburettor or throttle body to each cylinder. The exhaust manifold collects burnt gases from each cylinder and directs them overboard. Even distribution through the inlet manifold is important for smooth running.
exam_tip: Inlet manifold distributes mixture to cylinders; exhaust manifold collects burnt gases. Even distribution matters.
key_facts: Inlet manifold: carries fuel-air mixture from carburettor/throttle to cylinders
Exhaust manifold: collects and directs burnt gases overboard
Uneven inlet distribution causes rough running and power variation between cylinders

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (l) manifold pressure
concept: Manifold pressure (MAP) is the air pressure inside the inlet manifold, measured in inches of mercury (inHg). It indicates how much air (and therefore fuel-air mixture) is being delivered to the cylinders. At full throttle, MAP is highest; at idle, it's lowest. MAP is a direct indicator of engine power output.
exam_tip: MAP indicates power output -- higher MAP means more mixture entering the cylinders and more power.
key_facts: MAP is measured in inches of mercury (inHg)
Higher MAP = more air/fuel entering cylinders = more power
MAP gauge is essential for power management, especially with constant-speed propellers

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (m) crank angle
concept: Crank angle refers to the angular position of the crankshaft measured in degrees from a reference point (usually TDC). All valve timing and ignition timing events are expressed as crank angles. For example, ignition might occur at 25 degrees BTDC, and the exhaust valve might open at 55 degrees BBDC.
exam_tip: Crank angle measures crankshaft position in degrees -- all engine timing events reference crank angle from TDC.
key_facts: Measured in degrees from TDC (or BDC)
Used to express ignition timing (e.g., 25 degrees BTDC)
Used to express valve timing events (e.g., exhaust opens 55 degrees BBDC)

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (a) valve lag
concept: Valve lag means the valve closes after the piston reaches the dead centre point. For example, the inlet valve closes after BDC (about 60 degrees ABDC) to take advantage of the inertia of the incoming charge -- the mixture keeps flowing in even after the piston starts moving up. Similarly, the exhaust valve closes after TDC.
exam_tip: Valve lag = valve closing delayed past dead centre. Inlet closes after BDC; exhaust closes after TDC.
key_facts: Valve lag: valve closure is delayed beyond the dead centre position
Inlet valve closes after BDC to use charge inertia (about 60 degrees ABDC)
Exhaust valve closes after TDC to allow continued gas outflow

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (b) valve lead
concept: Valve lead means the valve opens before the piston reaches the dead centre point. The inlet valve opens before TDC (about 15 degrees BTDC) so it's already open when the induction stroke begins. The exhaust valve opens well before BDC (about 55 degrees BBDC) because little useful power is produced in the last part of the power stroke.
exam_tip: Valve lead = valve opening early before dead centre. Exhaust opens before BDC; inlet opens before TDC.
key_facts: Valve lead: valve opens before the dead centre position
Inlet valve opens about 15 degrees before TDC
Exhaust valve opens about 55 degrees before BDC

syllabus_desc: With respect to a four-stroke piston engine, explain the meaning of the following terms: (c) valve overlap
concept: Valve overlap is the brief period near TDC when both the inlet and exhaust valves are open simultaneously. It occurs because the exhaust valve hasn't closed yet (lag) while the inlet valve has already opened (lead). During overlap, the outflowing exhaust gases help draw fresh charge into the cylinder. The overlap period is typically about 30 degrees of crank angle.
exam_tip: Valve overlap occurs near TDC when both valves are briefly open -- the exiting exhaust helps draw in fresh charge.
key_facts: Both valves open simultaneously near TDC for about 30 degrees
Exhaust gas inertia helps scavenge spent gases and draw in fresh mixture
Overlap improves volumetric efficiency at cruise RPM

syllabus_desc: Explain the advantages of valve overlap.
concept: Valve overlap improves cylinder scavenging—removing residual burnt gases so the next intake stroke can fill with fresh mixture. When both valves are open briefly and the piston's near BDC, the outgoing exhaust creates pressure that pushes out the last bits of burnt gas, and the negative pressure from the descending piston draws fresh mixture in. This overlap creates a natural 'sweeping' action that improves gas exchange. Better scavenging means more fresh mixture per cycle, which means more power. Better filling means the engine operates more efficiently across the RPM range. Modern engines' camshaft profiles are optimised for this overlap; a wider overlap helps engine breathing at higher RPMs, though it can cause issues at very low idle speeds if not compensated for. That's why idle's rougher on some high-performance engines.
exam_tip: Advantages: improved scavenging, better cylinder filling, increased power output, better breathing at altitude. Know why engines with wide overlap might have rough idle.
key_facts: Improves scavenging of exhaust gases
Improves fresh mixture intake
Increases power output
Better engine breathing at altitude
Can cause rough idle if excessive

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the term ignition timing, and the need for spark advance.
concept: Ignition timing is when the spark occurs relative to the piston position. You can't wait until TDC to spark—combustion takes a few milliseconds to develop, and you need maximum pressure right after TDC (when the piston's driving downward). So spark happens before TDC, called 'spark advance.' Typically 20-30 degrees before TDC depending on the engine and operating conditions. At takeoff with high power, you want more advance; at cruise, less. Some older engines have manual advance/retard controls; modern ones adjust automatically. Spark too early and you get detonation (pre-ignition). Spark too late and combustion finishes after the power stroke starts, wasting energy. Getting it right is critical for smooth, efficient operation.
exam_tip: Spark at TDC? No, too late. Spark before TDC = spark advance. Why? Combustion needs time to build pressure. Understand the timing relationship to piston motion.
key_facts: Ignition timing = spark position relative to piston
Spark advance = occurs before TDC
Typical 20-30 degrees before TDC
Combustion takes milliseconds to develop
Too early = detonation, too late = power loss

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between detonation and pre-ignition.
concept: Detonation and pre-ignition are two different abnormal combustion conditions, and you need to distinguish between them clearly. Detonation happens when the end gases—the fuel-air mixture at the far end of the combustion chamber—auto-ignite from the heat and pressure created by the normal flame front spreading from the spark plug. The pressure and temperature rise to the point where the remaining unburnt gas spontaneously ignites instead of burning in a controlled way. You'll hear a characteristic metallic knock or ping. Pre-ignition, by contrast, is when the fuel-air mixture ignites before the spark plug fires, triggered by a hot spot in the combustion chamber—a badly eroded piston, carbon deposit, or overheated valve. The timing's different, the cause is different, but both cause the same kind of damage. Both generate excessive pressure spikes that can crack pistons, damage bearing surfaces, and trash valves. In the air, if you suspect either condition, you've got to lean the mixture, reduce power, increase airspeed for cooling, or switch to a lower power setting immediately.
exam_tip: Detonation: end gases auto-ignite from pressure/heat during normal combustion. Pre-ignition: fuel-air mixture ignites before spark from hot spot. Both damage pistons and bearings.
key_facts: Detonation: unburnt end gases auto-ignite from pressure/temperature
Pre-ignition: mixture ignites before spark plug fires from hot spot
Detonation causes characteristic metallic knock/ping
Both conditions cause piston cracking, bearing damage, valve damage
Lean mixture, reduce power, increase airspeed to manage both conditions

syllabus_desc: Explain the main causes of detonation (pre-ignition) including the reasons for avoiding them.
concept: The main causes of detonation (which is sometimes called pre-ignition by pilots, though technically that's different) centre on conditions that raise combustion temperature and pressure excessively. High manifold air pressure—pushing more air into the engine—increases the pressure and temperature when that mixture ignites, increasing the risk of the end gases auto-igniting. A lean fuel-air mixture burns hotter than a rich one, raising combustion temperature dangerously. High ambient temperature or a hot engine already heat-soaked makes it worse. Low-octane fuel has a lower resistance to auto-ignition, so using fuel below your engine's rating invites trouble. Spark advance that's too aggressive—igniting the mixture too early—means the pressure and temperature rise to dangerous levels before the piston's in the right position. Accumulated carbon deposits in the combustion chamber act as hot spots and raise compression, pushing temperature higher. The damage from detonation is severe: you can crack pistons, destroy bearing surfaces, burn valves, and wreck your engine in minutes. In the air, if you suspect detonation, lean the mixture to cool combustion, reduce power to lower manifold pressure, increase airspeed to cool the engine, and use higher-octane fuel if available.
exam_tip: Causes of detonation: high MAP, lean mixture, high temps, low octane, spark advance too far. Damage includes piston cracking, bearing failure, valve damage.
key_facts: High manifold air pressure increases combustion pressure/temperature
Lean mixture burns hotter, increasing detonation risk
High engine temperature and high ambient temperature increase risk
Low-octane fuel has lower auto-ignition resistance
Spark advance that's too aggressive increases combustion pressure
Detonation causes piston cracking, bearing damage, valve damage
Management: lean mixture, reduce power, increase airspeed, use higher octane

syllabus_desc: Explain diesel (compression ignition) knock (detonation).
concept: Diesel engines (compression ignition engines) operate completely differently from petrol engines, and so does the problem of diesel knock. In a compression-ignition engine, there's no spark plug. Instead, you compress the air so heavily that the heat of compression alone—typically 500-600°C—ignites the fuel when it's injected into the combustion chamber. The problem arises if there's too long a delay between fuel injection and ignition. During that delay, more and more fuel accumulates in the chamber, and when ignition finally happens, all that fuel burns at once in a sudden, violent explosion rather than a controlled burn. This rapid pressure rise causes the characteristic diesel knock—a heavy metallic clang. Diesel knock happens when injection timing is retarded, when fuel quality's poor (low cetane rating), or when combustion chamber deposits interfere with the ignition process. The knock itself is less damaging than petrol engine detonation, but it's a sign of poor running and inefficient combustion. Modern diesel engines have high-precision fuel injection systems that control timing extremely accurately to prevent knock.
exam_tip: Diesel knock: in compression-ignition engines, if ignition delay is too long, accumulated fuel causes rapid pressure rise when ignition occurs, creating heavy knock.
key_facts: Compression-ignition engines rely on heat of compression (500-600°C) to ignite fuel
Ignition delay too long = fuel accumulates before ignition
Rapid pressure rise from sudden combustion causes diesel knock
Diesel knock is characteristic heavy metallic clang
Caused by retarded injection timing, poor fuel quality, combustion chamber deposits
Less damaging than petrol engine detonation but indicates poor combustion

syllabus_desc: Describe the use of a Ground Power Unit (GPU).
concept: A Ground Power Unit, or GPU, is an external electrical power source that lets you start your helicopter without relying solely on the aircraft battery. It's essentially a large, portable power pack—either a cart with a diesel engine driving an alternator, or a solid-state power supply—that you plug into your electrical system. The GPU provides the high current your starter motor demands, which can be 300+ amps momentarily. If you tried to pull that much current from your onboard battery in cold weather, you'd drain it flat within seconds, and a drained battery won't support starting. A GPU solves this problem beautifully, especially in cold climates where battery performance drops significantly. It also lets you run electrical systems—instruments, lights, avionics—while parked without draining the battery. At Christchurch or Hamilton on a cold winter morning, a GPU is your friend. Most helicopter operations at larger bases like Ardmore or Queenstown have GPUs available. Using one is simple: connect the GPU to the aircraft's external power socket, switch the battery selector to GPU (or ensure the system draws from GPU), and you've got full electrical power for starting without battery stress.
exam_tip: GPU provides external electrical power for engine starting. Prevents battery drain, essential in cold weather. Connect to external power socket before starting.
key_facts: GPU is external electrical power source (cart or solid-state unit)
Provides high current for starter motor (300+ amps)
Prevents battery drain during starting, especially in cold weather
Allows electrical systems to run while parked without depleting battery
Connection via external power socket on aircraft
Common at larger helicopter bases in NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principle of operation of a simple float-type carburettor.
concept: A float carb's old-school but brilliant. Fuel enters a float chamber (a reservoir) where a floating cork or foam ball sits on the fuel surface. As fuel level rises, the float rises with it, mechanically closing a needle valve that shuts off fuel inflow. Fuel level stays constant around a set level. Below the float chamber is a venturi—a narrowed section in the airflow—where the airspeed increases and pressure drops. This low pressure sucks fuel from the float chamber through a jet (small hole) into the airstream, where it mixes with air. The faster the airflow, the more fuel gets sucked in. Throttle controls the overall airflow; you open it for more power. Simple, mechanical, no electrics needed. You'll still see these on older trainers, especially singles at Ardmore or Christchurch. Fuel injection's replaced them on modern planes.
exam_tip: Float controls fuel level. Venturi creates low pressure. Jet sprays fuel. Throttle controls airflow. Understand the three-way control: float holds level, venturi sucks fuel, throttle sets power.
key_facts: Float chamber maintains fuel level
Venturi creates low pressure zone
Pressure drop sucks fuel through jet
Airspeed controls fuel quantity
Throttle controls total airflow
Mechanical, no electrical components

syllabus_desc: Explain the following in relation to fuel-air mixture ratios: (a) rich and lean
concept: A rich mixture has more fuel than the ideal ratio -- too much fuel for the available air. A lean mixture has less fuel than ideal -- too little fuel for the air. Rich mixtures run cooler (excess fuel absorbs heat) but waste fuel. Lean mixtures are more efficient but run hotter and can cause detonation if taken too far.
exam_tip: Rich = excess fuel (cooler, less efficient). Lean = less fuel (hotter, more efficient but detonation risk).
key_facts: Rich mixture: more fuel than ideal, runs cooler, wastes fuel
Lean mixture: less fuel than ideal, runs hotter, more efficient
Over-leaning at high power settings risks detonation and engine damage

syllabus_desc: Explain the following in relation to fuel-air mixture ratios: (b) normal workable mixture ratio limits
concept: Normal workable mixture ratios range from about 8:1 (very rich) to about 18:1 (very lean) by weight. Outside these limits, the mixture won't ignite reliably. The engine runs smoothly within this range, though performance varies -- richer for power, leaner for economy.
exam_tip: Workable mixture range is approximately 8:1 (rich limit) to 18:1 (lean limit) by weight.
key_facts: Workable range: approximately 8:1 to 18:1 (fuel to air by weight)
Below 8:1 is too rich to burn; above 18:1 is too lean to burn
Engine performance varies across this range

syllabus_desc: Explain the following in relation to fuel-air mixture ratios: (c) the chemically correct or stoichiometric ratio
concept: The stoichiometric ratio (chemically correct mixture) is approximately 15:1 by weight -- 15 parts air to 1 part fuel. At this ratio, all the fuel and all the oxygen are completely consumed during combustion, with no excess of either. In practice, engines rarely run at exactly this ratio.
exam_tip: Stoichiometric ratio is approximately 15:1 by weight -- all fuel and oxygen are consumed completely.
key_facts: Stoichiometric ratio: approximately 15:1 (air to fuel by weight)
All fuel and oxygen are completely consumed at this ratio
Produces highest combustion temperatures -- not the best ratio for either power or economy

syllabus_desc: Explain the following in relation to fuel-air mixture ratios: (d) the approximate ratios for maximum power output and best economy
concept: Maximum power occurs at a slightly rich mixture of about 12:1 -- the excess fuel ensures every oxygen molecule finds a fuel molecule to burn with. Best economy occurs at a slightly lean mixture of about 16:1, where all the expensive fuel is burnt but some air passes through unused. You sacrifice some power for better fuel efficiency.
exam_tip: Best power: about 12:1 (slightly rich). Best economy: about 16:1 (slightly lean of stoichiometric).
key_facts: Maximum power: approximately 12:1 (slightly rich of stoichiometric)
Best economy: approximately 16:1 (slightly lean of stoichiometric)
Best economy gives longest range; best power gives best rate of climb and speed

syllabus_desc: Describe a typical carburettor mixture setting curve and the operating area where detonation will occur.
concept: A carb mixture curve shows how engine power varies with mixture ratio at a given RPM and altitude. If you plot it, you get a peak—maximum power at a particular mixture ratio (usually around 12:1, rich of stoichiometric). As you lean from that peak, power drops gradually on the economy side. As you enrich past the peak, power also drops because excess fuel cools combustion and increases weight without burning. The detonation zone is the lean side of the power peak—as you lean out, you're running hotter and risking knock. If conditions are hot (high ambient temperature, high power), the detonation zone starts earlier (richer). This is why at high altitude where it's cold, you can lean further without knock. A typical operating curve shows a safe envelope where you can run without detonation; outside that, knock risk rises sharply.
exam_tip: Power peak at rich side of stoichiometric. Lean side = economy. Detonation zone = lean side of power peak. Know why temperature and altitude shift the zone.
key_facts: Power peaks around 12:1 (rich of stoichiometric)
Leaning past peak = economy but losing power
Detonation zone on lean side of peak
High temperature pushes detonation zone richer
High altitude (cold) allows leaner mixture
Safe operating envelope defined by detonation margin

syllabus_desc: Explain the need for the following in an aero-engine carburetor: (a) a venturi
concept: A venturi is a narrowing in the carburettor throat that accelerates the airflow and creates a pressure drop. This low pressure draws fuel from the float chamber through the main jet. The faster the air flows (more throttle), the greater the pressure drop and the more fuel is drawn in. It's the same principle as a wing generating lift.
exam_tip: The venturi creates low pressure by accelerating airflow -- this pressure drop draws fuel from the float chamber.
key_facts: Venturi narrows the airflow path, increasing velocity and decreasing pressure
The pressure drop draws fuel through the main jet (Bernoulli's principle)
Fuel flow is roughly proportional to airflow through the venturi

syllabus_desc: Explain the need for the following in an aero-engine carburetor: (b) atomization and diffusion
concept: Atomisation breaks the liquid fuel into a fine mist of tiny droplets as it enters the airstream. Diffusion mixes these droplets evenly throughout the air. Together, they ensure the fuel-air mixture reaching each cylinder is uniform and burns efficiently. Poor atomisation leads to uneven combustion and wasted fuel.
exam_tip: Atomisation = breaking fuel into fine droplets. Diffusion = mixing droplets evenly through the airstream.
key_facts: Atomisation breaks liquid fuel into tiny droplets for rapid vaporisation
Diffusion spreads fuel evenly through the airflow
Both are needed for efficient, even combustion across all cylinders

syllabus_desc: Explain the need for the following in an aero-engine carburetor: (c) an accelerating system
concept: The accelerating system (accelerator pump) provides a brief squirt of extra fuel when the throttle is opened rapidly. Without it, the sudden increase in airflow would momentarily lean the mixture (because fuel is heavier and responds more slowly than air), causing a hesitation or stumble. The pump compensates by mechanically injecting fuel during throttle advancement.
exam_tip: The accelerator pump prevents a lean hesitation during rapid throttle opening by injecting extra fuel.
key_facts: Prevents momentary lean condition during rapid throttle opening
Mechanically linked to the throttle -- squirts fuel as throttle advances
Without it, the engine would hesitate or stumble on sudden power increases

syllabus_desc: Explain the need for the following in an aero-engine carburetor: (d) an idling system
concept: The idling system provides fuel at low throttle settings when airflow through the main venturi is too slow to draw fuel from the main jet. It uses a separate small passage downstream of the throttle butterfly where suction is still strong enough to draw fuel. Without this system, the engine would starve and stop at idle.
