exam_tip: Purpose: verify system's operational before flight. Catches failures early. Automatic or test-switch based.
key_facts: Built-in self-test capability
Verifies all components communication
Detects sensor, coil, plug issues
Usually automatic on master activation
Clear pass/fail indication
Prevents flying with degraded system

syllabus_desc: Explain the procedure for conducting typical solid state ignition integrity checks.
concept: Exact procedures vary by system, so always consult the POH. General procedure: activate master switch; the system self-test runs automatically (usually a few seconds). LED indicators or annunciators show result—green/OK light means system's healthy; red/fault light means problem. Some systems allow manual test by pressing a test button; you'd hold it briefly and watch for the correct response (spark at plugs, coil responding, etc.). On more basic systems, you'd simply observe that the system powers up correctly when master's on and instruments show normal readings. Some aircraft also have a ground-check procedure: with master on and engine off, you can verify certain system functions without running. Once you start the engine, the system's continuously self-monitoring during flight. If a problem develops, an annunciator or warning light alerts you. Never skip these checks; solid state systems depend on power and electronics, so verifying they're healthy before flight is essential.
exam_tip: Check POH for exact procedure. Usually: master on, observe self-test, verify pass. No major interpretation like mag checks.
key_facts: Master on activates self-test
Observe LED/annunciator indication
Manual test button on some systems
Green = OK, red/fault = problem
System continuously monitors in flight
Follow POH exactly
Don't fly if test fails

syllabus_desc: With reference to a solid-state ignition system: (a) explain the significance of maintaining the ignition power supply
concept: Solid-state (electronic) ignition systems depend on an external power supply (usually the aircraft's electrical bus) rather than magneto self-generation. If the power supply is lost, the ignition system fails completely. Maintaining a reliable power supply is therefore critical -- unlike magnetos, which are self-powered and keep running even in a total electrical failure.
exam_tip: Solid-state ignition depends on external power -- unlike magnetos, it fails if the electrical supply is lost.
key_facts: Requires external electrical power to operate
Loss of power supply = loss of ignition (unlike self-powered magnetos)
Power supply integrity is critical for flight safety

syllabus_desc: With reference to a solid-state ignition system: (b) describe a typical power supply backup system
concept: A typical backup for solid-state ignition uses a dedicated battery or capacitor bank that can power the ignition system independently if the main bus fails. Some systems use a dual power supply arrangement where the ignition can draw from either the main bus or an emergency bus. This provides redundancy similar to the independence of traditional magnetos.
exam_tip: Backup systems include dedicated batteries or emergency bus connections to maintain ignition if the main bus fails.
key_facts: Dedicated backup battery or capacitor provides independent ignition power
Some systems use dual bus feeds for redundancy
Backup must be tested during pre-flight checks

syllabus_desc: Describe the functioning of the starter motor.
concept: A starter motor's an electric motor powered by the aircraft battery. When you engage the starter (via a cockpit switch), battery current flows through a solenoid (electromagnet relay). The solenoid pulls a mechanical linkage that engages a gear on the starter shaft with a larger gear on the engine's flywheel. Simultaneously, the solenoid completes a heavy-current circuit that powers the motor itself. The motor's armature (rotating coil) spins in a magnetic field, creating torque. This torque, multiplied by the gear ratio (small gear on starter, large gear on flywheel), provides enough force to crank the engine over against compression resistance. Once the engine fires and speed exceeds starter speed, a one-way clutch (sprag clutch) in the starter gear disengages—the engine stops driving the starter back. Current ceases (you've released the starter switch), and the solenoid releases, disengaging the gear. Simple but effective.
exam_tip: Solenoid engages gear and powers motor. Motor drives flywheel gear. One-way clutch releases after engine start.
key_facts: Battery powers starter motor via solenoid
Solenoid engages starter gear to flywheel
Electric motor creates cranking torque
Gear reduction multiplies torque
One-way clutch disengages after start
Solenoid releases when starter switch released

syllabus_desc: Describe the operational limitations of the starter motor.
concept: Starter motors draw enormous current—several hundred amps—from the battery. This is only sustainable briefly; prolonged cranking drains the battery quickly. Typical cranking duration: 5-10 seconds maximum. If the engine doesn't catch in that time, you release the starter, wait 30 seconds to let the battery recover, then try again. Multiple rapid starts in succession can completely drain the battery. Starter motors also generate intense heat during operation. Continuous operation (beyond 10 seconds) can overheat the armature and damage windings. POH specifies maximum continuous crank time (often 10 seconds) and mandatory cool-down periods between attempts. Starter motors are designed for intermittent duty, not continuous running. In very cold conditions or with a weak battery, starting becomes difficult or impossible—the motor spins slowly, generating weak cranking torque. At places like Christchurch in winter, you might need GPU (ground power unit) assistance for reliable starting. Maintenance: if the starter turns slowly or won't engage (solenoid clicks but motor doesn't crank), the battery or electrical connections are likely faulty.
exam_tip: Starter draws huge current, brief use only. Max 10 seconds cranking, then cool-down. Multiple starts risk battery depletion.
key_facts: Draws 300+ amps from battery
Generates intense heat
Intermittent duty only
Max 10 seconds continuous cranking
Requires 30+ second cool-down between attempts
Multiple starts risk total discharge
Cold/weak battery complicates starting

syllabus_desc: Explain the correct operation of a typical starter switch.
concept: Your starter switch is spring-loaded to the off position—you have to actively hold it (or turn it past a detent). When you engage it, the switch energises the starter solenoid, which closes a heavy-duty contactor connecting battery power directly to the starter motor. The starter motor cranks the engine, and once the engine fires and reaches self-sustaining RPM, you release the switch. It springs back, the solenoid de-energises, and the starter disengages. The spring-return design is a safety feature—if you let go, it stops. You don't want the starter engaged once the engine's running; it would overheat the starter motor or damage the engagement mechanism. On many helicopter starters (like the R22), the start sequence involves holding the starter while monitoring RPM and engine instruments. Once the engine lights off and stabilises, you release. Some systems have a time limit—typically 10 to 30 seconds of cranking—to prevent overheating the starter motor. If it doesn't start, you wait a cooling period before trying again. Check your flight manual for specific limits.
exam_tip: Spring-loaded to off = safety feature. Starter disengages when released. Know the cranking time limits and cooling periods for your type. Starter solenoid is an electromagnetic switch controlling heavy starter current.
key_facts: Starter switch: spring-loaded to off position. Energises starter solenoid when engaged. Solenoid closes heavy-duty contactor to battery. Release switch = starter disengages. Cranking time limits (typically 10-30 seconds). Cooling period required between attempts. Must monitor RPM and instruments during start. Spring-return prevents accidental engagement after engine start.

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the terms; (a) force; (b) work; (c) power; (d) energy; (e) torque.
concept: Force, work, power, energy, and torque are fundamental concepts you'll use constantly when thinking about helicopter performance and engine operation. Force is straightforward: it's a push or pull, measured in newtons. Your engine develops force through combustion pressure pushing pistons. Work is force multiplied by distance—it's what happens when you apply force over a distance and actually accomplish something. If you push with 100 newtons over a distance of one metre, you've done 100 joules of work. Power is work divided by time—how quickly you do that work. If you do 100 joules of work in one second, you're delivering 100 watts of power. Energy is the capacity to do work; it's stored in fuel, in spinning rotors, in elevated position. Torque is rotational force—the twisting effort around a shaft. If your engine crankshaft is being pushed by combustion with a twisting force, that's torque, measured in newton-metres. These concepts interconnect: power is the rate at which energy is delivered; torque × rpm determines power; work is energy transferred from one form to another. In your helicopter, the engine's combustion develops force pushing pistons, which creates torque in the crankshaft, which delivers power to the rotor. Understanding these five concepts clearly helps you grasp how engines perform and why weight, altitude, and temperature affect your helicopter's capabilities.
exam_tip: Force = push/pull (N). Work = force × distance (J). Power = work/time (W/hp). Energy = capacity to do work. Torque = rotational force (Nm).
key_facts: Force: push or pull, measured in newtons (N)
Work: force × distance, measured in joules (J)
Power: work/time, measured in watts (W) or horsepower (hp)
Energy: capacity to do work (stored in fuel, position, kinetic energy)
Torque: rotational force, measured in newton-metres (Nm)
Power = torque × rpm ÷ constant (interconnection of power and torque)

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the terms: (a) force
concept: Force is a push or pull that can change the motion of an object, measured in newtons (N). In engine terms, combustion pressure acting on the piston area produces the force that drives the piston down. Force = pressure x area.
exam_tip: Force = pressure x area. More pressure on the piston = more force pushing it down.
key_facts: Measured in newtons (N)
Force = pressure x area
Combustion pressure acting on piston area creates the driving force

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the terms: (b) work
concept: Work is done when a force moves an object through a distance, measured in joules (J). Work = force x distance. The piston moving down the cylinder under combustion force is doing work. If the force doesn't move anything, no work is done no matter how strong the force.
exam_tip: Work = force x distance. No movement = no work done, regardless of force applied.
key_facts: Measured in joules (J)
Work = force x distance moved in the direction of the force
The power stroke does work; the compression stroke has work done on the gas

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the terms: (c) power
concept: Power is the rate of doing work -- how fast work is done. Measured in watts (W) or horsepower (hp). An engine producing 180 hp delivers a fixed amount of work per second. Power = work / time. Two engines might do the same total work, but the more powerful one does it faster.
exam_tip: Power = work / time. Horsepower measures how quickly the engine delivers energy.
key_facts: Measured in watts (W) or horsepower (hp); 1 hp = 746 W
Power = work / time
Higher RPM generally means more power (more power strokes per minute)

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the terms: (d) energy
concept: Energy is the capacity to do work, measured in joules (J). Fuel contains chemical energy that's converted to heat energy during combustion, then to mechanical energy as the piston moves. Energy can change form but cannot be created or destroyed -- only some of the fuel's energy ends up as useful work.
exam_tip: Energy is the ability to do work -- fuel's chemical energy converts through heat to mechanical energy.
key_facts: Measured in joules (J)
Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted between forms
Much of the fuel's energy is lost as waste heat through exhaust and cooling

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the terms: (e) torque
concept: Torque is a rotational or twisting force, measured in newton-metres (Nm) or foot-pounds (ft-lb). It depends on both the force applied and the distance from the centre of rotation (the arm). In engine terms, combustion force on the piston creates torque on the crankshaft through the connecting rod. Maximum torque occurs when the connecting rod makes a right angle with the crank.
exam_tip: Torque = force x arm. Best engine torque occurs when the crank arm is at maximum effective length (90 degrees).
key_facts: Torque = force x perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation
Measured in Nm or ft-lb
Maximum torque occurs when connecting rod is perpendicular to the crank throw

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between horsepower and kilowatts.
concept: Horsepower and kilowatts are two different ways of measuring the same thing—power. Most older aircraft and some current ones specify power in horsepower (hp), while modern engineering increasingly uses kilowatts (kW). Understanding the conversion between them is essential for reading aircraft specifications and comparing performance. One horsepower equals 746 watts. Therefore, one horsepower equals 0.746 kilowatts. If your helicopter engine is rated at 200 horsepower, you can convert to kilowatts by multiplying: 200 × 0.746 = 149.2 kilowatts. Conversely, if an engine is rated at 150 kilowatts and you want horsepower, you'd multiply by 1.34 (which is approximately 1/0.746): 150 × 1.34 = 201 horsepower. The conversion factor's consistent because it's based on the fundamental definition of horsepower: the power required to lift 550 pounds one foot per second, which happens to equal 746 watts. Some specifications you'll see are in metric horsepower (ch), which is slightly different at 735.5 watts, so watch for that distinction. But for aviation purposes in English-speaking countries, you'll typically work with mechanical horsepower (hp) and kilowatts (kW), and the 0.746/1.34 conversion works perfectly.
exam_tip: 1 hp = 746 watts = 0.746 kW. Multiply hp by 0.746 for kW. Multiply kW by 1.34 for hp.
key_facts: 1 horsepower = 746 watts
1 horsepower = 0.746 kilowatts
Multiply hp by 0.746 to convert to kW
Multiply kW by 1.34 to convert to hp
Conversion based on standard definition of mechanical horsepower
Watch for metric horsepower (ch) = 735.5 watts (slightly different)

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between horsepower and kilowatts.
concept: Horsepower and kilowatts are two different ways of measuring the same thing—power. Most older aircraft and some current ones specify power in horsepower (hp), while modern engineering increasingly uses kilowatts (kW). Understanding the conversion between them is essential for reading aircraft specifications and comparing performance. One horsepower equals 746 watts. Therefore, one horsepower equals 0.746 kilowatts. If your helicopter engine is rated at 200 horsepower, you can convert to kilowatts by multiplying: 200 × 0.746 = 149.2 kilowatts. Conversely, if an engine is rated at 150 kilowatts and you want horsepower, you'd multiply by 1.34 (which is approximately 1/0.746): 150 × 1.34 = 201 horsepower. The conversion factor's consistent because it's based on the fundamental definition of horsepower: the power required to lift 550 pounds one foot per second, which happens to equal 746 watts. Some specifications you'll see are in metric horsepower (ch), which is slightly different at 735.5 watts, so watch for that distinction. But for aviation purposes in English-speaking countries, you'll typically work with mechanical horsepower (hp) and kilowatts (kW), and the 0.746/1.34 conversion works perfectly.
exam_tip: 1 hp = 746 watts = 0.746 kW. Multiply hp by 0.746 for kW. Multiply kW by 1.34 for hp.
key_facts: 1 horsepower = 746 watts
1 horsepower = 0.746 kilowatts
Multiply hp by 0.746 to convert to kW
Multiply kW by 1.34 to convert to hp
Conversion based on standard definition of mechanical horsepower
Watch for metric horsepower (ch) = 735.5 watts (slightly different)

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (a) brake power; (b) rated power; (c) rated altitude (critical altitude); (d) full throttle height.
concept: Brake power is the actual power delivered by the engine shaft—the useful power you can extract and use (driving the propeller, etc.). It's measured by applying a controlled load (brake) to the shaft and measuring the torque and RPM needed to resist it. Rated power is the maximum continuous power the engine's approved to produce without damage; exceeding it shortens engine life. Most engines have both a takeoff power (high for 5 minutes) and a cruise power rating (sustainable indefinitely). Rated altitude (or critical altitude) is the altitude at which the engine can no longer produce rated power, even at full throttle. Above this altitude, power drops because air density's too low. A naturally-aspirated engine might have critical altitude of 8000-10,000 feet. Full throttle height is similar—it's the altitude where the engine reaches its rated power at full throttle (below this, it reaches rated power before hitting full throttle; above, it can't reach rated power even throttle-firewalled). For training, these matter because you know your engine's limitations—in the heat at Ardmore, you might not achieve rated power at sea level due to temperature. At altitude climbing out from Christchurch, power decreases as you climb.
exam_tip: Brake power = shaft power (what's available). Rated = max continuous approved. Critical altitude = loses rated power. Full throttle height = reaches rated power.
key_facts: Brake power: useful shaft power output
Rated power: maximum continuous approved
Takeoff vs cruise ratings differ
Critical altitude: power falls below rated
Full throttle height: reaches rated at full throttle
Power decreases above critical altitude

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (a) brake power
concept: Brake power (BHP -- brake horsepower) is the actual power available at the crankshaft to drive the propeller after all internal friction and accessory losses are subtracted. It's measured by a 'brake' (dynamometer) on a test stand. BHP = IHP - FHP (indicated horsepower minus friction horsepower). This is what the pilot actually gets to use.
exam_tip: BHP = IHP - FHP. Brake power is what actually reaches the propeller after friction and accessory losses.
key_facts: BHP = indicated horsepower (IHP) minus friction horsepower (FHP)
Measured by a dynamometer (brake) on a test stand
This is the usable power available to drive the propeller

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (b) rated power
concept: Rated power is the maximum power the engine is certified to produce continuously under standard conditions. It's the power output specified by the manufacturer at a given RPM and MAP. Operating above rated power risks engine damage and is outside the certification envelope.
exam_tip: Rated power is the maximum certified continuous power at specified RPM and MAP.
key_facts: Maximum power certified for continuous operation
Specified at a given RPM and MAP under standard conditions
Exceeding rated power risks engine damage and voids certification

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (c) rated altitude (critical altitude)
concept: Rated altitude (critical altitude) is the highest altitude at which the engine can still produce its full rated power at full throttle. Above this altitude, even at full throttle, the engine can no longer produce rated power because air density has dropped too much. For a normally aspirated engine, critical altitude is essentially sea level. For turbocharged engines, it's much higher.
exam_tip: Critical altitude = highest altitude for full rated power at full throttle. Above it, power drops with altitude.
key_facts: Highest altitude at which full rated power is achievable
Normally aspirated engines: critical altitude is near sea level
Turbocharged engines have a much higher critical altitude

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (d) full throttle height
concept: Full throttle height is essentially the same concept as critical altitude -- it's the altitude above which full throttle can no longer maintain rated MAP. Below full throttle height, the throttle must be partially closed to avoid exceeding rated MAP. Above it, even full throttle gives less than rated MAP and power decreases with further altitude gain.
exam_tip: Full throttle height = altitude where full throttle just produces rated MAP. Above it, power drops off.
key_facts: Altitude where full throttle just maintains rated manifold pressure
Below this altitude, throttle must be restricted to avoid over-boosting
Above this altitude, power decreases with increasing altitude

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (a) thermal efficiency; (b) mechanical efficiency; (c) volumetric efficiency.
concept: Thermal efficiency, mechanical efficiency, and volumetric efficiency are three separate measures of how well an engine converts fuel into useful power, and they're critical concepts for understanding engine performance limitations. Thermal efficiency is the percentage of the fuel's heat energy that actually becomes mechanical work. In a typical piston aircraft engine, that's roughly 30-35%. The rest escapes as heat through the exhaust and cooling system. No piston engine is ever going to be more efficient than that theoretical limit due to the laws of thermodynamics. Mechanical efficiency refers to friction losses within the engine itself. The crankshaft spins, bearings and pistons slide, accessories run—all of that creates friction that absorbs power. A well-maintained engine achieves roughly 80-90% mechanical efficiency, meaning 80-90% of the power produced by combustion actually reaches the propeller or, in your case, the rotor shaft, while 10-20% is lost to internal friction. Volumetric efficiency is the amount of air actually flowing into the cylinders compared to the theoretical amount if the intake system were perfectly efficient. At sea level, a well-designed engine achieves roughly 100% volumetric efficiency or slightly better. But as altitude increases and air density decreases, volumetric efficiency drops dramatically. At 10,000 feet, the air's only about 70% as dense as sea level, so volumetric efficiency might drop to 70%, meaning fewer oxygen molecules per cylinder, less power available. Superchargers and turbochargers are specifically designed to restore volumetric efficiency at altitude by compressing the incoming air back to higher density.
exam_tip: Thermal (~30-35%): fuel heat to work. Mechanical (~80-90%): friction losses. Volumetric (~100% sea level, drops at altitude): actual air vs theoretical.
key_facts: Thermal efficiency: percentage of fuel heat converted to mechanical work (~30-35%)
Mechanical efficiency: accounts for friction losses inside engine (~80-90%)
Volumetric efficiency: actual air mass vs theoretical maximum
At sea level, volumetric efficiency ~100% for well-designed engines
At altitude, volumetric efficiency drops as air density decreases
At 10,000 ft, air density ~70% of sea level, reducing available power
Superchargers/turbochargers restore volumetric efficiency at altitude

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (a) thermal efficiency
concept: Thermal efficiency compares the total heat energy released by combustion to the energy actually delivered to the piston as useful work. A typical aircraft piston engine has a thermal efficiency of only about 30% -- roughly 40% is lost through the exhaust, 20% is lost heating engine components, and only 30% reaches the piston.
exam_tip: Thermal efficiency is about 30% for piston engines -- most heat energy is lost through exhaust and cooling.
key_facts: Thermal efficiency = useful energy to piston / total combustion energy
Typical aircraft piston engine: about 30%
About 40% lost through exhaust, 20% lost to engine component heating

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (b) mechanical efficiency
concept: Mechanical efficiency compares the brake horsepower (actual output at the crankshaft) to the indicated horsepower (power delivered to the pistons). The difference is friction horsepower -- power lost to internal friction and driving accessories like oil pumps, fuel pumps, and magnetos. Mechanical efficiency is typically around 85-90%.
exam_tip: Mechanical efficiency = BHP / IHP. About 10-15% of piston power is lost to friction and accessories.
key_facts: Mechanical efficiency = BHP / IHP x 100%
Typically 85-90% for aircraft piston engines
Losses include internal friction, oil pump, fuel pump, and magneto drives

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (c) volumetric efficiency
concept: Volumetric efficiency compares the actual volume of air drawn into the cylinders to the theoretical maximum (swept volume). An engine rarely achieves 100% because of flow restrictions, heating, and valve timing. Factors that improve volumetric efficiency include ram air, supercharging, and optimised valve timing. Higher volumetric efficiency means more power.
exam_tip: Volumetric efficiency = actual air intake / swept volume. Supercharging can push it above 100%.
key_facts: Actual air volume drawn in vs theoretical maximum (swept volume)
Normally aspirated engines: typically 75-85%
Supercharging increases volumetric efficiency beyond 100%

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between typical brake power and power available curves.
concept: A brake power curve plots engine power (vertical axis) against RPM (horizontal axis). At low RPM, power's low. As RPM increases, power increases (more combustion cycles per minute). The curve typically rises steeply initially, then levels off, reaching a peak somewhere around cruise RPM, then might drop slightly or plateau at maximum RPM. A power-available curve (often used in aircraft performance analysis) shows how much power the propeller can absorb at various airspeeds and altitudes. At low airspeed, the propeller demands little power; as airspeed increases (denser air loading the propeller), power required increases dramatically. The intersection of available power (what the engine delivers) and power required (what the propeller needs at that airspeed) determines performance. Below critical altitude, maximum brake power's available at wide-open throttle. Above critical altitude, power drops despite full throttle. In level flight, the aircraft climbs when power available exceeds power required, descends when power required exceeds available, and flies level when they balance. Understanding both curves helps predict aircraft performance across the speed envelope and altitude.
exam_tip: Brake power curve: engine capability vs RPM. Power available: what propeller absorbs vs airspeed. Intersection determines performance balance.
key_facts: Brake power increases with RPM
Peaks near cruise RPM
Power available: propeller demand at each speed
Climb: available exceeds required
Level flight: balanced
Descent: required exceeds available

syllabus_desc: Explain the general conditions for the most efficient engine operation.
concept: Most efficient operation occurs at cruise power and altitude, where the engine's running at moderate settings (not maximum power, not low power), mixture's properly leaned, and ignition's optimally timed. Specific conditions: manifold pressure set to the value specified for cruise (typically 60-75% power), RPM set for the desired speed-efficiency trade-off (lower RPM = less fuel burn but slower; higher RPM = more speed but more fuel), mixture leaned to the smooth setting just before the power peak (around 17:1 to 18:1 ratio), engine temperature in the green band (not too hot, not too cold), and smooth flying (no excess maneuvring or attitude changes that waste energy). Avoid high-power climbs and descents that stress the engine and waste fuel. Smooth power transitions are more efficient than abrupt throttle changes. Flying at altitude (thinner air but cooler) often provides better efficiency than sea level (denser but hotter). Every engine's different; the POH specifies cruise power settings for best economy or best range. Following the POH exactly gives the best efficiency. Essentially: steady-state moderate-power flight with properly-leaned mixture.
exam_tip: Efficient operation: cruise power, altitude, leaned mixture, smooth flying. Avoid high power and abrupt changes.
key_facts: Cruise power (not max, not low)
Proper altitude (cooler, thinner air)
Mixture leaned to optimum
Smooth flying, minimal maneuvring
Engine temps in green band
POH-specified cruise settings
Steady-state operation

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of changing altitude on manifold pressure and power output of normally aspirated engines. Ancillary Systems
concept: A normally-aspirated engine (no supercharger or turbo) draws intake air directly from the atmosphere. As altitude increases, atmospheric pressure decreases, so air density decreases. The engine draws the same volume of air per stroke, but that volume contains fewer oxygen molecules. Manifold pressure (measured on the aircraft's manifold pressure gauge, which shows intake pressure) decreases with altitude. At sea level, manifold pressure might read 28-30 inches of mercury at full throttle. At 10,000 feet, it might read only 18 inches even with throttle wide open. Less air means less combustion force, so brake power decreases. The power decrease is roughly proportional to air density—at 10,000 feet (about 70% sea-level density), you'd have roughly 70% of sea-level power. This is why aircraft can't climb indefinitely; at some critical altitude, power available equals power required (no excess for further climb), and the aircraft reaches its service ceiling. Pilots manage this by descending power settings at altitude (e.g., 'at cruise, reduce to 65% power') to match what the atmosphere allows. Above critical altitude, the manifold pressure gauge becomes a power limiter rather than a throttle—you can't exceed the power available regardless of throttle position.
exam_tip: Higher altitude = lower air density = lower manifold pressure = lower power. Effect: power drop roughly matches density drop.
key_facts: Manifold pressure decreases with altitude
Air density decreases with altitude
Power output decreases proportionally
Roughly 70% power at 10,000 ft
Above critical altitude, power's density-limited
Climb performance worsens higher
Service ceiling: power available = power required

syllabus_desc: Describe the systems which typically require DC power in an aircraft.
concept: Your DC electrical system is the backbone of the aeroplane. Everything that needs power before you get the engines running—and quite a bit after—relies on that battery and alternator. Think about what happens when you're doing your walk-around at Ardmore or anywhere else: you need lights, you need the radio to talk to ATC, the fuel pump needs juice to get fuel to the engines. Once you're running, systems like landing lights, navigation lights, instrument panel lighting, the autopilot, and the entire avionics suite all depend on DC power. Even your flaps and landing gear on many twins use electrically-driven pumps or motors. The point is, without DC power, you're not flying very far.
exam_tip: List the major consumers: battery charging/starting, lighting (cockpit, nav, landing), radio, fuel pump, engine instruments, autopilot, landing gear/flaps, and avionics. Focus on what's essential for flight ops.
key_facts: DC power comes from the battery and alternator/generator
Critical systems: radio, fuel pump, engine instruments, lighting
Landing gear and flaps often use electric motors or pump drives
Autopilot and modern avionics require steady DC supply
Loss of electrical power means loss of radio comms and engine instrumentation

syllabus_desc: Explain the function(s) of the following in a typical electrical system: (a) the battery
concept: The battery provides electrical power when the engine isn't running (for pre-flight checks, radio calls) and supplies the high current needed for engine starting. Once airborne, it acts as a backup if the alternator fails, and smooths out voltage spikes. In a 24V system, the battery provides 24 volts while the alternator charges at 28 volts.
exam_tip: The battery provides starting power and acts as emergency backup if the alternator fails in flight.
key_facts: Provides power for engine starting (high current demand)
Backup power source if alternator fails
Smooths voltage fluctuations in the electrical system

syllabus_desc: Explain the function(s) of the following in a typical electrical system: (b) a ground power source
concept: A ground power unit (GPU) is an external power source used to supply electrical power to the aircraft on the ground, saving the battery for engine starting. It's particularly useful after maintenance, long periods of inactivity, or in cold weather when battery performance is reduced. The GPU is disconnected before taxi.
exam_tip: A GPU supplies external power on the ground -- saves battery capacity for engine start.
key_facts: External power source for ground operations
Preserves battery charge for engine starting
Must be disconnected before taxi and flight

syllabus_desc: Explain the function(s) of the following in a typical electrical system: (c) the alternator or generator
concept: The alternator (or generator) is the primary source of electrical power once the engine is running. It's driven by the engine and produces power for all electrical loads while simultaneously recharging the battery. The alternator output voltage (typically 28V in a 24V system) is set higher than battery voltage to ensure the battery stays charged.
exam_tip: The alternator is the primary power source in flight -- its output exceeds battery voltage to keep it charged.
key_facts: Engine-driven primary power source during flight
Output voltage higher than battery voltage to maintain charge
Supplies all electrical loads and recharges the battery simultaneously

syllabus_desc: Explain the function(s) of the following in a typical electrical system: (d) bus bars
concept: Bus bars are common connection points that distribute electrical power to all the aircraft's electrical services. Think of them as a power distribution board -- the alternator and battery feed into the bus bar, and each electrical service taps off it through its own circuit breaker and switch. Some aircraft have multiple buses for redundancy.
exam_tip: Bus bars distribute power from the source (alternator/battery) to all electrical services via circuit breakers.
key_facts: Central distribution point for electrical power
All services connect to the bus bar through individual circuit breakers
Multiple bus configurations provide redundancy in more complex aircraft

syllabus_desc: Explain the function(s) of the following in a typical electrical system: (e) over voltage protection
concept: Overvoltage protection prevents damage to sensitive avionics if the voltage regulator fails and alternator output rises too high. An overvoltage relay monitors system voltage and disconnects the alternator from the bus if voltage exceeds a set limit. Some aircraft have an overvoltage warning light to alert the pilot.
exam_tip: Overvoltage protection disconnects the alternator if voltage exceeds safe limits -- protects sensitive avionics.
key_facts: Monitors system voltage and disconnects alternator if too high
Protects sensitive electronic equipment from voltage spikes
Usually indicated by a warning light on the instrument panel

syllabus_desc: Given appropriate data, determine the duration of battery operation following a generator failure.
concept: If your alternator fails mid-flight, you're running on battery power alone—and that battery's capacity is finite. You've got your battery amp-hour rating (typically 25-35 Ah on a light twin) and the aircraft's electrical load in amps. Simple division: capacity divided by load equals remaining time. So if you've got a 30 Ah battery and your electrical load is pulling 40 amps (lights, radio, instruments), you've got roughly 45 minutes before that battery's flat. But here's the reality: you'll start dropping non-essential loads, shed the landing light, maybe dim the panel—so you can stretch it. The exam will give you specific numbers, so the maths is straightforward, but the practical lesson is urgent: get the aircraft on the ground at the nearest suitable airfield.
exam_tip: Use the formula: time (hours) = battery capacity (Ah) ÷ electrical load (amps). In the real world, you'll reduce non-essential loads immediately after an alternator failure and declare an emergency with ATC.
key_facts: Battery capacity is rated in amp-hours (Ah)
Calculate remaining time: capacity ÷ load = hours
Typical light twin battery: 25–35 Ah
Cockpit load in normal flight: 20–50 amps depending on configuration
Reduce non-essential loads immediately (landing lights, autopilot, non-essential avionics)

syllabus_desc: Describe the relative advantages and disadvantages of: (a) a generator
concept: Generator advantages: simpler construction, no rectifier needed (produces DC directly). Disadvantages: heavier, less reliable (brushes and commutator wear), cannot produce rated voltage at low RPM, needs three regulators (voltage, current, and reverse current relay), and lower power output relative to weight.
exam_tip: Generators produce DC directly but are heavier, less reliable, and need three separate regulators.
key_facts: Produces DC directly -- no rectifier needed
Heavier and less reliable than alternators
Cannot produce rated voltage at low RPM; needs three regulators

syllabus_desc: Describe the relative advantages and disadvantages of: (b) an alternator
concept: Alternator advantages: lighter, more reliable (no commutator), reaches rated voltage at low RPM, higher power-to-weight ratio, only needs a voltage regulator, and diodes prevent reverse current flow automatically. Disadvantages: requires a rectifier to convert AC to DC, and needs initial excitation (battery power) to start generating.
exam_tip: Alternators are lighter, more reliable, and reach rated voltage at low RPM -- the modern standard for light aircraft.
key_facts: Lighter, more reliable, better power-to-weight ratio
Reaches rated voltage at low RPM
Only needs a voltage regulator (diodes handle reverse current protection)

syllabus_desc: Explain the functions and interpretation of: (a) left-zero ammeters
concept: A left-zero ammeter sits in the alternator circuit and shows the total alternator output. During normal operation, the needle deflects right of zero, showing total current being supplied. If the alternator fails, the needle drops to zero -- even though the battery is now powering the system. The left-zero ammeter should never read zero during normal operation.
exam_tip: Left-zero ammeter: shows alternator output. Zero reading = alternator failure. Should never read zero normally.
key_facts: Located in the alternator circuit, shows total alternator output
Normal reading: deflected right of zero (total system load)
Zero reading indicates alternator failure

syllabus_desc: Explain the functions and interpretation of: (b) centre-zero ammeters
concept: A centre-zero ammeter sits in the battery circuit and shows current flowing to or from the battery. A slight charge (deflection to the + side) is normal -- the alternator is topping up the battery. A discharge (deflection to the - side) means the battery is supplying power, indicating alternator failure or excessive electrical load. A centre-zero ammeter should never show a discharge during normal flight.
exam_tip: Centre-zero ammeter: slight charge = normal. Any discharge = alternator not keeping up or has failed.
key_facts: Located in the battery circuit
Charge (+) = alternator feeding battery; Discharge (-) = battery feeding system
Should show slight charge during normal operation -- discharge indicates a problem

syllabus_desc: Explain the functions and correct operation of a: (a) single battery master switch
concept: A single battery master switch controls power from both the battery and alternator together. When switched on, the battery connects to the bus and the alternator field circuit is energised. When switched off, all electrical power is disconnected. Simple but less flexible -- you can't isolate the alternator independently.
exam_tip: Single master: one switch controls both battery and alternator together -- no independent isolation.
key_facts: One switch controls both battery and alternator connections
ON: both connect to bus; OFF: all power disconnected
Simpler but less flexible than a split switch

syllabus_desc: Explain the functions and correct operation of a: (b) split battery/alternator switch
concept: A split battery/alternator switch has separate switches for the battery and alternator field. This lets you isolate the alternator while keeping battery power (useful if the alternator malfunctions), or run on alternator only without the battery connected. More flexible for troubleshooting electrical problems in flight.
exam_tip: Split switch: independent control of battery and alternator -- more flexibility for fault isolation in flight.
key_facts: Separate switches for battery and alternator field circuits
Can isolate alternator independently if it malfunctions
Provides more options for electrical troubleshooting

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of: (a) fuses
concept: Fuses are thin metal strips designed to melt and break the circuit if current exceeds a safe limit. They protect wiring and equipment from overheating and fire. Once a fuse blows, it must be physically replaced. Aircraft carry spare fuses of each rating for in-flight replacement.
exam_tip: Fuses melt to break the circuit -- single use only, must be replaced with the correct rating.
key_facts: Thin metal strip that melts when current exceeds its rating
Single use -- must be replaced after blowing
Must be replaced with the same rating -- never substitute a higher-rated fuse

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of: (b) circuit breakers
concept: Circuit breakers serve the same protective function as fuses but can be reset by pushing them back in after they trip. They use a bimetallic strip or magnetic mechanism that opens the circuit when current is excessive. More convenient than fuses but should only be reset once in flight -- repeated tripping indicates a real fault.
exam_tip: Circuit breakers are resettable -- but only reset once in flight. Repeated tripping means a real fault exists.
key_facts: Resettable protection devices -- push in to reset after tripping
Only reset once in flight -- repeated tripping indicates a genuine fault
Can also be pulled out manually to isolate a faulty circuit

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of: (c) overload switches
concept: Overload switches (current limiters) protect high-current circuits like the alternator output. Unlike circuit breakers, they automatically reset once the overload condition clears. They provide continuous protection without requiring pilot intervention, but repeated cycling indicates a persistent fault that needs attention.
exam_tip: Overload switches auto-reset when the overload clears -- no pilot action needed, but repeated cycling means trouble.
key_facts: Automatically reset when the overload condition clears
Protect high-current circuits like alternator outputs
Repeated cycling indicates a persistent electrical fault

syllabus_desc: Explain operational principles for the handling of the electrical DC system, including: (a) avoiding overheating electrical services if operated during pre-flight
concept: Avoid overheating electrical services by not operating them beyond their rated duty cycle. Pitot heat, landing lights, and starter motors draw heavy current and generate heat. Running them continuously when they're designed for intermittent use can cause insulation breakdown and fire risk.
exam_tip: Don't run high-current services beyond their rated duty cycle -- overheating causes insulation failure and fire risk.
key_facts: High-draw services have rated duty cycles
Exceeding duty cycle causes overheating and insulation breakdown
Landing lights and starter motors are common examples of limited-duty items

syllabus_desc: Explain operational principles for the handling of the electrical DC system, including: (b) not starting or stopping the engine with unnecessary electrical equipment switched on
concept: Turn off all non-essential electrical equipment before starting or stopping the engine. Large voltage transients occur during these operations as the alternator comes online or goes offline. These surges can damage sensitive avionics, particularly modern glass cockpit displays and GPS receivers.
exam_tip: Radios and avionics off before engine start/stop -- voltage transients during these operations can damage electronics.
key_facts: Voltage transients occur during engine start-up and shutdown
Sensitive avionics can be damaged by voltage spikes
Standard practice: all electrical equipment off before start and shutdown

syllabus_desc: Explain operational principles for the handling of the electrical DC system, including: (c) avoiding prolonged use of the starter motor
concept: Don't crank the starter motor for prolonged periods. The starter draws extremely high current (hundreds of amps) and overheats quickly. Limit each start attempt to about 10 seconds with cooling pauses between attempts. Extended cranking can overheat the starter, flatten the battery, and damage wiring.
exam_tip: Limit starter motor use to about 10 seconds per attempt with cooling pauses -- it draws hundreds of amps.
key_facts: Starter motor draws very high current (hundreds of amps)
Limit cranking to about 10 seconds per attempt
Allow cooling time between start attempts to prevent overheating

syllabus_desc: Explain operational principles for the handling of the electrical DC system, including: (d) checking satisfactory operation of the alternator/generator after start, and periodically during flight
concept: After engine start, check that the alternator or generator is operating correctly. Verify the ammeter shows a charge (or positive output), the voltage is in the normal range, and any alternator warning light is extinguished. A failure to come online after start means you're running on battery alone.
exam_tip: After start: check ammeter for charge, voltage normal, and alternator warning light out.
key_facts: Check ammeter for charge indication after start
Verify alternator/generator warning light is extinguished
Voltage should be at normal system level (14V or 28V)

syllabus_desc: Explain operational principles for the handling of the electrical DC system, including: (e) ensuring the battery master is switched off before vacating the aircraft after flight
concept: Always ensure the battery master switch is off before leaving the aircraft. A master switch left on will drain the battery and could activate electrical services unattended, creating a fire risk. It also prevents any chance of the starter motor being activated accidentally.
exam_tip: Battery master off when leaving the aircraft -- prevents battery drain and unattended fire risk.
key_facts: Leaving master on drains the battery
Unattended electrical services create fire risk
Part of the standard shutdown checklist

syllabus_desc: Explain operational principles for the handling of the electrical DC system, including: (f) use of a ground power unit
concept: A ground power unit (GPU) should be used for extended ground operations to preserve battery charge for engine starting. Connect the GPU before turning on avionics, and ensure the correct voltage (12V or 24V) matches the aircraft system. Disconnect the GPU before taxi. Using a GPU also reduces wear on the battery during maintenance and testing.
exam_tip: Use a GPU for extended ground operations -- match the voltage to the aircraft system and disconnect before taxi.
key_facts: Preserves battery charge for engine starting
Must match aircraft system voltage (12V or 24V)
Disconnect before taxi

syllabus_desc: Explain the identification of: (a) an excessive charge rate
concept: An excessive charge rate shows as a large deflection on the ammeter toward the charge (+) side, especially if it persists well after engine start. This usually indicates a voltage regulator fault allowing the alternator to overcharge the battery. It can cause battery overheating, electrolyte boiling, and eventually thermal runaway. Disconnect the alternator if excessive charging persists.
exam_tip: Persistent high charge rate = probable voltage regulator failure. Risk of battery thermal runaway.
key_facts: Large persistent charge indication on ammeter
Usually caused by voltage regulator malfunction
Can lead to battery overheating and thermal runaway -- shed the alternator

syllabus_desc: Explain the identification of: (b) alternator/generator failure
concept: Alternator failure is indicated by the alternator warning light illuminating and the ammeter showing either zero output (left-zero type) or a discharge (centre-zero type). Once confirmed, shed non-essential electrical loads immediately to preserve battery endurance. Your battery life depends on its capacity and remaining loads.
exam_tip: Alternator failure: warning light ON, ammeter shows zero/discharge. Shed electrical loads to extend battery life.
key_facts: Warning light illuminates and ammeter confirms failure
Centre-zero ammeter shows discharge; left-zero shows zero
Immediately shed non-essential loads to extend battery endurance

syllabus_desc: Explain the identification of: (c) a total electric failure
concept: Total electrical failure means both the alternator and battery have failed or been disconnected. You lose all electrically-powered instruments, lights, radios, and flaps (if electrically operated). The engine keeps running (magnetos are self-powered). Fly to the nearest suitable aerodrome and use light signals from ATC if available.
exam_tip: Total electrical failure: engine still runs (magnetos are independent). No radios, no electric instruments. Use light signals.
key_facts: Engine continues running -- magnetos are self-powered
All electrically-powered instruments, radios, and lights are lost
Proceed to nearest suitable aerodrome; use ATC light signals if needed

syllabus_desc: Explain the risks associated with: (a) resetting blown fuse/popped circuit breakers
concept: Resetting a blown fuse or popped circuit breaker reconnects a potentially faulty circuit. If the fault is a short circuit, resetting could cause a fire. In flight, reset only once -- if it trips again, the fault is real and the circuit should be left open. Never hold a circuit breaker in against spring pressure.
exam_tip: Reset only once in flight -- if it trips again, leave it open. The fault is real and forcing it risks fire.
key_facts: Resetting reconnects a potentially faulty circuit
Only reset once in flight -- repeated tripping means a genuine fault
Never hold a circuit breaker in -- doing so defeats its protection

syllabus_desc: Explain the risks associated with: (b) using fuses as a switch
concept: Never use fuses as switches by pulling them to disconnect equipment. Fuses are not designed for repeated insertion and removal -- this damages the fuse holder contacts and can cause a loose connection that generates heat. Use the equipment switch to turn services on and off; fuses are for circuit protection only.
exam_tip: Fuses are circuit protection, not switches -- pulling and reinserting damages the contacts and creates heat.
key_facts: Fuses are for overcurrent protection, not routine switching
Repeated removal damages fuse holder contacts
Loose contacts from worn holders can generate heat and cause fire

syllabus_desc: Explain the issues related specifically to ‘technically enhanced aircraft’ (TEA) during the handling of electrical malfunctions.
concept: Technically Enhanced Aircraft (TEA) present unique electrical system challenges. TEA aircraft feature glass cockpits, integrated avionics suites, and advanced autopilot systems that increase electrical demand and complexity. During electrical failures, TEA aircraft may lose primary flight displays, navigation capability, and automated systems simultaneously. Pilots must understand degraded mode operations, know which instruments remain available on backup power, and be able to revert to basic 'steam gauge' flying if needed. Battery endurance in TEA is critical because more systems depend on electrical power than in conventional aircraft.
exam_tip: TEA = glass cockpit aircraft with high electrical dependency. Know what you lose in an electrical failure and how to manage degraded modes. Backup battery duration is critical.
key_facts: TEA = Technically Enhanced Aircraft (glass cockpit)
Higher electrical demand than conventional aircraft
Electrical failure can lose PFD, MFD, autopilot simultaneously
Must know degraded mode operations
Battery endurance more critical in TEA

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between gravity-fed and pump-fed fuel systems.
concept: Fuel systems come in two basic flavours: gravity-fed and pump-fed. The fundamental difference is how the fuel gets from the tank to the engine. In a gravity-fed system, the fuel tank is mounted higher than the engine—at least two to three feet of elevation difference. Gravity—simple physics—feeds fuel downward into the carburettor. No mechanical or electrical pump is needed. The system is simple, reliable, and requires no power to operate. Some light helicopters and very basic aircraft use gravity feed. The disadvantage is that it strictly limits where you can mount the fuel tank. You need that elevation, which sometimes conflicts with aircraft design and weight distribution. In a pump-fed system, an engine-driven pump (mechanically driven by the engine itself) or an electric boost pump (powered by the aircraft battery) supplies the fuel under pressure to the engine. Pump-fed systems are far more flexible: you can mount fuel tanks anywhere—low, high, wherever you need for weight distribution and structural efficiency. You can easily feed multiple tanks to a single engine. The system is more reliable in manoeuvring flight because mechanical or electrical pumps maintain consistent fuel pressure regardless of aircraft attitude. If you're rolling, diving, or pulling G-forces that would interrupt gravity feed, a pump maintains pressure. Most modern helicopters, including training helicopters like the R22, use pump-fed systems. The engine-driven pump provides main fuel flow during normal operation, and an electric boost pump provides fuel for engine starting and backup.
exam_tip: Gravity-fed relies on tank height above engine. Pump-fed uses engine or electric pump. Pump systems more flexible, reliable in manoeuvring.
key_facts: Gravity-fed: tank mounted higher than engine, fuel flows downward
Gravity-fed advantage: simple, reliable, no power required
Gravity-fed disadvantage: strictly limits tank mounting location
Pump-fed: mechanical (engine-driven) or electric pump supplies pressure
Pump-fed advantage: flexible tank location, works in any aircraft attitude
Pump-fed: more reliable in manoeuvring due to maintained pressure
Most helicopters use pump-fed systems (engine pump + electric boost pump)

syllabus_desc: For a typical fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (a) fuel strainers and filters
concept: Fuel strainers and filters remove contaminants (dirt, water, debris) from the fuel before it reaches the carburettor or injectors. The main strainer (gascolator) is usually at the lowest point of the fuel system and has a drain valve for preflight sampling. Check for water (which settles to the bottom) and sediment during every preflight.
exam_tip: Drain the fuel strainer during preflight -- check for water (clear layer at bottom) and sediment contamination.
key_facts: Removes water, dirt, and debris from fuel
Main strainer (gascolator) at lowest point of fuel system
Must be drained and checked during every preflight inspection

syllabus_desc: For a typical fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (b) engine-driven fuel pump
concept: The engine-driven fuel pump provides a reliable, continuous fuel supply at the correct pressure to the carburettor or injection system. It's mechanically driven from the engine accessory gearbox and is the primary fuel pump during flight. If it fails, the boost pump provides backup.
exam_tip: The engine-driven pump is the primary fuel pump in flight -- mechanically driven, so it runs whenever the engine runs.
key_facts: Mechanically driven from the engine accessory gearbox
Primary fuel pump during normal engine operation
Provides correct fuel pressure to carburettor or injection system

syllabus_desc: For a typical fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (c) boost (auxiliary) pump(s)
concept: The boost (auxiliary) pump is electrically operated and provides backup fuel pressure if the engine-driven pump fails. It's also used during engine start, take-off, and landing as a precaution, and to pressurise the fuel system to suppress vapour lock. Run the boost pump for fuel tank changes to maintain fuel pressure during the switchover.
exam_tip: Boost pump: backup for engine pump failure, used during start/take-off/landing, and prevents vapour lock.
key_facts: Electrically operated backup fuel pump
Used during start, take-off, landing, and tank changes
Pressurises the fuel system to prevent vapour lock

syllabus_desc: For a typical fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (d) engine primers and priming systems
concept: Engine primers inject raw fuel directly into the cylinder intake ports to provide a rich mixture for cold engine starting. The primer draws fuel from the fuel strainer and delivers it directly to one or more cylinders. After starting, the primer must be locked in the closed position -- an unlocked primer can cause a rich mixture and rough running.
exam_tip: Lock the primer after starting -- an unlocked primer leaks fuel into the intake, causing rough running.
key_facts: Injects raw fuel directly into cylinder intake ports for cold starts
Must be locked after engine start to prevent fuel leakage
An unlocked primer is a common cause of rough running after start

syllabus_desc: For a fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (a) tank filler caps and drains
concept: Tank filler caps must seal properly to prevent fuel leakage and contamination entry. They're vented or have a separate vent -- a sealed cap without venting would create a vacuum as fuel is consumed, eventually stopping fuel flow. During preflight, check caps are secure and seals are intact.
exam_tip: Check filler caps are secure during preflight -- a loose cap can cause fuel siphoning in flight.
key_facts: Must seal to prevent leakage and contamination
Many caps are vented or work with separate tank vents
Loose caps can siphon fuel overboard in flight

syllabus_desc: For a fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (b) expansion spaces
concept: Expansion spaces are unfilled volumes at the top of each fuel tank that allow fuel to expand as temperature increases. Without expansion space, thermal expansion could rupture the tank or force fuel out the vents. Never fill a tank beyond the specified full level -- leave room for expansion.
exam_tip: Never overfill fuel tanks -- expansion space prevents fuel expanding and rupturing the tank in hot conditions.
key_facts: Allows for thermal expansion of fuel as temperature rises
Overfilling removes expansion space and risks tank damage
Fuel volume increases approximately 1% per 10 degrees C temperature rise

syllabus_desc: For a fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (c) tank vents
concept: Tank vents allow air to enter the tank as fuel is consumed, preventing a vacuum from forming that would stop fuel flow. They also allow expanded fuel vapour to escape. A blocked vent is a serious hazard -- the engine will eventually stop as the fuel can't flow. Check vents are clear during preflight.
exam_tip: A blocked fuel vent will starve the engine -- fuel can't flow if air can't replace it. Check vents during preflight.
key_facts: Allows air to replace fuel as it's consumed
Blocked vent = vacuum builds = fuel flow stops = engine failure
Check vents are clear during every preflight inspection

syllabus_desc: For a fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (d) baffles
concept: Baffles are internal partitions inside fuel tanks that reduce fuel sloshing during manoeuvres. Without baffles, fuel would surge to one side during turns or turbulence, potentially uncovering the fuel outlet and allowing air into the fuel lines. Baffles have holes to allow fuel to flow between sections while limiting surge.
exam_tip: Baffles reduce fuel sloshing in tanks -- they prevent the fuel outlet being uncovered during manoeuvres.
key_facts: Internal partitions that limit fuel surge during manoeuvres
Prevent fuel outlet from being uncovered by sloshing
Have holes to allow fuel to flow between tank sections

syllabus_desc: For a fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (e) sumps and drains
concept: Sumps and drains are located at the lowest point of each fuel tank to collect water and sediment that settle by gravity. During preflight, drain a sample from each sump and check for water (clear layer below the fuel) and contaminants. Water in fuel is a serious hazard -- it can block jets or freeze and stop fuel flow.
exam_tip: Drain fuel sumps during every preflight -- water settles to the bottom and can freeze or block fuel flow.
key_facts: Located at lowest point of each tank to collect water and sediment
Drain and check during every preflight
Water in fuel can freeze at altitude and block fuel lines

syllabus_desc: For a fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (f) fuel quantity detectors
concept: Fuel quantity detectors (gauges) measure the amount of fuel in each tank. Most light aircraft use float-type or capacitance-type sensors. Fuel gauges in light aircraft are notoriously inaccurate, especially when not indicating full or empty. Always cross-check gauge readings against dipstick measurements and known fuel burn.
exam_tip: Don't trust fuel gauges blindly -- cross-check with dipstick and fuel burn calculations. They're only required to read accurately at empty.
key_facts: Float-type or capacitance-type sensors measure fuel level
Light aircraft fuel gauges are often inaccurate at mid-range readings
Only required to be accurate at the 'empty' indication

syllabus_desc: For a fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (g) fuel strainers and filters
concept: Fuel strainers and filters in the fuel system remove water, dirt, and contaminants at various points between the tanks and the engine. The main strainer (gascolator) has a preflight drain. Finger screens in the tank outlets, inline filters, and carburettor inlet screens provide additional filtration layers.
exam_tip: Multiple filtration points between tank and engine -- the gascolator is the primary one you check during preflight.
key_facts: Multiple filtration stages: tank screens, gascolator, inline filters
Gascolator is the primary strainer with a preflight drain valve
Regular draining and filter checks prevent contamination reaching the engine

syllabus_desc: For a fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (h) tank selector valves
concept: Tank selector valves allow the pilot to choose which fuel tank feeds the engine -- left, right, both, or off. Correct selector management is critical for fuel balance and ensuring the engine is fed from a tank with fuel. Mismanagement of selectors (selecting an empty tank or leaving on 'off') is a well-known cause of engine failure.
exam_tip: Always confirm the selector is on a tank with fuel -- selector mismanagement is a common cause of engine failure.
key_facts: Selects which tank feeds the engine: left, right, both, or off
Proper management prevents fuel imbalance and engine fuel starvation
Selecting an empty tank or 'off' position will stop the engine

syllabus_desc: For a fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (i) cross feed valves
concept: Cross-feed valves allow fuel to be transferred between tanks or allow one engine to draw fuel from the opposite wing tank (in multi-engine aircraft). In singles, cross-feeding can help balance fuel loads. Use according to the POH -- incorrect use can lead to fuel imbalance or starvation.
exam_tip: Cross-feed allows fuel transfer between tanks -- use per the POH to maintain fuel balance.
key_facts: Allows fuel transfer between tanks for load balancing
In multi-engine aircraft, allows one engine to draw from opposite tank
Must be used according to POH procedures

syllabus_desc: For a fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (j) fuel flow meters
concept: Fuel flow meters measure the rate of fuel consumption in gallons or litres per hour. They help the pilot monitor engine performance, manage fuel, and detect problems (abnormally high flow might indicate a leak or stuck mixture). Totalising flow meters also calculate fuel remaining based on cumulative consumption.
exam_tip: Fuel flow meters help with fuel management -- abnormal flow rates can indicate engine or fuel system problems.
key_facts: Measures fuel consumption rate (gallons or litres per hour)
Helps monitor engine performance and manage fuel reserves
Totalising meters calculate fuel remaining from cumulative consumption

syllabus_desc: Describe: (a) the general rules for refuelling
concept: General refuelling rules: use only the approved fuel type/grade for the aircraft, ensure the aircraft is properly grounded (bonded) to prevent static discharge, supervise the refuelling process, check fuel for correct colour and contamination, and never refuel near ignition sources. In NZ, AVGAS 100LL is dyed blue and Jet A-1 is clear/straw-coloured.
exam_tip: Always bond the aircraft before refuelling -- static discharge can ignite fuel vapour. Use only the approved fuel grade.
key_facts: Use only approved fuel type: AVGAS 100LL (blue) for piston engines
Bond (earth) the aircraft to prevent static discharge
Check fuel colour and clarity before and after refuelling

syllabus_desc: Describe: (b) the correct use of fuel tank dipsticks
concept: Fuel tank dipsticks are calibrated measuring devices inserted through the filler cap opening to physically measure the fuel level. They give a more accurate reading than fuel gauges. Each aircraft type has specifically calibrated dipsticks -- don't use a dipstick from a different aircraft type as tank shapes vary.
exam_tip: Dipsticks are more accurate than fuel gauges -- but use the correct dipstick calibrated for your aircraft type.
key_facts: Physically measures fuel level in the tank
More accurate than cockpit fuel gauges
Must be calibrated for the specific aircraft type and tank shape

syllabus_desc: Describe: (c) the correct use of magna-sticks
concept: Magna-sticks are magnetic dipsticks that allow you to read the fuel level without removing the filler cap in some installations. The magnetic indicator on the outside of the tank moves in response to a float inside. They provide a quick visual check of approximate fuel quantity.
exam_tip: Magna-sticks give a quick external fuel level reading using a magnetic float -- convenient but cross-check with dipstick.
key_facts: Magnetic float system provides external fuel level reading
Gives a quick approximate reading without opening the filler cap
Should be cross-checked with a calibrated dipstick for accuracy

syllabus_desc: Describe the correct procedures to be used for carrying out fuel quality checks.
concept: Fuel quality checks are simple but non-negotiable. At refuelling, you've already verified the grade. On pre-flight, you'll drain a sample from the lowest sump point of each tank into a clear container (usually a fuel sampler cup at the refuelling point). Let it sit for a few seconds; look for water settling at the bottom—it'll be cloudy or a distinct layer. Check for sediment (particles, rust, debris) which should be absent. The fuel should be clear and bright, with the correct colour for the grade: Avgas 100LL is typically light blue, Avgas 100 is green, Jet A is clear to pale straw. Any discolouration, cloudiness, or sediment means the tank needs flushing or fuel dumping. On a multi-tank aircraft like a twin, check every tank—you might find contamination in one wing tank but not the other. If you find water, don't fly; declare the aircraft unserviceable and contact maintenance. This isn't a shortcut—it's the difference between a normal flight and an engine failure at low level.
exam_tip: Sample every tank pre-flight. Look for water (cloudiness at bottom), sediment (particles), and correct colour. Cloudy fuel = no-go. Know the colour standards for fuel grades used in NZ.
key_facts: Drain fuel sample from lowest sump point of each tank
Sample into clear container; let settle briefly
Look for water (cloudiness), sediment (particles), and correct colour
Avgas 100LL: light blue; Avgas 100: green; Jet A: clear to straw
Any cloudiness or sediment means tank requires maintenance
Check every tank on multi-tank aircraft
Fuel contamination is a no-go condition

syllabus_desc: Describe the indications of water in a fuel sample.
concept: Water in fuel is the enemy. It'll corrode fuel system components, it'll plug injectors and filters, and it can freeze in the fuel lines at altitude, blocking fuel flow mid-flight. When you drain a fuel sample and water's present, you'll see a distinct layer at the bottom of the container—it looks cloudy or milky if it's emulsified (mixed with fuel), or it might be a clear layer below the fuel. Tiny droplets suspended throughout the fuel make it look hazy or turbid. On a hot day, you might see no water initially, but as the sample cools in your hand, water that was vapour condenses out and becomes visible. If you let the sample sit for a minute, water will settle to the bottom and you'll see it clearly. The colour changes too: normal fuel is bright and clear; fuel with water is dull, cloudy, or has a distinct separation. In serious contamination, you might even smell the water—it has that distinct odour. Don't take any chances: if you see water, the aircraft doesn't fly, and you need to get maintenance involved to find the source (usually a leaking fuel cap or a tank vent failure in damp conditions).
exam_tip: Water indicators: cloudiness, haze, distinct bottom layer, colour change (dull instead of bright). Let sample sit to let water settle. Any visible water = no-go.
key_facts: Water appears as cloudiness, haze, or distinct layer at bottom of sample
Water may be emulsified (mixed throughout) or separated (bottom layer)
Cool sample in hand; water vapour condenses as temperature drops
Fuel with water looks dull or turbid instead of bright and clear
Serious contamination has visible water column at bottom
Water in fuel risks filter blockage, injector damage, and fuel line freeze
Any visible water is a no-go condition

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of a blocked fuel tank vent in a gravity feed fuel system.
concept: A blocked vent in a gravity-feed system creates a vacuum in the tank as fuel is consumed. The engine will run fine at first, but as fuel level drops, the vacuum gets stronger and eventually strangles the fuel flow. You'll notice the engine running rough, losing power, and if it's bad enough, cutting out completely. The problem is it's intermittent—climb or descend slightly, maybe the siphoning effect changes, and fuel flows again. You might chalk it up to carburettor ice or fuel starvation from leaning the mixture. But the real giveaway is that fuel quantity gauge starts fluctuating wildly because the float is bouncing around as the vacuum sucks it. If you suspect a blocked vent mid-flight, you might try a gentle climb or descent to change the pressure differential, or you might open a cabin air vent to let air into the cabin, which could relieve some back-pressure. But really, it's a land-and-sort-it situation. On the ground, check that the vent isn't clogged with dirt, ice, or a blocked valve.
exam_tip: Blocked vent symptoms: engine rough or cutting out as fuel level drops, fluctuating fuel gauge, progressive power loss. On the ground, check vent for blockage or ice.
key_facts: Blocked vent creates vacuum as fuel is consumed
Engine roughness and power loss as vacuum builds
Fuel quantity gauge fluctuates erratically
Problem worsens as fuel level drops (more vacuum)
Changing altitude may temporarily relieve symptom
Vents can block with dirt, ice, or internal failure
Land immediately if blocked vent suspected

syllabus_desc: Describe the importance of correct management of fuel selection.
concept: On a twin-engine aeroplane, you've got multiple fuel tanks—typically left wing, right wing, and maybe a centre tank. You've got a selector valve on the flight deck that lets you choose which tank feeds each engine. Get this wrong and you'll have an engine failure, or worse, an asymmetric thrust situation where one engine's running and the other's starved. Proper fuel management means: know your tank capacities, monitor which tank is supplying each engine, understand your fuel consumption rates, and plan your takeoff sequence. Some aircraft require you to burn from specific tanks in a specific order to maintain centre of gravity. If you're flying out of Hamilton and you've got an asymmetric load (say, full right tank and half-full left), you need to be deliberate about which tank feeds which engine so you don't end up with both engines trying to feed from the depleted side. On some operations, you'll cross-feed in flight to balance consumption. And critically, if one engine dies, you need to immediately switch that engine to an alternate tank (if available) or declare the situation and plan your approach. Sloppy fuel selection has killed a lot of pilots—it's not a details-to-ignore kind of thing.
exam_tip: Know your tank layout and capacities. Plan takeoff fuel sequence. Monitor consumption. Maintain CG balance. If one engine dies, switch to alternate fuel source immediately. Document fuel selection in your pre-flight checklist.
key_facts: Multi-tank aircraft require deliberate tank selection
Wrong selection causes single-engine failure or dual failure
Tank selector valves must be properly identified and used
Fuel consumption must be monitored to maintain aircraft balance
Asymmetric fuel loads require cross-feed planning
Engine failure due to fuel selection requires immediate alternate tank selection
Fuel management is part of pre-takeoff and in-flight checklist

syllabus_desc: Detail the actions recommended in the case of loss of power through faulty fuel selection.
concept: You've selected the wrong tank, one engine's lost power, and suddenly you're flying an asymmetric twin—not a fun situation. First thing: don't panic. Immediately check your fuel selector: it's probably in the wrong position. Switch it to the correct tank, and usually the engine will pick up within 5–10 seconds as fuel pressure builds. If the engine doesn't respond, try the opposite tank if you've got alternatives, or try your crossfeed valve if available. If you've got an engine failure that you can't recover—the selector's already in the right position and fuel's available elsewhere—then you're declaring a single-engine emergency with ATC. You've lost asymmetric thrust, so trim carefully, reduce power on the good engine to maintain control, and plan your descent to the nearest suitable aerodrome. Call it early so ATC can vector you and prepare the airfield. Don't try to climb or maintain altitude on one engine in a twin with a significant loading—you'll just burn fuel inefficiently and stress the airframe. Get down safely.
exam_tip: Wrong fuel selection = engine loss. Immediately check and correct selector position. Verify fuel's actually in that tank (gauge might show empty). If engine doesn't recover, declare emergency and plan landing at nearest suitable aerodrome. Manage asymmetric thrust carefully.
key_facts: Check fuel selector immediately when engine power is lost
Switch to correct tank; engine usually responds within 5–10 seconds
Confirm fuel exists in the selected tank (check gauge)
Try crossfeed if available
If unrecovered, declare single-engine emergency
Plan descent to nearest suitable aerodrome
Manage asymmetric thrust: reduce good engine power, trim carefully
Don't attempt to climb on one engine unnecessarily

syllabus_desc: Describe the typical methods of cross-feeding fuel.
concept: Cross-feeding is transferring fuel from one tank to another, usually from a wing tank to the opposite wing tank. On many twins, you've got a crossfeed selector valve that lets you feed a single engine from the opposite wing tank. Why would you do this? If one wing tank's running low and the other's still full, you can cross-feed to balance consumption and maintain centre of gravity. If an engine's on the verge of failure and you've got fuel in the opposite tank, you can try cross-feeding to see if the problem's a blocked feed line instead of total fuel starvation. Some aircraft have manual crossfeed valves; others have electric selectors on the panel. The procedure is simple: move the crossfeed selector to 'on' or to the desired position, monitor that the engine stays running, and watch your fuel gauges to ensure you're actually consuming from the opposite tank. Cross-feeding during cruise helps balance aircraft loading on long flights. Some operations require specific cross-feed sequences—check your flight manual. And remember: you can only cross-feed if you've got fuel in both tanks and the crossfeed system is serviceable. You can't pull fuel from an empty tank.
exam_tip: Crossfeed transfers fuel between left and right tanks. Used to balance consumption and maintain CG. Operate selector, monitor engine and fuel gauges. Confirm fuel actually transfers. Know your aircraft's crossfeed valve location and operation.
key_facts: Crossfeed transfers fuel between wing tanks to maintain balance
Valve is either manual or electric selector on flight deck
Monitor fuel gauges to confirm crossfeed operation
Engine should stay running; observe no fluctuation
Used during cruise for extended flights to manage CG
Can help if one feed line is blocked (try crossfeed to opposite tank)
Requires fuel in both tanks; can't transfer from empty tank
