syllabus_desc: Explain the main functions of an engine oil system.
concept: Engine oil is absolutely vital to engine operation—it's far more than just a lubricant. An oil system serves five interconnected functions, and failure of any one can destroy an engine rapidly. Lubrication is the obvious one: oil creates a slippery film between moving parts—pistons sliding in cylinders, crankshaft spinning in bearings, camshaft lobes sliding against tappets. Without this film, metal-to-metal friction would generate heat so intense that the engine would seize within seconds. Cooling is the second function. Engine oil absorbs heat from the engine's hot spots and carries it away to the oil cooler (or in simpler systems, to the air flowing around the engine). Combustion temperatures exceed 2000°C in the cylinders; oil carries away excess heat so components don't overheat. Cleaning is the third function. As the engine runs, combustion byproducts and wear particles accumulate. The oil carries these contaminants—suspended as a slurry—back to the oil filter, where the filter removes them. Without this cleaning action, sludge and debris would accumulate and block passages. Sealing is the fourth function. Piston rings are the primary seal between the combustion chamber and the crankcase, but they're not perfect. Gas molecules leak past the rings, a process called blowby. The oil's viscosity helps seal these tiny gaps, preventing excessive blowby and keeping pressure in the combustion chamber where it belongs. Corrosion protection is the fifth function. Oil contains additives—detergents, dispersants, anti-wear compounds, and corrosion inhibitors—that protect metal surfaces from oxidation and acid attack, especially during cold starts when moisture is present. All five functions must work together for engine health.
exam_tip: Oil lubricates, cools, cleans (removes wear particles), seals cylinders (reduces blowby), protects against corrosion.
key_facts: Lubrication: reduces friction between moving parts
Cooling: absorbs and carries away heat from hot spots
Cleaning: carries combustion byproducts and wear particles to filter
Sealing: reduces blowby past piston rings
Corrosion protection: additives protect from oxidation and acid attack
Each function essential—failure of one can destroy engine
Oil additives include detergents, dispersants, anti-wear, inhibitors

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of temperature on the viscosity and lubrication qualities of oil.
concept: Oil viscosity is its thickness, and it changes dramatically with temperature. Cold oil is thick and sluggish—on a winter morning at Christchurch, you might have to wait longer before the oil pressure stabilises because thick oil doesn't flow easily through the bearings. Hot oil is thin and watery—high altitude or a long descent in summer heat makes the oil fluid, which is good for flow but bad for the film between bearing surfaces. The viscosity needs to be in a sweet spot: thick enough to maintain a protective film between bearings (low viscosity = poor lubrication = bearing wear), thin enough to flow and circulate (high viscosity = poor flow = pressure drop and cooling problems). That's why you use specific oil grades: a SAE 40 oil stays relatively consistent across temperature ranges, while a SAE 10 oil is thin and flows better in winter but might be too thin in summer. Modern ashless dispersant oils (used in aviation piston engines) maintain viscosity better across temperature swings than straight mineral oil, which is why you use them.
exam_tip: Cold oil: thick, high pressure initially, slow to warm. Hot oil: thin, low pressure, poor bearing film. Know your oil grade and acceptable pressure/temperature ranges. Monitor both gauges.
key_facts: Oil thickens when cold; thins when hot
Cold engine: high oil pressure, long warm-up needed
Hot engine: low oil pressure, risk of bearing damage
Oil viscosity must balance flow (thin) and bearing protection (thick)
SAE ratings (10, 30, 40, etc.) define oil weight
Multi-grade oils (e.g., 15W-50) adapt to temperature changes
Ashless dispersant oils maintain viscosity better than mineral oils

syllabus_desc: Compare the features of the ashless dispersant (AD) oils used in piston aero- engines with straight mineral oil and detergent oils.
concept: Straight mineral oil is the simple stuff—refined petroleum with minimal additives. It's used for engine break-in because it lets piston rings and cylinder walls wear against each other to form a proper seal. Once break-in's done, you switch to ashless dispersant (AD) oil. AD oil contains additives that grab contamination particles (carbon, metal wear, acids) and hold them in suspension throughout the oil rather than letting them settle and form sludge. 'Ashless' means the additives don't leave metalite ash deposits when they burn—important because ash deposits on spark plugs and valve seats cause problems. Detergent oils (automotive type) clean existing deposits off surfaces, which sounds great except those loosened chunks can block oil passages and filters. That's why you never use automotive detergent oils in aircraft engines. AD oil prevents deposits forming in the first place without scrubbing existing ones loose. In your helicopter's piston engine, AD oil is the standard for normal operations after break-in. Don't mix mineral and AD oils unless the manufacturer specifically allows it.
exam_tip: Mineral oil = break-in only. AD oil = normal operations, holds contaminants in suspension, ashless (no ash deposits). Detergent oil = automotive, NOT for aircraft (loosens deposits that block passages). Never mix types unless approved.
key_facts: Straight mineral oil: minimal additives, used for break-in. Ashless dispersant (AD): holds contaminants in suspension, prevents sludge. 'Ashless' = no metallic ash deposits on plugs/valves. Detergent oils: automotive, loosens existing deposits (dangerous in aircraft). Never use automotive detergent oil in aero engines. Don't mix mineral and AD oils unless manufacturer-approved. AD oil is standard for normal operations post-break-in.

syllabus_desc: With respect to oil grades, differentiate between the commercial aviation numbers and SAE ratings.
concept: Oil grades are specified two ways in aviation—commercial aviation numbers (developed specifically for aircraft) and SAE ratings (developed for automobiles and industries). The two systems roughly correspond but aren't identical, and you absolutely must follow your Pilot's Operating Handbook's specification exactly. Aviation oil grades use numbers like 80, 100, 120, and others. These numbers don't correspond directly to viscosity units; they're simply a numbering system developed by aviation authorities. The grade 100 is by far the most common for light piston aircraft and helicopters like the R22. SAE grades use a different numbering system: 10W, 20, 30, 40, 50, and so on. The 'W' stands for winter and applies to oils that flow at cold temperatures. SAE 20 is roughly equivalent to aviation 80, SAE 30 to aviation 100, and SAE 40 to aviation 120, but that's an approximation, not a precise conversion. If your POH says to use aviation 100, you should use aviation 100—not automobile SAE 30. The difference in additives and viscosity characteristics matters. Some modern aircraft and helicopters are approved for multi-viscosity oils (like SAE 15W-40), which flow better in cold conditions while maintaining proper viscosity when hot. But again, this must be explicitly approved in your POH. Read your operating handbook, stick to its specification, and don't assume equivalence between aviation and SAE grades.
exam_tip: Aviation grades (80, 100, 120) vs SAE (10W, 20, 30, 40); roughly correspond but not identical. Follow POH exactly, don't assume equivalence.
key_facts: Aviation grades: 80, 100, 120 developed for aircraft engines
SAE grades: 10W, 20, 30, 40, 50 for automotive and industrial
Roughly correspond: avi 80 ≈ SAE 20, avi 100 ≈ SAE 30, avi 120 ≈ SAE 40
NOT exact equivalents—additives and characteristics differ
Always use grade specified in POH, not assumed equivalent
W suffix in SAE grade indicates winter (cold flow) performance
Some modern engines approved for multi-viscosity (e.g. SAE 15W-40)

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between a wet sump and a dry sump oil system.
concept: Wet sump and dry sump are two entirely different approaches to storing engine oil, and each has distinct advantages and limitations. A wet sump system is simpler and far more common in light aircraft and helicopters. The oil pan is bolted directly to the bottom of the engine block and serves as both the sump (storage reservoir) and the structural part of the engine. Oil gravity-feeds back into the pan after circulating through the engine. The system's simple, cheap to manufacture, reliable, and requires no additional plumbing beyond the engine itself. The downside is that a wet sump limits the aircraft's attitude envelope. The oil sump relies on gravity to keep oil against the pan's internal baffles and pickups. If you pitch the nose up steeply, roll inverted, or manoeuvre aggressively, the oil can slosh away from the pickup point, starving the oil pump and destroying the engine in seconds. Most light helicopters tolerate moderate attitudes, but extended inverted flight or extreme attitudes can cause oil starvation. That's why aerobatic aircraft avoid wet sumps. A dry sump system separates the oil storage tank from the engine itself. Oil circulates from the engine to a remote tank, and a scavenge pump actively sucks oil out of the engine and back to the tank. A separate feed pump delivers oil from the tank back into the engine. This system works in any attitude—aerobatic pilots love dry sumps. The downside is complexity: you've got multiple pumps, remote tank plumbing, and more components to maintain. It's also heavier due to the additional plumbing and tank. Most small piston helicopters use wet sump because of the weight and cost advantage, though some military or high-performance helicopters use dry sump.
exam_tip: Wet sump: pan bolted to engine, simple, attitude-limited. Dry sump: separate tank with scavenge pump, works any attitude, heavier/complex.
key_facts: Wet sump: oil pan integral to engine block, gravity return
Wet sump simple, cheap, reliable, but attitude-limited
Wet sump: oil can slosh away from pickup in steep attitudes
Dry sump: separate oil tank with scavenge and feed pumps
Dry sump works in any aircraft attitude including inverted
Dry sump: more complex, heavier, higher maintenance
Light helicopters typically use wet sump (weight/cost advantage)

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of: (a) the engine-driven oil pump and pressure relief valve
concept: The engine-driven oil pump draws oil from the sump and delivers it under pressure to all engine components needing lubrication -- crankshaft main bearings, big end bearings, camshaft, and accessories. A pressure relief valve limits maximum oil pressure by diverting excess oil back to the sump when pressure exceeds the set limit.
exam_tip: The oil pump is engine-driven and provides pressurised oil to all critical bearings. The relief valve prevents over-pressure.
key_facts: Gear-type pump driven by the engine accessory gearbox
Delivers oil under pressure to all critical bearings
Pressure relief valve diverts excess oil back to the sump

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of: (b) oil lines, passages and galleries
concept: Oil lines, passages, and galleries are the network of tubes and channels that carry pressurised oil from the pump to all the components needing lubrication. Main galleries run through the crankcase to feed the main bearings and crankshaft, with smaller passages branching off to camshaft bearings, valve gear, and accessory drives.
exam_tip: Oil galleries are internal passages in the crankcase that distribute pressurised oil to all critical components.
key_facts: Network of tubes and internal passages distributing oil
Main galleries feed crankshaft main bearings
Smaller passages branch to camshaft, valve gear, and accessories

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of: (c) oil sumps, and scavenge pumps
concept: In a dry-sump system, oil collects in the bottom of the crankcase and is returned to a separate oil tank by scavenge pumps. The scavenge pump(s) have a higher capacity than the pressure pump to ensure oil doesn't accumulate in the crankcase. In a wet-sump system, oil simply drains back to an integral sump at the bottom of the crankcase.
exam_tip: Dry sump: separate tank + scavenge pumps. Wet sump: oil sits in the crankcase. Scavenge pump capacity exceeds pressure pump.
key_facts: Wet sump: oil stored in integral crankcase sump
Dry sump: separate oil tank, scavenge pumps return oil from crankcase
Scavenge pump capacity exceeds pressure pump to prevent oil accumulation

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of: (d) oil cooler
concept: The oil cooler removes excess heat from the engine oil before it recirculates. It works like a small radiator -- oil passes through tubes or channels while air flows over cooling fins. A thermostatic bypass valve routes oil around the cooler when the oil is cold (during warm-up) and through the cooler when it reaches operating temperature.
exam_tip: The oil cooler maintains oil temperature -- a bypass valve routes cold oil around it during warm-up.
key_facts: Air-cooled heat exchanger that removes heat from oil
Thermostatic bypass valve routes cold oil around the cooler
Essential for maintaining oil within the correct temperature range

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of: (e) oil tank and filter
concept: In a dry-sump system, the oil tank stores the engine oil supply separate from the crankcase. It includes an oil level sight gauge or dipstick, a filler cap, and breather venting. The oil filter (either full-flow or bypass type) traps particles and contaminants before they can damage bearing surfaces. The filter should be checked and changed at regular maintenance intervals.
exam_tip: Check oil level on the dipstick or sight gauge during preflight. The oil filter protects bearings from contaminants.
key_facts: Oil tank (dry sump) stores the oil supply externally
Oil filter traps contaminants to protect bearing surfaces
Check oil level during every preflight inspection

syllabus_desc: Explain the need for periodic oil changes.
concept: Oil degrades with use. The ashless dispersant additive gets depleted, the oil picks up moisture and contaminants, carbon buildup increases, and eventually the oil's no longer effective at lubrication and cooling. Typically, you'll change piston engine oil every 50–100 hours (your POH specifies exactly) or annually, whichever comes first. On a twin that's being flown regularly (50+ hours a month), you'll hit the hour limit first. If the aircraft is flown infrequently, oil degrades anyway and you'll hit the annual limit. The oil filter gets changed at every oil change—it's collecting debris, and once it's full of gunk, it restricts flow. Your pre-flight oil check is a visual inspection of quantity only; the contamination and degradation you can't see until you have oil analysed in a lab (which some operators do). Flying with old, degraded oil is like flying with a leaking seal bearing—you're running on borrowed time waiting for bearing failure.
exam_tip: Oil changes at 50–100 hour intervals or annually. Filter changed at every oil change. Old oil = poor lubrication and cooling. Stick to your POH change schedule.
key_facts: Oil degrades with time and use
Ashless dispersant additive depletes gradually
Typical change interval: 50–100 hours or annually
Whichever interval comes first is the limit
Oil filter changed at every oil change
Regular oil sampling can detect contamination early
Flying with expired oil risks bearing damage and engine failure

syllabus_desc: Describe the likely results of operating an engine with: (a) incorrect oil type
concept: Using the wrong oil type (viscosity grade or specification) can mean the oil is too thin or too thick for the operating conditions. Too thin: inadequate film strength at high temperatures, leading to accelerated bearing wear. Too thick: poor flow at cold temperatures, starving components during start-up. Always use the grade specified in the POH.
exam_tip: Wrong oil type: too thin = bearing wear at high temp; too thick = poor cold flow. Use the POH-specified grade.
key_facts: Too thin: inadequate protection at operating temperature
Too thick: poor circulation at cold start, starving bearings
Always use the oil type and grade specified in the POH

syllabus_desc: Describe the likely results of operating an engine with: (b) incorrect oil quantity
concept: Too much oil can cause increased oil consumption as excess is blown past piston rings and out the breather. It can also cause foaming, which reduces lubrication effectiveness. Too little oil means reduced cooling capacity and risk of oil starvation to critical components, especially in manoeuvres that slosh oil away from the pickup.
exam_tip: Too much oil: foaming, high consumption. Too little: overheating, bearing starvation in manoeuvres.
key_facts: Overfill: foaming, high consumption, oil blown past rings and breather
Underfill: reduced cooling, risk of oil starvation in manoeuvres
Maintain oil level within the specified range on the dipstick

syllabus_desc: Describe the likely causes of: (a) low oil pressure
concept: Low oil pressure can be caused by: low oil quantity, worn bearings (increasing clearances), a weak pressure relief valve, oil that's too thin (wrong grade or overheated), a blocked oil pickup screen, or oil pump wear. Low oil pressure is serious -- without adequate pressure, bearings will fail rapidly. Land as soon as practicable.
exam_tip: Low oil pressure = land as soon as practicable. Common causes: low oil level, worn bearings, overheated oil.
key_facts: Causes: low oil level, worn bearings, weak relief valve, thin oil, pump wear
Inadequate oil pressure leads to rapid bearing failure
Low oil pressure is a land-as-soon-as-practicable situation

syllabus_desc: Describe the likely causes of: (b) high oil pressure
concept: High oil pressure can be caused by: oil that's too thick (wrong grade or very cold), a blocked oil cooler, or a stuck pressure relief valve. It can also occur during cold start before the oil warms up, which is normal and temporary. Persistently high pressure strains seals and gaskets and can cause leaks.
exam_tip: High oil pressure: usually cold oil (normal at start-up) or a stuck relief valve. Persistent high pressure strains seals.
key_facts: Causes: cold (thick) oil, blocked cooler, stuck relief valve
Normal briefly after cold start as oil warms up
Persistent high pressure can burst seals and cause leaks

syllabus_desc: Describe the likely causes of: (c) high oil temperature
concept: High oil temperature indicates the oil is being overheated. Causes include: prolonged high power at low airspeed (climb), low oil quantity (less oil doing the same cooling), blocked oil cooler, or a stuck bypass valve keeping oil away from the cooler. High temperature reduces oil viscosity, which then leads to low oil pressure -- a dangerous combination.
exam_tip: High oil temp + low oil pressure is a dangerous combination -- reduce power, increase airspeed, and land promptly.
key_facts: Causes: prolonged climb, low oil level, blocked cooler, stuck bypass valve
High temperature thins the oil, leading to low pressure
High temp + low pressure = critical -- land as soon as practicable

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between a fluctuating or low oil pressure reading accompanied by a rise in oil temperature.
concept: This combination is a red flag that points to bearing wear. Here's the chain of events: as a bearing wears, the clearance between the rotating shaft and the bearing surface increases. Oil that should stay in a thin film between the surfaces starts leaking past, increasing oil flow but reducing the protective film. With less film pressure, the bearing surfaces make closer contact, friction rises, heat generation spikes. You see the oil temperature climbing. Meanwhile, the pressure reading drops because the worn bearing isn't holding pressure—the oil's just flowing through and leaking out. The fluctuation you're seeing is the pressure bouncing around as the bearing rocks or the oil flow becomes intermittent. This is a bearing on the way out. You're not going to recover from this in flight, and continuing to run the engine makes it worse. Declare a precautionary landing immediately and get the aircraft on the ground and into maintenance. Don't power back up until a mechanic has inspected and cleared it.
exam_tip: Low pressure + high temperature + fluctuation = bearing wear. Immediate landing required. Don't continue running. Each minute of flight accelerates bearing damage.
key_facts: Bearing wear increases clearance between shaft and bearing surface
Increased clearance = oil leakage from bearing film
Oil leakage = lower pressure, higher friction, higher temperature
Fluctuating pressure indicates bearing movement or intermittent contact
This combination is a critical failure in progress
Immediate landing required; do not continue flying
Aircraft grounded until maintenance inspection and repair

syllabus_desc: Describe the actions the pilot should take in the low pressure/high temperature situation.
concept: You've got low pressure and high temperature gauges. First, verify it's a real problem and not a gauge fault: cross-check with the other engine if it's a twin, or look for other symptoms like roughness or unusual vibration. If both engines show normal readings, suspect the gauge. If it's one engine and real, reduce power immediately to the minimum needed to maintain safe flight and climb (if possible). Reducing load reduces friction and might bring the temperature down and pressure up slightly. Get a descent planned to your nearest suitable aerodrome—you're landing soon. Call ATC immediately and declare a precautionary landing; they'll clear vectors and prepare the field. Don't try to climb to altitude or stretch the glide; just head for the nearest place you can put it down safely. Avoid manoeuvres that increase engine load or G-forces. Once on the ground, ensure the engine is shut down properly and the aircraft is secured. Don't restart it; let maintenance inspect it. If you're at an outstation without immediate maintenance, inform the operator immediately.
exam_tip: Verify real (not gauge fault). Reduce power. Plan descent to nearest suitable airfield. Declare precautionary landing with ATC. No climbing or stretching. Land smoothly and shut down. Notify operator; don't restart.
key_facts: First: verify it's a real problem, not gauge failure
Reduce power immediately to minimum safe level
Plan descent to nearest suitable aerodrome
Declare precautionary landing with ATC early
Avoid manoeuvres increasing engine load
Land and shut down; do not restart
Secure aircraft and notify operator
Maintenance inspection required before further flight

syllabus_desc: Describe: (a) mechanical advantage
concept: Mechanical advantage is the ratio of the output force to the input force in a machine. A lever, pulley, or hydraulic system lets you apply a small force to produce a much larger one -- at the cost of moving the input further than the output moves. In aircraft, hydraulic systems use mechanical advantage to operate heavy components like landing gear and flaps with manageable pilot effort.
exam_tip: Mechanical advantage = output force / input force. You trade distance of movement for increased force.
key_facts: Ratio of output force to input force
Greater force comes at the cost of greater input distance
Used in aircraft hydraulic systems to multiply pilot input force

syllabus_desc: Describe: (b) how mechanical advantage can be gained hydraulically
concept: Hydraulic systems gain mechanical advantage by using pistons of different sizes. A small piston connected to a large piston through a fluid-filled system allows a small force on the small piston to create a much larger force on the large piston. The ratio of forces equals the ratio of piston areas. Pascal's law ensures pressure is transmitted equally throughout the fluid.
exam_tip: Hydraulic advantage: force ratio equals the piston area ratio. Small piston to large piston multiplies force.
key_facts: Pascal's law: pressure is transmitted equally throughout a confined fluid
Force multiplication = ratio of large piston area to small piston area
The small piston must travel further to move the large piston a short distance

syllabus_desc: Describe: (c) the principle of operation of typical aircraft hydraulic services
concept: Aircraft hydraulic services use an engine-driven or electric pump to pressurise hydraulic fluid (typically MIL-H-5606 or Skydrol). This pressurised fluid is directed through selector valves to actuators that extend or retract landing gear, operate flaps, and apply brakes. The system provides smooth, powerful operation of heavy components with precise control.
exam_tip: Hydraulic systems use pressurised fluid to operate heavy components -- gear, flaps, and brakes are common applications.
key_facts: Pressurised hydraulic fluid operates actuators for gear, flaps, brakes
Provides smooth, powerful operation of heavy components
Hydraulic fluid is incompressible, giving precise control

syllabus_desc: Explain the advantages of using hydraulics to operate aircraft services.
concept: Mechanical advantage means using a small force to move something heavy. Hydraulically, you achieve it with different-sized pistons. Say you apply force to a small input piston and it connects to a large output piston—the pressure's the same everywhere (Pascal), but force equals pressure times area. Small area input, large area output, and you've multiplied your force. Practical example: your landing gear extension might need thousands of pounds of force, but you get it from a small hydraulic pump that you can power with your engine. The pump doesn't need to be huge; it just needs to overcome pressure, which is modest. The area difference in cylinders does the heavy lifting.
exam_tip: Mechanical advantage = small force creates large force. Hydraulically: small input piston, large output piston. Know the formula: Force = Pressure × Area.
key_facts: Pressure same everywhere (Pascal) Force = Pressure × Area Small input piston, large output = force multiplication Hydraulic pump doesn't need high force, just modest pressure Different piston areas create advantage

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between the three types of hydraulic oil.
concept: Your aircraft's hydraulic system starts with a pump driven by engine power, usually via a gearbox or belt. The pump forces hydraulic fluid through filter and pressure relief valve (prevents excessive pressure) into a distribution manifold. From there, fluid goes to various actuators—landing gear cylinders, flap jacks, trim units, brake systems. Each actuator is a cylinder with a piston; the fluid pressure moves the piston, doing work. Used fluid returns to a reservoir for reuse. Redundancy's built in: two independent systems, separate pumps, separate fluid supplies. If one system fails, the other keeps you flying. The entire system relies on incompressible fluid and sealed lines; any leak loses system pressure and function.
exam_tip: Pump → filter → relief valve → actuators → return reservoir. Redundancy is critical—know why. Be ready to explain what happens if you lose a hydraulic system.
key_facts: Engine-driven pump provides pressure Fluid flows to multiple actuators simultaneously Pressure relief prevents overpressure Dual systems for redundancy Return to reservoir for reuse Any leak = pressure loss = system failure

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of common hydraulic system components including: (a) reservoirs
concept: The reservoir stores the hydraulic fluid supply and allows for thermal expansion. It's usually located at the highest point in the system so gravity assists fluid return to the pump. The reservoir includes a sight glass for level checking and a breather or pressurisation system.
exam_tip: The reservoir stores fluid and is usually the highest point in the system -- check level via sight glass during preflight.
key_facts: Stores the system's hydraulic fluid supply
Usually at the highest point for gravity-assisted return
Includes sight glass, filler cap, and breather

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of common hydraulic system components including: (b) pumps
concept: Hydraulic pumps create the fluid pressure that drives the system. Engine-driven pumps provide continuous pressure during flight. Electric pumps may serve as backup or for ground operations. Hand pumps can be used for emergency gear extension. The pump draws fluid from the reservoir and delivers it under high pressure to the system.
exam_tip: Engine-driven pump = primary. Electric pump = backup/ground use. Hand pump = emergency gear extension.
key_facts: Engine-driven pumps provide primary hydraulic pressure
Electric pumps serve as backup or for ground operations
Hand pumps available for emergency operations like gear extension

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of common hydraulic system components including: (c) pressure regulators
concept: Pressure regulators maintain system pressure within a set operating range. When pressure reaches the upper limit, the regulator unloads the pump (allows it to circulate fluid without building pressure). When pressure drops below the lower limit, the pump is loaded again to restore pressure. This prevents the pump from working continuously against maximum pressure.
exam_tip: Pressure regulators cycle the pump between loaded (building pressure) and unloaded (circulating freely).
key_facts: Maintains pressure within set upper and lower limits
Unloads the pump at maximum pressure to reduce wear
Reloads the pump when pressure drops below minimum

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of common hydraulic system components including: (d) accumulators
concept: Accumulators store pressurised hydraulic fluid to provide an immediate supply for sudden demands. They contain a gas charge (usually nitrogen) separated from the fluid by a piston or bladder. The gas compresses to store energy and expands to deliver fluid quickly. Accumulators also smooth out pressure pulsations and provide emergency power for limited operations.
exam_tip: Accumulators store pressurised fluid using compressed nitrogen -- they provide instant power for sudden demands and emergencies.
key_facts: Store pressurised fluid using compressed nitrogen gas
Provide instant fluid for sudden demands without waiting for pump response
Smooth pressure pulsations and provide limited emergency operation

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of common hydraulic system components including: (e) check valves and release valves
concept: Check valves allow fluid to flow in one direction only -- like a one-way door. They prevent backflow that could cause components to drift or retract unexpectedly. Release valves (relief valves) protect the system from overpressure by opening at a set pressure limit and directing excess fluid back to the reservoir.
exam_tip: Check valves = one-way flow (prevent backflow). Relief valves = overpressure protection (dump excess to reservoir).
key_facts: Check valves: one-way flow, prevent backflow
Relief valves: open at set pressure to prevent system overpressure
Both are essential safety components in hydraulic systems

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of common hydraulic system components including: (f) selector valves
concept: Selector valves direct pressurised fluid to the correct actuator and control the direction of actuator movement. When the pilot selects 'gear down', the selector valve routes pressure to the extend side of the gear actuators and opens the retract side to return. Selector valves can be manually or electrically operated.
exam_tip: Selector valves direct fluid to the correct side of the actuator -- they determine which way things move.
key_facts: Direct fluid flow to the correct actuator port
Control direction of actuator movement (extend or retract)
Operated manually by lever or electrically by switch

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of common hydraulic system components including: (g) actuators
concept: Actuators convert hydraulic pressure into mechanical movement. Linear actuators (rams) produce a push-pull motion for extending and retracting landing gear or operating flaps. Rotary actuators produce turning motion. The actuator's output force depends on the piston area and system pressure.
exam_tip: Actuators convert fluid pressure into mechanical movement -- force = pressure x piston area.
key_facts: Convert hydraulic pressure into mechanical force and movement
Linear actuators for push-pull (gear, flaps); rotary for turning motion
Output force = system pressure x piston area

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of common hydraulic system components including: (h) filters
concept: Hydraulic filters remove contaminants (metal particles, dirt, seal fragments) from the fluid to protect precision components like valves and actuators. Even tiny particles can damage closely-fitted components. Filters are placed at critical points in the system and must be inspected and replaced at maintenance intervals.
exam_tip: Hydraulic filters protect precision components -- even tiny contaminants can damage closely-fitted valve surfaces.
key_facts: Remove contaminants to protect precision hydraulic components
Located at critical points throughout the system
Must be inspected and replaced at regular maintenance intervals

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of the following hydraulic systems: (a) open centre system
concept: In an open centre system, hydraulic fluid circulates continuously from the pump through the selector valves and back to the reservoir when no service is selected. The pump runs at low pressure most of the time. When a service is selected, the valve blocks the return path and directs fluid to the actuator under pressure. Simpler and cheaper than pressurised systems.
exam_tip: Open centre: fluid circulates freely at low pressure when idle. Pressure builds only when a service is selected.
key_facts: Fluid circulates continuously through open selector valves at low pressure
Pressure builds only when a service is selected
Simpler, lighter, and cheaper than pressurised systems

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of the following hydraulic systems: (b) pressurised system
concept: In a pressurised (closed centre) system, the pump maintains system pressure at all times using a pressure regulator and accumulator. When a service is selected, pressurised fluid is immediately available -- no delay while pressure builds. This gives faster response and allows multiple services to operate simultaneously. More complex but essential for heavier aircraft.
exam_tip: Pressurised system: fluid always at pressure via accumulator. Faster response, multiple simultaneous operations.
key_facts: Maintains constant system pressure using regulator and accumulator
Immediate response when a service is selected
Allows multiple services to operate simultaneously

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of a typical aircraft hydraulic system.
concept: A typical modern aircraft hydraulic system works like this: at engine start, the engine-driven pump begins pressurising hydraulic fluid from the reservoir. The pump pushes fluid through a pressure regulator (relief valve) set to, say, 2850 psi. If the system is idle, the regulator opens and excess flow returns to the reservoir, keeping pressure steady. If pressure drops below the set point, the regulator closes and the pump pressure rises to meet demand. Accumulators pre-charged with nitrogen to about 80% of system operating pressure sit in the lines, ready to supply power. When you command a function—say, gear down—the selector valve routes pressurised fluid into the landing gear actuator cylinder, pushing it. The gear descends, and the actuator displacement generates back-pressure, which the selector valve sees and routes return flow back to the reservoir through the filter. Throughout the system, check valves ensure fluid flows the right direction, and shutoff valves provide isolation for maintenance and safety. The fluid circulates through the filter to remove contaminants, then back to the reservoir. The whole cycle is continuous as long as you're demanding actuator movement. Once you release the selector, the actuator holds its position (locked by check valves), and the pump returns to low flow circulating mode.
exam_tip: Engine start: pump pressurises → regulator holds set pressure → selectors route flow on demand → actuators move → return flows through filter → reservoir. Accumulators provide reserve. Check valves hold actuator position.
key_facts: Engine-driven pump pressurises fluid from reservoir
Pressure regulator maintains system pressure at set point
Accumulators store pressurised fluid for backup and smoothing
Selector valve routes flow to desired actuator
Actuator extends/retracts under hydraulic pressure
Return flow passes through filter to remove contaminants
Check valves prevent backflow and hold actuator position
Shutoff valves provide isolation for safety and maintenance

syllabus_desc: Explain the relative: (a) advantages of pneumatic systems over hydraulic systems
concept: Pneumatic systems are lighter than hydraulic systems because air weighs less than hydraulic fluid and the plumbing can be simpler. Leaks are less messy and less of a fire risk (air vs flammable fluid). The air supply is readily available from the engine-driven vacuum/pressure pump or bleed air. Return lines aren't needed -- used air is simply vented overboard.
exam_tip: Pneumatic advantages: lighter, no return lines needed, leaks are clean (just air), and no fire risk from fluid.
key_facts: Lighter than hydraulic systems (air vs heavy fluid)
No return lines needed -- used air vents overboard
Leaks are clean and not a fire hazard

syllabus_desc: Explain the relative: (b) disadvantages of pneumatic systems over hydraulic systems
concept: Pneumatic systems operate at much lower pressures than hydraulic systems, so they can't generate the same forces -- unsuitable for heavy loads like landing gear retraction. Air is compressible (unlike hydraulic fluid), giving spongy, less precise control. Response is slower, and maintaining consistent pressure is harder because air volume changes with temperature.
exam_tip: Pneumatic disadvantages: lower force capability, spongy feel (air compresses), and less precise control than hydraulics.
key_facts: Much lower operating pressure than hydraulic systems
Air is compressible -- gives spongy, imprecise control
Not suitable for high-force applications like gear retraction

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of a typical pneumatic system.
concept: A typical pneumatic system starts with a compressor that's usually engine-driven or electrically powered. This compressor draws air from the atmosphere, compresses it to high pressure (typically 1000-3000 psi depending on system design), and feeds it into a reservoir or accumulator. The reservoir stores the compressed air and acts as a buffer, smoothing pressure fluctuations. It also provides time for water vapour in the compressed air to condense and settle at the bottom. A moisture trap—a small drain at the lowest point of the reservoir—regularly purges condensed water. Failure to drain this trap regularly can lead to water accumulation, which corrodes internal components and reduces system reliability. From the reservoir, the compressed air passes through a regulator. The regulator maintains the working pressure at a steady, safe level regardless of whether the compressor is running or the air demand fluctuates. Too high pressure would damage actuators; too low pressure wouldn't provide enough force. From the regulator, air is distributed through a network of small-diameter tubing to individual pneumatic actuators—cylinders, motors, or switches. Control valves direct air to specific actuators on demand. An actuator might be a simple on-off solenoid valve (press a button, air flows) or a proportional valve (move a lever, air flow increases proportionally). When the actuator's job is done (landing gear extended, door closed), the control valve exhausts air from the actuator back to atmosphere, and the piston or motor returns to neutral. It's elegantly simple: compress air, store it, regulate it, distribute it, use it, and exhaust it.
exam_tip: Compressor → reservoir (stores air, allows water condensation) → regulator (maintains pressure) → control valves → actuators. Moisture trap drains water.
key_facts: Compressor draws air, compresses to high pressure (1000-3000 psi)
Reservoir stores compressed air and allows water condensation
Moisture trap at reservoir bottom drains condensed water
Regulator maintains steady working pressure
Control valves direct air to specific actuators
Actuators perform work (cylinders, motors, solenoid switches)
Exhausts to atmosphere when actuator work is complete

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of a typical fire detection system.
concept: Fire detection systems use sensors (usually thermocouples or continuous-loop detectors) strategically placed in engine bays, cargo holds, or other high-risk areas. The most common in piston aircraft are continuous-loop systems: a Inconel or stainless-steel tube runs through the engine bay, and inside the tube is a eutectic wire (a specially formulated alloy) that melts at a fixed temperature—say, 180°C. When that temperature is reached, the wire melts, breaking the electrical circuit and triggering the fire warning on the flight deck. A thermocouple system uses multiple small thermocouples wired in series; if any thermocouple sees a rapid temperature rise (the rate-of-rise detector) or an absolute temperature above a threshold, it triggers the warning. Modern systems use both: absolute temperature (190°C) and rate-of-rise (15°C per second) so you catch a fire quickly. The warning activates a light (usually red, with 'FIRE' label) and possibly a bell or buzzer. It's not foolproof—false alarms do happen from electrical faults—but it's your first and often best warning that you've got a fire. Once the warning sounds, you isolate the affected engine and execute the fire-out procedure (cutting fuel, electrical power, closing firewall shutoff valves, deploying fire bottles if equipped).
exam_tip: Fire sensors: thermocouples or continuous-loop detectors in engine bay. Trigger on absolute temperature or rapid rate-of-rise. Warning light on flight deck. Know where your fire warning sensors are and what temperature triggers them.
key_facts: Continuous-loop systems: eutectic wire melts at set temperature Thermocouple systems: detect absolute temperature or rate-of-rise Sensors placed in engine bay, cargo hold, or other high-risk areas Temperature threshold typically 180–200°C Rate-of-rise detector: 10–15°C per second triggers warning Warning activates red light and possibly audible alarm False alarms can occur from electrical faults Pilot action on warning: isolate engine, cut fuel, execute fire-out procedure

syllabus_desc: Explain any precautions to be taken when using the common fire extinguishing agents available.
concept: Common aircraft fire extinguishing agents are CO2 (carbon dioxide), Halon 1211, Halon 1301, and dry powder (mostly on ground equipment). CO2 is effective but heavier than air, so it can stratify and leave pockets of oxygen-rich air. It's also cold, which can shock a hot engine. Halon 1211 and 1301 are halogenated hydrocarbons that interrupt the chemical reaction of combustion—they're extremely effective and leave no residue. Halon is the preferred agent on modern aircraft because of its efficiency. However, Halon production is being phased out worldwide due to ozone depletion concerns, so supplies are dwindling and very expensive. Dry powder is used in ground-based systems (hangars, maintenance areas) because it's messy but cheap and effective. Precautions when using any extinguishing agent: ensure good ventilation because CO2 displaces oxygen (asphyxiation risk in confined spaces), wear protective equipment (gloves, eye protection), and follow manufacturer procedures exactly. Don't mix agents. If you deploy a fire bottle in flight, expect reduced visibility (Halon dissipates, but can cloud the cabin briefly), and be aware that some agents cool the engine significantly and can affect handling. After deploying any agent, plan an immediate landing—the fire might be suppressed but you want inspection and maintenance before flying again.
exam_tip: CO2: effective but cold, heavy, asphyxiation risk. Halon: effective, leaves no residue, expensive, phasing out. Dry powder: messy, ground-based. Precautions: ventilate, PPE, follow procedures, plan landing after deployment.
key_facts: CO2: effective, but heavy and cold, asphyxiation risk in confined spaces Halon 1211/1301: very effective, no residue, preferred for aircraft Dry powder: messy, ground-based, used in hangars and shops Never mix fire extinguishing agents Ventilation essential when using CO2 Personal protective equipment required (gloves, eye protection) Expect visibility degradation with airborne agents Plan immediate landing after in-flight agent deployment

syllabus_desc: Describe typical fire extinguishing systems.
concept: A typical in-flight fire extinguishing system on a light twin consists of one or more fire bottles (pressurised cylinders of Halon or CO2) mounted in an accessible but protected location, usually the fuselage or an engine bay bulkhead. The bottle is connected via a distribution line (usually a pipe or rigid tube) to the suspected fire location. A pushbutton on the flight deck activates the system: you press the button, a solenoid valve opens, and pressurised extinguishing agent flows down the pipe to disperse in the engine compartment. Some systems have multiple bottles with individual switches (one for each engine) so you can suppress fires on either engine independently. Manual override systems exist on some aircraft: if the electrical system fails, you can pull a mechanical lever or cable to open the valve manually. Ground-based systems in hangars are similar: a pull handle or push button releases the agent over a wide area. All fire extinguishing systems have inspection and maintenance requirements: hydrostatic testing of the bottle (every few years), checking the pressure gauge to ensure the bottle is charged, and verifying all valves and lines are clear. A discharged fire bottle is useless, so this is a critical pre-flight item. Once a bottle is deployed, it must be professionally refilled before the aircraft flies again.
exam_tip: Fire bottle: pressurised, solenoid-activated, distribution line to engine bay, pushbutton activation. Know where your bottle is, what agent it contains, and its pressure status. Discharged bottle = no-go.
key_facts: Fire bottle: pressurised cylinder of Halon or CO2, mounted in fuselage Solenoid valve: electrically activated, releases agent on pushbutton Distribution line: pipe routing agent to fire location Multi-engine aircraft: separate bottle or selector for each engine Manual override: mechanical lever or cable backup if electrical fails Inspection: hydrostatic testing every 5 years, pressure check before flight Discharged bottle: unserviceable, must be refilled by certified facility Post-deployment: plan immediate landing, ground aircraft for inspection

syllabus_desc: Explain the associated limitations of typical fire extinguishing systems.
concept: Fire extinguishing systems are good, but they have real limitations. First, they only suppress the fire where the agent is directed; if the fire's in an area the distribution line doesn't reach, the agent won't help. Second, they work best on fuel fires or electrical fires; structural fires (where the airframe itself is burning) are harder to suppress and may reignite after the agent dissipates. Third, the agent cools the engine rapidly, and that thermal shock can damage components or cause subsequent failures. Fourth, once the agent is deployed, it's gone—you get one shot. If it doesn't work, you've got no backup. Some aircraft have multiple bottles to address this, but it costs weight and space. Fifth, the agent can obscure vision (CO2 fog, Halon vapour) making it harder to see instruments or navigate. Sixth, deployment doesn't address the root cause—if you've got an electrical short that's causing the fire, the agent suppresses it, but the short's still there waiting to reignite. That's why immediate landing and professional investigation are mandatory after any fire suppression. Finally, false alarms do happen from faulty detectors or electrical faults, and deploying the bottle unnecessarily uses up your one-shot protection.
exam_tip: Fire suppression limitations: limited coverage area, doesn't address root cause, one-shot only, thermal shock risk, vision degradation, possible reignition. Always land immediately after any fire warning, even if suppression deployed.
key_facts: Agent reaches only the area covered by distribution lines Structural fires difficult to suppress and prone to reignition Rapid cooling (thermal shock) risks engine damage Single-shot deployment; no backup if first attempt fails Halon/CO2 fog reduces visibility Suppression treats symptom, not root cause Electrical faults that cause fire still exist after suppression False alarms from detector faults waste agent protection Immediate landing and professional inspection mandatory after fire warning

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between anti-icing systems and de-icing systems.
concept: Anti-icing and de-icing are two different strategies for dealing with ice, and you need to understand the distinction. Anti-icing systems prevent ice from forming in the first place. They're applied before ice accumulates. A heated pitot tube is a classic anti-ice system: before you enter icing conditions, you switch on the pitot heat, which electrically heats the pitot probe. Ice can't form on a hot surface. TKS fluid is another anti-ice system: it's a glycol-based fluid that weeps out of tiny pores in the airframe (wing leading edges, propeller blades), creating a protective film. This film lowers the freezing point of water, so supercooled water runs off the surface instead of freezing. Anti-ice systems are always preventative. De-icing systems remove ice after it has formed on the airframe. Pneumatic rubber boots are the classic de-icing system: as ice forms on the leading edges of wings or rotor blades, you inflate the boots, which crack the ice and release it. Once the ice is gone, you deflate the boots, and they mould back to the aerodynamic shape. Hot air systems—where bleed air from the engine is ducted to leading edges—are another de-icing approach: hot air melts the ice after it forms. Electrical heating elements on rotor blade leading edges work the same way. De-icing systems are reactive—you let ice form, then remove it. The trade-off between anti-ice and de-ice is cost, weight, and mission profile. Anti-ice is continuous power consumption but prevents ice altogether. De-ice is lighter and cheaper but requires monitoring for ice and active removal. Most light helicopters in NZ use some combination, especially pneumatic boots for the rotor blades.
exam_tip: Anti-ice PREVENTS ice (heated pitot, TKS fluid). De-ice REMOVES ice (rubber boots, hot air). Anti-ice = continuous; de-ice = reactive.
key_facts: Anti-icing prevents ice formation (applied before ice accumulates)
Heated pitot tube: electrical heating prevents pitot icing
TKS fluid: glycol weeping system lowers freezing point
De-icing removes ice after formation
Pneumatic rubber boots inflate to crack and shed ice
Hot air from engine bleed melts ice on leading edges
De-ice is reactive, requiring observation and activation

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation and the proper handling of the following ice protection systems: (a) mechanical
concept: Mechanical de-icing uses inflatable rubber boots on wing and tail leading edges. When ice accumulates, the boots are inflated with air pressure, cracking the ice off the surface. They're then deflated so ice doesn't form around the inflated shape. Wait for a sufficient thickness of ice to build before activating -- activating too early creates an ice bridge over the boots.
exam_tip: De-icing boots: wait for ice to build before inflating -- activating too early lets ice bridge over the boots.
key_facts: Inflatable rubber boots on leading edges crack ice off when inflated
Must wait for sufficient ice build-up before activating
Premature activation creates ice bridges that boots can't remove

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation and the proper handling of the following ice protection systems: (b) fluid
concept: Fluid de-icing and anti-icing systems distribute a freezing point depressant (like TKS fluid) through porous panels on the leading edges. The fluid flows over the surface, either preventing ice from forming (anti-ice mode) or loosening existing ice (de-ice mode). The system uses a finite fluid supply that must be monitored.
exam_tip: Fluid systems (TKS) use a finite supply of de-icing fluid -- monitor the quantity and don't waste it.
key_facts: Porous panels distribute freezing point depressant fluid over surfaces
Can operate as anti-ice (prevent) or de-ice (remove)
Finite fluid supply -- must monitor quantity during icing encounters

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation and the proper handling of the following ice protection systems: (c) thermal
concept: Thermal anti-icing uses hot air (usually engine bleed air) or electrical heating elements to keep surfaces warm enough to prevent ice from forming. It's most effective as anti-icing (prevention) rather than de-icing (removal). Pitot heat, windscreen heat, and engine inlet anti-ice are common thermal systems in light aircraft.
exam_tip: Thermal systems prevent ice by keeping surfaces warm -- pitot heat and windscreen heat are the most common in light aircraft.
key_facts: Uses hot air or electrical heating to prevent ice formation
Most effective as anti-icing (prevention) rather than de-icing (removal)
Common examples: pitot heat, windscreen heat, carburettor heat

syllabus_desc: Describe typical systems for: (a) ice detection
concept: Ice detection systems alert the crew to icing conditions. Visual detection includes ice evidence probes (small rods visible from the cockpit) and ice lights for night inspection of leading edges. Electronic systems use vibrating probes whose frequency changes when ice accumulates, or optical sensors that detect ice build-up.
exam_tip: Ice detection: visual probes, ice lights (night), and electronic vibrating probes that detect ice accumulation.
key_facts: Visual: ice evidence probes and ice lights for night inspection
Electronic: vibrating probes detect ice by frequency change
Early detection is critical -- ice accumulates faster than most pilots expect

syllabus_desc: Describe typical systems for: (b) windscreen heating
concept: Windscreen heating prevents ice formation and maintains visibility. Most systems use embedded electrical heating wires or a conductive film within the windscreen layers, powered by the electrical system. Some aircraft use hot air ducted across the inside of the windscreen. Heated windscreens should be turned on before entering icing conditions.
exam_tip: Windscreen heat uses embedded electrical elements or hot air -- activate before entering icing conditions.
key_facts: Embedded electrical heating wires or conductive film
Some systems use ducted hot air
Activate before entering icing conditions to prevent obscured visibility

syllabus_desc: Describe typical systems for: (c) rain clearance
concept: Rain clearance systems maintain forward visibility in rain. Windscreen wipers are the simplest system. Some aircraft use rain repellent fluid sprayed on the windscreen to cause water to bead and blow off. High-speed aircraft may use hot air bleed across the windscreen. Maintaining clear visibility is essential for safe visual approaches.
exam_tip: Rain clearance: wipers, rain repellent fluid, or hot air bleed -- maintaining forward visibility is the objective.
key_facts: Windscreen wipers: simplest and most common system
Rain repellent fluid causes water to bead and blow off
Hot air systems used on some higher-performance aircraft

syllabus_desc: Describe the performance reductions associated with: (a) ice accumulation
concept: Ice accumulation degrades aircraft performance dramatically. On wings and tail, it disrupts airflow, reduces lift, increases drag, and increases stall speed. On the propeller, it reduces thrust and causes vibration from uneven ice shedding. Ice on the pitot tube and static ports gives false instrument readings. Even a thin layer of ice on the wings can reduce climb performance by 20% or more.
exam_tip: Even light ice significantly increases stall speed and drag -- climb performance can drop 20%+ from thin wing ice.
key_facts: Wing ice: reduced lift, increased drag, higher stall speed
Propeller ice: reduced thrust, vibration from asymmetric shedding
Pitot/static ice: false airspeed, altitude, and VSI readings

syllabus_desc: Describe the performance reductions associated with: (b) operation of anti-ice and de-icing equipment
concept: Operating anti-ice and de-icing equipment also costs performance. Engine bleed air for thermal anti-icing reduces available engine power. Electrical heating systems increase electrical load (watch for alternator limits). De-icing boots create slight drag when inflated. Running these systems while also carrying ice penalties means performance is reduced from both the ice and the countermeasures.
exam_tip: Anti-ice systems reduce performance too -- bleed air reduces power, electrical heat loads the alternator.
key_facts: Engine bleed air anti-ice reduces available engine power
Electrical heating increases alternator load
Combined ice + system penalties can significantly reduce performance margins

syllabus_desc: Explain the function and operation of: (a) static discharge wicks
concept: Static discharge wicks (static dischargers) are small conductive rods mounted on trailing edges of wings, ailerons, elevators, and rudder. In flight, the aircraft builds up static charge from friction with air and precipitation. The wicks provide sharp points where this charge can dissipate quietly into the atmosphere, preventing noisy radio interference (precipitation static).
exam_tip: Static wicks discharge built-up static electricity to prevent radio interference -- check they're intact during preflight.
key_facts: Mounted on trailing edges of flight control surfaces
Dissipate static charge quietly to prevent radio interference
Must be intact -- missing wicks cause radio static (precipitation static)

syllabus_desc: Explain the function and operation of: (b) bonding strips
concept: Bonding strips are metal connections between aircraft components that ensure the entire airframe is at the same electrical potential. Without bonding, different parts could accumulate different static charges, causing sparks (hazardous near fuel) and radio interference. Bonding strips connect control surfaces, engine mounts, and other moving or isolated components to the airframe.
exam_tip: Bonding strips prevent static potential differences between components -- critical near fuel systems to prevent sparks.
key_facts: Metal connections ensuring equal electrical potential across the airframe
Prevent static sparks between components (fire hazard near fuel)
Connect control surfaces, engine mounts, and isolated parts to airframe

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: (a) tachometers (centrifugal, drag cup, electrical and electronic types)
concept: Tachometers measure engine RPM. Centrifugal types use spinning weights (like a governor). Drag cup (magnetic) types use a rotating magnet that drags a metal cup, deflecting a needle proportional to RPM. Electrical types send a signal from an engine-driven generator to a cockpit indicator. The tacho is your primary power reference on a fixed-pitch prop aircraft.
exam_tip: Drag cup tachometers are most common in light aircraft -- a rotating magnet drags a metal cup proportional to RPM.
key_facts: Measures engine RPM -- primary power gauge for fixed-pitch prop aircraft
Drag cup type: magnetic coupling between rotating magnet and indicator cup
Electrical type: engine-driven generator sends signal to cockpit gauge

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: (b) manifold pressure and boost gauges
concept: Manifold pressure (MAP) and boost gauges measure the absolute pressure in the inlet manifold, indicating how much air (and therefore fuel-air mixture) is being fed to the cylinders. MAP is measured in inches of mercury (inHg) or millibars. This is your primary power reference on a constant-speed prop aircraft. With the engine off, MAP reads ambient atmospheric pressure.
exam_tip: MAP gauge reads atmospheric pressure with the engine off. In flight, it's the primary power gauge for CS prop aircraft.
key_facts: Measures inlet manifold absolute pressure in inHg
Primary power indicator for constant-speed prop aircraft
Reads atmospheric pressure with engine off; decreases as throttle is closed

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: (c) oil pressure gauges (direct reading, remote indicating and ratiometer types)
concept: Oil pressure gauges measure the pressure of oil being delivered to the engine's lubrication system. Direct-reading types use a Bourdon tube connected directly to the oil line. Remote-indicating types use an electrical sender at the engine that transmits to a cockpit gauge. Oil pressure is the single most critical engine health indicator -- low pressure demands immediate action.
exam_tip: Oil pressure is the most critical engine gauge -- low or zero pressure means land as soon as practicable.
key_facts: Direct-reading (Bourdon tube) or remote-indicating (electrical sender)
Most critical engine health indicator
Low oil pressure = potential bearing failure = land as soon as practicable

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: (d) fuel pressure gauges
concept: Fuel pressure gauges measure the pressure of fuel being delivered to the carburettor or injection system. Normal fuel pressure confirms the engine-driven fuel pump is working. A drop in fuel pressure may indicate pump failure, a blockage, or running a tank dry. Fuel-injected engines rely more heavily on fuel pressure than carburetted engines.
exam_tip: A fuel pressure drop may indicate pump failure or empty tank -- switch on the boost pump and check tank selection.
key_facts: Measures fuel delivery pressure to the engine
Normal reading confirms engine-driven fuel pump is operating
Fuel pressure drop: switch on boost pump and check tank selector

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: (e) vacuum gauges
concept: Vacuum (suction) gauges measure the vacuum produced by the engine-driven vacuum pump, which powers gyroscopic instruments (typically the attitude indicator and heading indicator). Normal vacuum is about 4.5-5.5 inHg. Low vacuum means the gyro instruments are unreliable. Check the vacuum gauge to confirm gyro instruments can be trusted.
exam_tip: Normal vacuum: about 4.5-5.5 inHg. Low vacuum = unreliable gyro instruments (attitude and heading indicators).
key_facts: Measures vacuum from engine-driven pump (typically 4.5-5.5 inHg)
Powers air-driven gyro instruments (AI and HI)
Low vacuum reading = gyro instruments are unreliable

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: (f) cylinder head and exhaust gas temperature gauges (thermocouples)
concept: CHT gauges monitor cylinder head temperature using a thermocouple embedded in the hottest cylinder head. EGT gauges measure exhaust gas temperature at the exhaust port using a thermocouple probe. CHT warns of overheating; EGT is used for precise mixture leaning -- peak EGT indicates the stoichiometric mixture. Both use thermocouple principles (two dissimilar metals generating a voltage proportional to temperature).
exam_tip: CHT monitors overheating risk. EGT is the precision tool for mixture leaning -- peak EGT = stoichiometric ratio.
key_facts: Both use thermocouples (voltage generated by temperature difference)
CHT: monitors hottest cylinder to prevent overheating
EGT: used for precise mixture leaning (peak EGT = stoichiometric)

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: (g) oil temperature gauges
concept: Oil temperature gauges measure the temperature of the engine oil, usually at the oil cooler outlet. They use either a bimetallic resistance element or a thermocouple. Oil temperature indicates how well the cooling system is working and helps detect problems. High oil temp combined with low oil pressure is a critical warning -- land as soon as practicable.
exam_tip: High oil temp + low oil pressure = critical combination. Oil temp also helps assess engine cooling effectiveness.
key_facts: Measures oil temperature, usually at oil cooler outlet
Uses resistance-type or thermocouple sensors
High temp + low pressure = critical warning, land as soon as practicable

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: (h) outside air temperature gauges
concept: Outside air temperature (OAT) gauges display the ambient air temperature, essential for performance calculations, carburettor icing assessment, and determining whether conditions support airframe icing. Most are simple bimetallic sensors mounted externally. At cruise speeds, ram rise (heating from air compression) makes the reading slightly higher than true OAT.
exam_tip: OAT is needed for density altitude, carb ice assessment, and icing conditions. At speed, ram rise inflates the reading slightly.
key_facts: Displays ambient temperature for performance and icing assessment
Bimetallic sensor mounted on the aircraft exterior
At cruise speeds, ram rise makes the reading slightly higher than true static air temperature

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: (i) fuel quantity gauges
concept: Fuel quantity gauges show the amount of fuel in each tank. Float-type gauges use a float arm connected to a variable resistor. Capacitance-type gauges measure the dielectric properties of the fuel between two plates. Aircraft fuel gauges are only required to read accurately at 'empty' -- always cross-check with a dipstick and fuel burn calculations.
exam_tip: Fuel gauges are only required to be accurate at 'empty' -- never rely solely on them for fuel planning.
key_facts: Float-type or capacitance-type sensors
Only required to be accurate at the empty indication
Always cross-check with dipstick measurement and fuel burn calculation

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: (j) fuel flow gauges
concept: Fuel flow gauges measure the rate of fuel consumption, typically in gallons or litres per hour. They help the pilot optimise mixture settings, verify engine performance, and manage fuel reserves. Some totalising systems also calculate fuel used and fuel remaining. An abnormally high fuel flow rate may indicate a fuel leak or stuck-rich mixture.
exam_tip: Fuel flow gauges help optimise mixture and manage fuel -- abnormal flow rates flag potential engine or fuel system problems.
key_facts: Measures fuel consumption rate in GPH or LPH
Used to optimise mixture and verify engine performance
Totalising types calculate fuel used and remaining

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of a simple AC synchro system.
concept: An AC synchro system (also called an autosyn system or sometimes a desynn system) is an elegant mechanical solution for transmitting measurement information—like fuel quantity or oil pressure—from a sensor in one location (the engine) to a display gauge in another location (the instrument panel). The system uses AC electrical signals, not DC. It has two main components: a transmitter and a receiver. The transmitter sits in the measured location—for instance, a fuel tank—and mechanically couples to a shaft that spins proportionally to the measurement. A float valve in a fuel tank mechanically connects to the transmitter shaft, so as fuel level changes, the float rises and falls, rotating the transmitter shaft. Inside the transmitter, a rotatable coil (called the rotor) sits at an angle that matches the float's position. The rotor is energized with AC current. This AC current in the rotor creates a magnetic field, and this field couples to three stationary coils (called stators) inside the transmitter. The strength of coupling between the rotor and each stator depends on the rotor's angular position. This varying coupling induces AC voltages in the stators. The three stators are wired—using three thin wires—to three corresponding stators in a receiver, which sits at the instrument panel. The receiver's three stator coils produce magnetic fields proportional to the signals they receive. Inside the receiver is another rotor coil, which experiences these magnetic fields. The rotor automatically positions itself to match the transmitter rotor's position. The receiver rotor mechanically couples to the display needle, so the needle moves to indicate the fuel quantity or oil pressure value. It's entirely passive—no amplifiers, no servo motors, no feedback loops needed. The transmitter and receiver synch automatically. AC synchro systems are robust, reliable, tolerant of electrical interference, and have been used in aircraft for decades. They're still common in many helicopters.
exam_tip: AC synchro: transmitter (rotor position) and receiver (coupled display) connected electrically. Rotor position in transmitter determines receiver rotor position.
key_facts: AC synchro transmitter uses rotor coupled to measured value (fuel, pressure, etc.)
Rotor's AC magnetic field couples inductively to three stator coils
Three stators in transmitter send AC signals via three wires to receiver
Receiver's three stators recreate the magnetic field pattern
Receiver rotor automatically syncs to transmitter rotor position
Receiver rotor mechanically drives display needle
Passive system—no amplifiers or servo motors required
Common for fuel quantity, oil pressure remote indication in helicopters

syllabus_desc: Define: (a) static air pressure
concept: Static air pressure is the pressure of the undisturbed atmosphere at your altitude, unaffected by the aircraft's movement. It decreases with altitude as there's less air above you. Static pressure is sensed through static ports (flush openings on the fuselage) and is used by the altimeter, VSI, and the airspeed indicator.
exam_tip: Static pressure = ambient atmospheric pressure at your altitude. It feeds the altimeter, VSI, and one side of the ASI.
key_facts: Atmospheric pressure unaffected by aircraft movement
Decreases with altitude (approximately 1 hPa per 30 ft near sea level)
Sensed through static ports on the fuselage sides

syllabus_desc: Define: (b) dynamic air pressure
concept: Dynamic air pressure (also called 'q') is the additional pressure caused by the aircraft moving through the air. It's the kinetic energy of the airflow converted to pressure. Dynamic pressure increases with the square of the airspeed -- double the speed, four times the dynamic pressure. It's sensed by the pitot tube facing into the airstream.
exam_tip: Dynamic pressure = the pressure created by forward motion. It increases with the square of airspeed.
key_facts: Additional pressure from aircraft movement through the air
Proportional to the square of the airspeed (V-squared)
Sensed by the pitot tube facing into the relative airflow

syllabus_desc: Define: (c) total (pitot) pressure
concept: Total (pitot) pressure is the sum of static pressure and dynamic pressure, measured by the pitot tube. The pitot tube faces directly into the airstream and captures both the ambient (static) pressure and the additional (dynamic) pressure created by forward motion. The ASI works by subtracting static pressure from total pressure to get dynamic pressure, which indicates airspeed.
exam_tip: Total pressure = static + dynamic. The ASI subtracts static from pitot to isolate dynamic pressure = airspeed.
key_facts: Total pressure = static pressure + dynamic pressure
Measured by the pitot tube facing into the airstream
ASI uses the difference between pitot and static to indicate airspeed

syllabus_desc: Explain the pressure requirements of the three basic pressure instruments.
concept: Your airspeed indicator (ASI) needs two pressures: total pressure (from the pitot tube) and static pressure (from the static vent). The ASI's internal mechanism measures the difference (dynamic pressure) and converts it to airspeed. Your altimeter needs only static pressure: as static pressure decreases with altitude, the altimeter mechanism expands a bellows and the needle climbs. Your vertical speed indicator (VSI) needs static pressure but in a specific way: it has a sensitive capsule inside that connects to the static system and a reference chamber also fed by static, but with a delayed connection through a metering restriction. When static pressure changes, the capsule responds faster than the reference chamber, creating a pressure difference that moves the needle to show the rate of change (climb or descent). So in summary: ASI = total + static. Altimeter = static only. VSI = static with internal delay mechanism. If you lose static pressure (blocked static vent), all three instruments fail or become unreliable. If you lose pitot pressure (pitot tube iced over), only the ASI fails; altitude and vertical speed still work. That's why understanding what each instrument depends on is critical for troubleshooting.
exam_tip: ASI: pitot (total) + static. Altimeter: static only. VSI: static only (internal delay for rate sensing). Loss of static = all three fail. Loss of pitot = ASI only.
key_facts: ASI requires: pitot pressure (total) and static pressure
Altimeter requires: static pressure only
VSI requires: static pressure with metering delay
ASI failure if pitot ices or blocked
ALT/VSI failure if static vent ices or blocked
Loss of both pitot and static = complete pressure instrument failure
Alternate static system: if main static blocked, use cabin pressure or alternate vent

syllabus_desc: Describe a typical pitot-static system.
concept: A typical system has a pitot tube (a small intake facing directly into the airflow), a static vent (or vents) usually on the fuselage side where the airflow runs parallel and static pressure predominates), and connecting tubing from both to the flight deck instruments. The pitot tube is exposed on the nose or wing leading edge and has a small heater (pitot heat) to prevent ice accumulation. The static vent is usually a pair of openings (left and right sides of the fuselage) to measure average static pressure and cancel out lateral pressure variations. Lines from the pitot run to the ASI and any digital airspeed system. Lines from the static vent branch to the altimeter, VSI, and any other pressure-referenced systems. The lines should be clear tubing so you can see for blockages (dust, insects, water, ice). Moisture can condense inside the lines and freeze, blocking them. That's why some systems include a drain valve at the lowest point to bleed condensation. The system is checked during pre-flight: you look at the pitot tube (make sure it's uncovered and clean), you check that the alternate static valve (if equipped) is in the correct position, and you verify instruments read sensibly (altimeter matches field elevation, VSI reads zero on the ground, ASI reads zero).
exam_tip: Pitot tube: nose or wing, faces into airflow. Static vent: fuselage side, parallel airflow. Lines connect to instruments. Pitot heat prevents ice. Check pre-flight: pitot clear, static vent uncovered, instruments reading correctly.
key_facts: Pitot tube: intake facing airflow, measures total (pitot) pressure
Pitot tube location: nose or wing leading edge
Pitot heat: electric heater prevents ice accumulation
Static vent: fuselage side, measures static pressure
Dual static vents (left and right): average pressure, cancel lateral variations
Tubing: clear plastic, check for blockages or condensation
Drain valve: at lowest point, drains condensed moisture
Alternate static system: cabin air or manual vent if main static blocked

syllabus_desc: Explain the difference between a single pitot (pressure) head and separate pitot tube and static vent system.
concept: A single pitot head (also called a combined pitot-static head or probe) is a single intake that measures both pitot and static pressures from internal ports. It's compact and you only have one thing to worry about keeping clear. The internal design routes air to either the pitot chamber (centre, high pressure) or the static chamber (outer annulus, low pressure). It's elegant if it works. A separate pitot tube (forward-facing intake) and static vent (side-facing openings) system is simpler and more reliable. Each measures its specific pressure without trying to do double duty. If one gets blocked, the other still works. Separate systems are preferred on modern aircraft because they're transparent: you can see and clear the pitot easily, and the static vent is usually accessible. Combined heads were popular in older designs but they can confuse ice formation or water ingestion—a combined head that ices over loses both pressures at once. Most modern general aviation aircraft have separate systems. On a piston twin, you'll almost certainly have a separate pitot tube and static vent. The combined head shows up on older singles or experimental aircraft.
exam_tip: Single pitot head: compact, both pressures in one probe, more blockage risk if it ices or plugs. Separate: pitot tube and static vent, transparent, fail independently. Separate is more reliable.
key_facts: Single pitot head: one probe measures pitot and static through internal passages
Single head: compact but higher blockage risk from ice or water
Separate pitot tube: forward-facing, measures total (pitot) pressure only
Separate static vent: side-facing, measures static pressure only
Separate systems: more reliable, each fails independently
If single head ices over: both ASI and ALT/VSI fail simultaneously
If separate pitot ices: ASI fails, ALT/VSI work
If separate static ices: ASI unreliable, ALT/VSI fail

syllabus_desc: Explain the function of pitot heat.
concept: Pitot heat is an electrical heating element wrapped around the pitot tube, powered by the aircraft electrical system. Its sole function is to prevent ice from forming on the pitot tube. You turn it on before entering known icing, or proactively if you're climbing through a layer where icing is expected. The heater warms the tube to 40–50°C, well above the freezing point of water. Without pitot heat, even light icing will plug the pitot opening, and your airspeed indicator becomes unreliable or stops functioning.
