exam_tip: Gimbal error: DI/HI becomes unreliable if pitch or bank exceeds about 55 degrees. The gyro may tumble.
key_facts: Occurs when gimbal rings approach alignment during steep manoeuvres
Most DIs have pitch and bank limits of approximately 55 degrees
Exceeding limits causes the gyro to tumble and lose its reference

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors which the DI/HI is subject to: (b) real drift (or real wander)
concept: Real drift (real wander) is caused by bearing friction and mechanical imperfections in the gyro. No gyro is perfect -- friction at the bearings causes the rotor's spin axis to drift slowly from its set position. This is the main reason the DI must be periodically realigned to the magnetic compass. The rate of real drift varies between individual instruments.
exam_tip: Real drift is caused by bearing friction -- it's why you must realign the DI to the compass every 10-15 minutes.
key_facts: Caused by bearing friction and mechanical imperfections
Gyro spin axis drifts slowly from its set position
Requires periodic realignment to the magnetic compass (every 10-15 minutes)

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors which the DI/HI is subject to: (c) apparent drift (or apparent wander)
concept: Apparent drift (apparent wander) occurs because the gyro maintains its orientation in space while the Earth rotates underneath it. To an observer on the rotating Earth, the gyro appears to drift even though it's actually holding steady. The rate of apparent drift depends on your latitude -- maximum at the poles (15 degrees/hour) and zero at the equator.
exam_tip: Apparent drift: 15 degrees/hour x sin(latitude). Maximum at the poles, zero at the equator.
key_facts: Earth rotates beneath the space-fixed gyro, creating apparent heading change
Rate = 15 degrees/hour x sin(latitude)
Maximum at poles (15 degrees/hour); zero at equator

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors which the DI/HI is subject to: (d) low rotor speed
concept: At low rotor speed, the gyro has less angular momentum (rigidity) and becomes more susceptible to external forces, making it drift faster and respond sluggishly. A failing vacuum pump will cause the gyro to slow down, and the DI readings become increasingly unreliable. Check the vacuum gauge to confirm the gyro is receiving adequate suction.
exam_tip: Low rotor speed = less rigidity = faster drift and unreliable readings. Check the vacuum gauge for adequacy.
key_facts: Low speed reduces gyroscopic rigidity
Gyro drifts faster and responds sluggishly
Check vacuum gauge -- low suction means unreliable gyro instruments

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of a toppled directional gyro (DI/HI).
concept: A toppled DI rotor has lost its alignment with the vertical axis. The rotor is tilted, and the heading card is no longer level. Characteristics of a toppled DI include: the heading card will show erratic or nonsensical headings (it might show multiple headings simultaneously if viewed from different angles), the card might be tilted or off-level, and most obviously, the card will show incorrect heading compared to the magnetic compass. If you topple the DI in a dive (by exceeding gimbal limits), you might recover from the dive and roll level, but the DI card will be tilted and showing wrong heading. You can't rely on the reading. To verify topple, compare the DI heading to your magnetic compass: if they differ by more than a small amount (say, >10 degrees) and the DI card looks tilted, the DI is toppled. Recovery: you can't fix a toppled DI in flight—the gimbal is mechanically locked. You must land, shut down, restart (allowing the rotor to spin up again), and re-synchronise to the compass. Some DIs have a 'cage' knob (a mechanical lock) that locks the rotor in the vertical position for ground use only; caging the DI on the ground after a topple helps re-align it before the next flight. Flying with a toppled DI is dangerous because you're flying blind on heading—you have only the magnetic compass for reference, and the magnetic compass has turning errors and lag. The lesson is to avoid unusual attitudes that exceed gimbal limits.
exam_tip: Toppled DI: rotor tilted, card shows wrong heading, may appear askew. Compare to compass to verify. Can't fix in flight. Land and reset. Avoid unusual attitudes to prevent topple.
key_facts: Toppled DI: rotor has lost vertical alignment
Heading card: shows incorrect heading, may appear tilted
Erratic reading: card might show multiple headings if tilted
Verification: compare DI to magnetic compass; large difference = topple
Gimbal lock: mechanical stops prevent recovery in flight
Fix: land, restart gyro, synchronise to compass
Cage function: locks rotor vertically for ground recovery
Prevention: avoid unusual attitudes exceeding gimbal limits

syllabus_desc: Explain the DI/HI typical: (a) operating limitations
concept: DI/HI operating limitations include maximum pitch and bank angles (typically 55 degrees before gimbal lock risk), a minimum vacuum suction requirement (about 4.5 inHg), and maximum rotation rate. Exceeding these limits can cause the gyro to tumble, requiring it to be recaged and reset. The DI has no north-seeking ability -- it must be manually set and periodically checked.
exam_tip: DI limits: about 55 degrees pitch/bank, minimum 4.5 inHg vacuum. It must be manually set -- no magnetic seeking.
key_facts: Pitch and bank limits: approximately 55 degrees
Requires minimum vacuum suction of about 4.5 inHg
No north-seeking capability -- must be manually aligned to compass

syllabus_desc: Explain the DI/HI typical: (b) serviceability checks
concept: Serviceability checks for the DI include: verifying adequate vacuum (4.5-5.5 inHg), setting the DI to the compass heading in steady flight and rechecking after 10-15 minutes (drift should not exceed about 3 degrees in 15 minutes for a serviceable instrument), and checking that the card moves smoothly without sticking when you turn.
exam_tip: Set DI to compass, recheck in 15 minutes -- more than 3 degrees drift suggests a problem with the gyro.
key_facts: Check vacuum gauge for adequate suction (4.5-5.5 inHg)
Set to compass heading; recheck drift after 10-15 minutes
Maximum acceptable drift: approximately 3 degrees per 15 minutes

syllabus_desc: Explain the advantages of an electrically-driven DI/HI versus an air-driven instrument.
concept: An air-driven (vacuum-driven) DI depends on the vacuum pump operating reliably. If the pump fails, the rotor slows, and the DI becomes unreliable or fails completely. Also, the vacuum system is complex (pump, regulator, filter, relief valve, gauges) and needs maintenance. An electrically-driven DI uses an electric motor to spin the rotor. It requires electrical power (AC or DC depending on design) but doesn't depend on an engine-driven vacuum pump. If the alternator fails but the battery is good, the electric DI might continue working longer than a vacuum-driven one. Electric drive is also more compact and can be designed with lower power draw. The main advantage is redundancy: if one gyro system fails, you still have the other. On modern glass-panel aircraft, the DI is often replaced by an electronic heading display (part of the attitude/heading reference system, AHRS) that uses heading sensors and no mechanical gyro at all. The disadvantages of electric drive: it depends on electrical power, and some electric DIs draw significant current (reducing available electrical capacity). If both the alternator and battery fail, the electric DI fails too. Vacuum-driven systems are mechanical and don't have electrical dependencies, but they're heavier and bulkier. Most modern aircraft transitioning away from mechanical gyro instruments favour electronic displays that use solid-state sensors (magnetometers, accelerometers) instead, providing both advantages: no mechanical complexity and no vacuum/electrical dependencies for the core heading reference.
exam_tip: Electric DI: independent of vacuum, depends on electrical power, compact. Vacuum DI: independent of power, depends on pump. Modern: electronic AHRS, no mechanical gyro needed.
key_facts: Vacuum-driven: depends on engine-driven pump, complex system
Vacuum advantage: independent of electrical power
Vacuum disadvantage: pump failure = DI failure
Electric-driven: uses electric motor to spin rotor
Electric advantage: independent of vacuum system
Electric disadvantage: depends on electrical power supply
Electronic (AHRS): solid-state sensors, no mechanical gyro
Electronic advantage: small, robust, fast response, no maintenance

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of an artificial horizon/attitude indicator (AH/AI).
concept: An artificial horizon (attitude indicator, AH/AI) is a gyroscopic instrument that displays the aircraft's pitch and roll attitude. A gyro rotor spins around a horizontal fore-aft axis (parallel to the fuselage). Due to rigidity in space, the rotor maintains a level orientation regardless of how the aircraft moves. A mechanical linkage transmits the rotor's orientation to an attitude indicator display: a ball or model airplane that tilts with the rotor, showing pitch (nose up or down) and roll (wings tilted). The display is set against a horizon line: the ball's upper hemisphere (sky) is dark or blue, the lower hemisphere (ground) is tan or brown. Pitch is indicated by the position of the model aircraft relative to the horizon line: nose above = climbing, nose below = descending. Roll is indicated by the tilt of the model: wings level = 0 degrees bank, tilted left = left bank, tilted right = right bank. The artificial horizon is vacuum-powered (or electrically-powered in modern variants). A vacuum-powered AH is typically the most reliable instrument because the rotor is spinning continuously and maintains its level orientation without requiring external reference. In instrument flight, the artificial horizon is your primary pitch and roll reference—it tells you immediately whether you're climbing, descending, or banking, which is critical for safe instrument flying. The AH doesn't drift like a directional indicator because level (horizontal) is an absolute reference—gravity defines what's level. Modern electronic displays (glass cockpits) replace the mechanical AH with a digital display fed by an AHRS (attitude heading reference system) using solid-state sensors, but the principle is the same: display aircraft pitch and roll attitude.
exam_tip: AH/AI: horizontal fore-aft gyro maintains level. Display shows pitch and roll relative to horizon line. Primary instrument for attitude in instrument flight. Vacuum or electric powered.
key_facts: Artificial horizon (AH): horizontal fore-aft gyro spins
Rigidity: rotor maintains level orientation in inertial space
Display: attitude ball or model aircraft on horizon line
Pitch: ball position relative to horizon (above = climb, below = descent)
Roll: ball tilt relative to horizon (tilted = banked)
Vacuum-powered: engine-driven pump, continuous operation
Electrically-powered: motor drive, similar operation
Modern: electronic AHRS provides same indication digitally

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect on an air-driven AH/AI, including the errors in indication arising from a: (a) straight-line acceleration
concept: During a straight-line acceleration, an air-driven attitude indicator (AI) shows a false nose-up indication (pitch up error). This is because the accelerating air through the gyro housing creates an asymmetric force on the gyro, causing it to precess. During deceleration, a false nose-down indication occurs. These errors are brief and self-correcting.
exam_tip: Air-driven AI: acceleration causes false nose-up; deceleration causes false nose-down. Brief and self-correcting.
key_facts: Acceleration: false nose-up indication
Deceleration: false nose-down indication
Caused by asymmetric airflow through the gyro housing; self-correcting

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect on an air-driven AH/AI, including the errors in indication arising from a: (b) turn
concept: During a turn, an air-driven AI develops a small bank error. After rolling out of a turn of 180 degrees or more, the AI may show a slight bank in the opposite direction to the turn, and a slightly displaced pitch indication. These errors are caused by the erection mechanism responding to the centrifugal force during the turn. They are small and self-correct within a minute or so of levelling.
exam_tip: After a 180-degree+ turn, the air-driven AI may show slight opposite bank error. It self-corrects after levelling.
key_facts: Prolonged turns cause small bank and pitch errors in air-driven AIs
Error appears after rolling out -- slight bank in opposite direction
Self-correcting within about a minute of straight and level flight

syllabus_desc: Explain the general operating limitations of an: (a) air-driven AH/AI
concept: Air-driven AIs are limited by the vacuum supply from the engine-driven pump. They require adequate vacuum (typically 4.5-5.5 inHg), have pitch and bank limits (typically 60-70 degrees), and the erection mechanism introduces errors during acceleration and prolonged turns. They take 3-5 minutes to erect fully after start-up.
exam_tip: Air-driven AI: needs 3-5 minutes to erect after start-up, limits of about 60-70 degrees pitch/bank.
key_facts: Requires adequate vacuum from engine-driven pump
Pitch and bank limits: approximately 60-70 degrees
Takes 3-5 minutes to erect and stabilise after engine start

syllabus_desc: Explain the general operating limitations of an: (b) electrically-driven AH/AI
concept: Electrically-driven AIs are generally more accurate and have wider pitch and bank limits than air-driven types. They erect faster after power-up and are less susceptible to acceleration and turning errors because their erection system uses gravity switches rather than airflow. However, they depend on electrical power and will fail during a total electrical failure.
exam_tip: Electric AI: faster erection, wider limits, fewer turning errors -- but depends on electrical power.
key_facts: Wider pitch and bank limits than air-driven types
Faster erection time and less susceptible to acceleration errors
Depends on electrical power -- fails in total electrical failure

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between the indications of the TC and AH/AI under various conditions.
concept: Turn co-ordinator (TC) shows both turn rate and bank angle (via the tilted aircraft symbol). The needle indicates rate of turn (standard rate at the mark). The aircraft symbol shows bank angle (tilted left/right for bank, level for level flight). The balance ball shows coordination. Artificial horizon (AH/AI) shows pitch and roll attitude directly. The horizon line stays fixed; the aircraft symbol pitches up/down and rolls left/right relative to the horizon. No turn-rate indication (the AH doesn't explicitly show how fast you're turning). Under level flight: TC shows level aircraft symbol, centred needle, centred ball. AH shows aircraft level with horizon line, level pitch. Under a turn: TC shows tilted aircraft symbol (banking) and deflected needle (turn rate). Ball shows coordination. AH shows rolled aircraft (tilted wings relative to horizon) and possibly pitched (depending on climb/descent during turn). Under a climb: TC shows the aircraft symbol slightly tilted and a slight needle deflection (if there's any yaw component of the climb). AH shows nose-up pitch, wings level if it's a straight climb. Under a descent: TC similar to climb (needle might show slight deflection if yawed). AH shows nose-down pitch. Under a turn while climbing: TC shows both bank and turn rate, ball coordination. AH shows both roll (wings tilted) and pitch (nose up). Distinguishing: TC is primarily for turn rate and bank; AH is primarily for pitch and roll attitude. In instrument flying, you use both together: TC tells you turn rate and helps coordinate, AH tells you pitch and roll attitude. A classic instrument cross-check uses all three (needle, ball, airspeed) plus AH for a complete picture: TC needle = turn rate, ball = coordination, AH = attitude, airspeed = speed being maintained. Together they define the aircraft's flight path and motion.
exam_tip: TC: turn rate (needle) and bank (symbol). AH: pitch and roll. Use together: TC for rate/coordination, AH for attitude. Both needed for complete instrument flying picture.
key_facts: TC needle: turn rate, standard rate at mark
TC aircraft symbol: bank angle (tilted = banked)
TC ball: coordination (centred = coordinated)
AH: pitch attitude (nose up/down relative to horizon)
AH: roll attitude (wing tilt relative to horizon)
TC no pitch indication, AH no explicit turn-rate indication
Level flight: TC level symbol centred needle, AH level attitude
Climbing turn: TC shows bank and turn, AH shows pitch and roll

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of a Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS).
concept: A GNSS (like GPS, GLONASS, Galileo) works by calculating your position based on signals from satellites orbiting Earth. Each satellite broadcasts a radio signal containing its location and the precise time the signal was transmitted. Your receiver picks up signals from at least four satellites (more is better for accuracy). By measuring the time delay between transmission and reception, the receiver calculates the distance to each satellite (distance = speed of light × time delay). With distances to four or more satellites, geometry allows the receiver to pinpoint your exact position (latitude, longitude, altitude). The principle is trilateration: you need at least three satellites to find a 2D position (latitude and longitude), and a fourth for altitude. GPS (USA) has about 30 satellites in orbit; Galileo (EU) has similar coverage; GLONASS (Russia) and BeiDou (China) provide backup. In practice, more satellites mean better accuracy and faster position updates. Your aircraft GNSS receiver calculates position continuously (typically 1–10 times per second depending on the unit) and displays your location on a moving map or alongside your compass heading. Modern glass cockpits integrate GNSS position with other sensors (air data computer, heading reference) to provide a complete picture: your location, altitude, heading, and velocity. GNSS is incredibly accurate (within metres for civilian receivers, centimetres for military or specialized receivers) and is unaffected by weather. It's revolutionised aviation navigation, replacing many traditional aids (NDB, VOR) for general aviation.
exam_tip: GNSS: satellite signals give position. At least 4 satellites needed. Trilateration: distance to satellites calculated from signal delay. Very accurate, weather-independent.
key_facts: GNSS: Global Navigation Satellite System (GPS, Galileo, GLONASS, BeiDou)
Satellites: 30+ in orbit, continuous global coverage
Receiver: picks up signals, calculates distances
Trilateration: 4 satellites for 3D position (lat, long, alt)
Accuracy: civil receivers ±5–10 metres typical
Weather independent: signal penetrates clouds, works anywhere
Position update: 1–10 times per second typical
Integration: combined with air data and heading for moving map

syllabus_desc: Explain the limitations of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS).
concept: GNSS relies on signals from satellites orbiting thousands of kilometres above the Earth — and that distance is also its main weakness. Several limitations come with this dependency. GNSS signals are very weak by the time they reach your receiver, making them easy to disrupt. Intentional jamming (transmitting interference on the same frequency) can wipe out your GNSS position without warning. Spoofing is worse — false signals make your receiver think it's somewhere it isn't. Ionospheric and tropospheric errors occur because the atmosphere slows and bends the signals, introducing position errors that vary with the weather and time of day. Multipath errors happen when signals bounce off terrain, buildings or the aircraft structure before reaching the antenna, creating inaccurate range measurements. Poor satellite geometry (high PDOP — Position Dilution of Precision) amplifies all other errors. Your GNSS also can't verify its own accuracy without augmentation — hence the requirement for RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring), which compares satellite measurements to flag when the position fix is unreliable. GNSS gives no indication when it's producing a wrong answer unless RAIM is operating and sufficient satellites are in view. For IFR operations in NZ, a RAIM prediction must confirm RAIM availability at your estimated time of arrival.
exam_tip: GNSS limitations: weak signal (easy to jam or spoof), ionospheric/tropospheric delay errors, multipath, poor satellite geometry (PDOP), and critically — cannot self-verify without RAIM. Know that RAIM requires a minimum number of satellites and that NZ IFR requires a RAIM prediction check.
key_facts: GNSS signals are weak — susceptible to jamming and spoofing
Ionospheric and tropospheric errors delay signals, causing position errors
Multipath errors from signal reflections off terrain/structures
High PDOP (poor satellite geometry) amplifies all errors
GNSS cannot self-verify accuracy — requires RAIM
RAIM requires minimum satellite count to detect faulty signals
NZ IFR requirement: RAIM prediction check before departure

syllabus_desc: Explain the term RAIM prediction and the significance of these predictions.
concept: RAIM stands for 'Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring'. It's a mathematical function that detects when a GNSS receiver's position solution is unreliable by comparing calculations made using different subsets of available satellites. If you have 5 satellites, the receiver calculates position using satellites 1-4, then using 1-3-4-5, then 1-2-4-5, etc. If all calculations yield the same position (within tolerance), RAIM says the solution is good. If one calculation is significantly different, RAIM detects a problem: one of the satellites might be providing bad data (or you're experiencing multipath/jamming). RAIM prediction is a pre-flight function: you input your desired departure and arrival locations and time of flight, and the GNSS receiver (or a ground-based tool) predicts whether RAIM will be available throughout your planned route and time. RAIM availability depends on satellite geometry: at certain times and locations, there might not be enough satellites with good geometry to provide reliable monitoring. If RAIM is predicted to be unavailable, the receiver warns you before flight. The significance is that RAIM availability determines whether you can safely rely on GNSS for primary navigation. In areas or times with poor satellite geometry, RAIM might not be available, meaning the receiver can't verify its solution. Without RAIM, you're flying GNSS-only without automatic integrity checking, which is considered less safe for critical operations. For general aviation in NZ, RAIM is usually available (good satellite coverage), but in remote areas or at high latitudes, RAIM gaps occur. Pilots planning to rely on GNSS for critical navigation (such as approach) need to verify RAIM availability before flight. Modern integrated systems (glass cockpits) display RAIM status in-flight, warning if RAIM is lost.
exam_tip: RAIM: receiver checks itself using multiple satellite subsets. Detects bad satellite or interference. Prediction: pre-flight check that RAIM will be available. No RAIM = less reliable, use backup aids.
key_facts: RAIM: receiver autonomous integrity monitoring
Method: compares position using different satellite combinations
Detects: bad satellite signal or multipath/jamming effects
RAIM prediction: pre-flight availability check
Depends on: satellite geometry at your location and time
RAIM gaps: occur in some areas or times with poor geometry
Significance: available RAIM = trustworthy position
No RAIM: position less trusted, use backup navigation aids

syllabus_desc: Explain the term RNP and the significance of the RNP value.
concept: RNP stands for 'Required Navigation Performance'. It's a specification of how accurately an aircraft must navigate to fly a particular route or procedure. RNP is expressed as a radius in nautical miles: RNP 10 means the aircraft must be within 10 nautical miles (19 km) of the intended track 95% of the time. RNP 5 means within 5 nautical miles 95% of the time. RNP 0.3 means within 0.3 nautical miles (about 550 metres) 95% of the time. Different procedures require different RNP: a standard GNSS approach might require RNP 0.3 to 1.0 (tight accuracy); cruise airways might allow RNP 10. Achieving a specific RNP requires the aircraft's navigation system to be capable, the pilot to be trained, and the RAIM/integrity checking to be available. An aircraft equipped with a modern GNSS receiver and integrated navigation system might achieve RNP 0.3. An older aircraft with only standalone GNSS might only achieve RNP 5. The significance is that RNP defines navigation safety for a given airspace or procedure. If you're flying an RNP 0.3 procedure but your aircraft can only achieve RNP 5, you're not qualified for that procedure and it's not safe. RNP requirements are becoming the standard way to define navigation performance globally, replacing older methods that relied on specific ground-based radio aids. Operators must know their aircraft's certified RNP level and only use it for procedures requiring that RNP or better.
exam_tip: RNP: required navigation performance, expressed in nautical miles. RNP 10 = 10 nm track, RNP 0.3 = 0.3 nm. Aircraft must achieve required RNP for procedure. Know your aircraft's certified RNP.
key_facts: RNP: required navigation performance
Unit: nautical miles radius from intended track
Standard: 95% compliance (5% outside allowed)
RNP 10: cruise, standard procedures, ±10 nm
RNP 5: more demanding airways, better systems
RNP 1: precision approach, modern GNSS
RNP 0.3: tight approach procedures, best equipment
Significance: defines qualification for procedure, ensures safety

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of a typical TCAS system.
concept: TCAS (Traffic Collision Avoidance System) is your last line of defence against mid-air collisions. It works independently of ATC—interrogating transponders on nearby aircraft to build a picture of who's around you and where they're going. TCAS I (found on smaller aircraft) gives you Traffic Advisories (TAs)—essentially 'heads up, traffic nearby' alerts with bearing and relative altitude. TCAS II (larger aircraft, airlines) adds Resolution Advisories (RAs)—actual 'climb' or 'descend' commands coordinated between the two aircraft so they don't both do the same thing. The system tracks closure rates and predicts if a collision is likely based on time, not distance. It interrogates Mode C and Mode S transponders, so it only sees aircraft with working transponders (not a glider with no transponder, for instance). In the helicopter world, TCAS is less common on training types but increasingly fitted to turbine helicopters doing IFR work. When you get an RA, you follow it—even if ATC says otherwise. The RA takes priority because TCAS can see things ATC might miss.
exam_tip: TCAS I = TAs only (traffic advisories). TCAS II = TAs + RAs (resolution advisories). RAs take priority over ATC instructions. Works by interrogating transponders—can't see non-transponder aircraft. Based on time to collision, not distance.
key_facts: TCAS: Traffic Collision Avoidance System. Independent of ATC. TCAS I: Traffic Advisories only. TCAS II: Traffic Advisories + Resolution Advisories. Interrogates Mode C and Mode S transponders. Cannot detect non-transponder aircraft. Based on time to collision (tau), not distance. RAs take priority over ATC instructions. TCAS II coordinates avoidance between aircraft.

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of a typical GPWS system. 28.64 “EFIS” Instrument Displays
concept: GPWS (Ground Proximity Warning System) prevents controlled flight into terrain—historically one of aviation's biggest killers. It uses radio altimeter data, barometric altitude rate, ILS glideslope deviation, and landing gear/flap configuration to detect dangerous situations. The basic modes trigger aural and visual warnings: 'PULL UP' for excessive descent rate, 'TERRAIN' for approaching rising ground, 'DON'T SINK' for altitude loss after takeoff, 'TOO LOW—GEAR' or 'TOO LOW—FLAPS' for approach configuration errors, and 'GLIDESLOPE' for excessive deviation below. EGPWS (Enhanced GPWS) adds a terrain database and GPS position, so it can look ahead and warn you before the terrain becomes a problem—not just react to what's directly below. For helicopters, HTAWS (Helicopter Terrain Awareness and Warning System) is the tailored version. Standard GPWS would trigger constant nuisance alerts during normal helicopter operations (hovering low, landing in fields, flying at low altitude). HTAWS filters these out and focuses on genuine threats to helicopter flight profiles. When GPWS or HTAWS says 'PULL UP', you pull up. Don't second-guess it.
exam_tip: Know the basic GPWS modes and their warnings. EGPWS adds terrain database + GPS (forward-looking). HTAWS = helicopter version, filters normal low-altitude ops. 'PULL UP' = immediate action required.
key_facts: GPWS: Ground Proximity Warning System. Uses radio altimeter, barometric rate, ILS, configuration data. Modes: excessive sink rate, terrain closure, altitude loss after takeoff, unsafe configuration, glideslope deviation. EGPWS: adds terrain database + GPS for forward-looking warnings. HTAWS: helicopter-specific version, filters normal low-altitude operations. Warnings include: PULL UP, TERRAIN, DON'T SINK, TOO LOW—GEAR/FLAPS, GLIDESLOPE.

syllabus_desc: Describe the components and operation of a typical EFIS cockpit display system.
concept: EFIS stands for 'Electronic Flight Instrument System'. It replaces traditional mechanical instruments with digital displays showing flight information on screens. A typical EFIS cockpit has two main displays: the Primary Flight Display (PFD) and the Navigation Display (ND). The PFD shows attitude (artificial horizon), airspeed, altitude, vertical speed, heading, and other critical flight parameters in a format familiar to pilots (resembling the steam gauges they're used to). The ND (or moving map display) shows the aircraft's position, navigation aids (VORs, NDBs), waypoints, airways, radar weather, and terrain. The EFIS system has several components: data sources (air data computer, heading reference system, navigation sensors, weather radar), processing units (flight management system, air data computer, display processors), and the displays themselves (typically large LCD or digital screens). The air data computer (ADC) processes pitot-static inputs to calculate true airspeed, altitude, and vertical speed. The heading reference system (HRS, also called attitude heading reference system, AHRS) uses accelerometers, gyros, and magnetometers to calculate attitude (pitch and roll) and heading. The flight management system (FMS) integrates all data and provides navigation, flight planning, autopilot commands, and synthesis. The displays are modular: you can have one large screen showing both PFD and ND side-by-side, or separate screens, or smaller auxiliary displays. Modern glass cockpits typically have two main displays (captain and first officer), a centre overhead display, and possibly smaller screens for engine parameters and checklists. The system is incredibly flexible: you can customize what information is displayed and how it's presented. Pilots can select different display modes (approach mode zooms the map, cruise mode shows a wider view) and can select which navigation sources are shown.
exam_tip: EFIS: electronic displays replace steam gauges. Main displays: PFD (attitude, speed, alt, heading), ND (moving map, navaids, weather). Sources: ADC (air data), AHRS (attitude/heading), FMS (integration). Flexible, customizable.
key_facts: EFIS: electronic flight instrument system
PFD: primary flight display (attitude, airspeed, altitude)
ND: navigation display (moving map, navaids, terrain)
ADC: air data computer (airspeed, altitude, VSI)
AHRS: attitude heading reference system (pitch, roll, heading)
FMS: flight management system (integration, planning, autopilot)
Data sources: pitot-static, attitude sensors, heading sensors, GPS, weather radar
Display: modular, can show PFD, ND, engine info, checklists
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the: (a) air data computer
concept: An air data computer (ADC) receives raw pitot and static pressure inputs, along with temperature, and calculates airspeed, altitude, vertical speed, and Mach number digitally. It replaces the individual mechanical instruments with electronic processing and feeds the computed data to glass cockpit displays. The ADC applies corrections for position error and compressibility automatically.
exam_tip: The ADC takes raw pitot/static/temperature inputs and computes airspeed, altitude, and VSI digitally for glass displays.
key_facts: Digitally computes airspeed, altitude, vertical speed from pitot/static inputs
Automatically applies position error and compressibility corrections
Feeds computed data to Primary Flight Display (PFD)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the: (b) signal generator
concept: Signal generators (AHRS -- Attitude and Heading Reference System) replace traditional mechanical gyros with solid-state sensors (accelerometers, rate gyros, and magnetometers) to provide attitude and heading information. The AHRS computes aircraft pitch, roll, and yaw electronically and sends the data to the PFD. No spinning gyro rotors -- more reliable with no moving parts to wear out.
exam_tip: AHRS uses solid-state sensors instead of spinning gyros -- more reliable, no mechanical wear, feeds the PFD.
key_facts: Solid-state sensors: accelerometers, rate gyros, magnetometers
Computes pitch, roll, and heading electronically
No spinning rotors -- more reliable than mechanical gyros
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the: (c) input data sources to a basic glass flight display system
concept: Input data sources to a basic glass flight display include: pitot and static pressure (for airspeed, altitude, VSI), AHRS sensors (for attitude and heading), GPS (for position, groundspeed, track), magnetometer (for magnetic heading), OAT probe (for temperature), and engine sensors (for RPM, MAP, fuel flow, etc.). The glass display integrates all these inputs onto one or two screens.
exam_tip: Glass displays integrate pitot/static, AHRS, GPS, magnetometer, OAT, and engine data onto unified screens.
key_facts: Pitot/static pressure: airspeed, altitude, VSI
AHRS: attitude and heading; GPS: position and groundspeed
Engine sensors: RPM, MAP, fuel flow, temperatures -- displayed on MFD
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Describe the components and operation of an Attitude Heading Reference System (AHRS).
concept: An AHRS (Attitude Heading Reference System) is a self-contained system that calculates aircraft attitude (pitch and roll) and heading using internal sensors and no external references. The main components are: accelerometers (measure acceleration in three axes), gyroscopes (measure rotation rates), and a magnetometer (measure Earth's magnetic field). The AHRS processes signals from these sensors using complex algorithms to calculate aircraft pitch, roll, and heading continuously. The accelerometers sense gravity and aircraft acceleration; the gyros sense rotation rates; the magnetometer provides heading reference. The algorithm fuses all three sensor inputs to produce accurate attitude and heading estimates. The fusion is necessary because individual sensors have errors: gyros drift over time, accelerometers are noisy and affected by aircraft acceleration, magnetometers are affected by local magnetic disturbances (aircraft structure, electrical fields). By combining all three, the AHRS compensates for individual sensor weaknesses. The AHRS outputs pitch, roll, and heading (and optionally turn rate, slip/skid) to the flight displays and autopilot. The system is independent of any external references: it doesn't need vacuum (like a mechanical gyro), doesn't need compass swing calibration, and works in any attitude including inverted flight. The disadvantage is it depends on electrical power: no power means no AHRS. Also, the system can 'drift' (attitude error increases over time) if the sensors develop faults or if the fusion algorithm is confused (such as during a spin or unusual manoeuvre where acceleration doesn't match the calculated attitude). Modern AHRS systems are very accurate (±1–2 degrees pitch/roll) and reliable, and they're standard in all modern glass cockpits.
exam_tip: AHRS: accelerometers + gyros + magnetometer + fusion algorithm = attitude and heading. No external reference needed. Accurate and independent. Requires electrical power.
key_facts: AHRS: attitude heading reference system
Sensors: accelerometers (3-axis), gyroscopes (rotation rates), magnetometer (heading)
Algorithm: sensor fusion combining all three inputs
Outputs: pitch, roll, heading, turn rate, slip/skid
Advantages: independent, works in any attitude, accurate, reliable
Disadvantage: requires electrical power
Drift: attitude error can accumulate if sensors fault or in unusual manoeuvres
Modern accuracy: ±1–2 degrees pitch and roll typical
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Describe the management of EFIS system failures.
concept: EFIS failure management follows a hierarchy. If one display fails, information can typically be transferred to the remaining display (reversionary mode). If the display processor fails, standby instruments (conventional 'steam gauges') provide backup—most EFIS-equipped aircraft retain at least a standby ASI, altimeter, and attitude indicator. Battery-powered standby instruments operate independently of the main electrical system. Pilots must know their aircraft's specific reversionary procedures, which displays can be combined, and what information is lost at each failure level. Regular practice switching to backup instruments is essential.
exam_tip: Know the failure hierarchy: single display failure → reversionary mode; processor failure → standby instruments. Standby instruments are usually battery-powered and independent.
key_facts: Reversionary mode transfers data to remaining display
Standby instruments provide backup
Standby typically: ASI, altimeter, attitude indicator
Standby instruments often battery-powered
Know aircraft-specific reversionary procedures
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Explain the reasons why excessively high engine temperatures must be avoided.
concept: Your engine's temperature management is all about keeping things in that sweet spot. If the cylinders get too hot, you're looking at detonation, pre-ignition, and your piston rings seizing. Cool things down too fast though—say, a rapid descent after climb—and you've got thermal shock cracking your cylinders. You want smooth, gradual temperature changes.
exam_tip: They'll test whether you understand detonation risk at high temps and thermal shock at low temps. Know that slow cooling is better than rapid cooling.
key_facts: Excessive heat causes detonation, pre-ignition, and component failure
Rapid cooling causes thermal shock and cylinder cracking
Maintain steady power settings to avoid temperature swings
Cowl flaps and airflow management control temperature rise and descent rate
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Explain the most common means of engine cooling in helicopters fitted with piston engines.
concept: Most light piston-engine helicopters use air cooling—the cylinders have external cooling fins that increase surface area, and air flowing over these fins carries heat away. The key to making this work is ducting: cowling and baffles direct air from the cooling intake across the cylinder fins in a controlled path. Without proper baffles, air takes the path of least resistance and bypasses the cylinders entirely. In a helicopter, cooling airflow comes from two sources: rotor downwash during hover (air pushed down by the rotor passes over the engine) and ram air in forward flight. Hover is the more challenging condition because downwash provides less consistent cooling than forward flight airflow—especially on hot days with high density altitude where the rotor is working harder and producing less downwash. Some installations use a belt-driven or electric cooling fan to supplement airflow. Oil also plays a cooling role—it circulates through the engine absorbing heat from the cylinders, pistons, and bearings, then passes through an oil cooler (a small air-to-oil radiator) before returning. Cylinder head temperature (CHT) and oil temperature gauges in the cockpit are your windows into how well the cooling system is coping.
exam_tip: Air-cooled: cooling fins + baffles + ducted airflow. Cooling sources: rotor downwash (hover) and ram air (forward flight). Hover = worse cooling. Oil cooler supplements cylinder cooling. Monitor CHT and oil temp gauges.
key_facts: Most light piston helicopters: air-cooled engines. Cooling fins on cylinders increase surface area. Baffles and cowling direct airflow over fins. Cooling air sources: rotor downwash (hover), ram air (forward flight). Hover: less consistent cooling than forward flight. Hot days + high density altitude = worst cooling conditions. Some use belt-driven or electric cooling fan. Oil cooling: oil absorbs heat, passes through oil cooler (radiator). Monitor CHT and oil temperature gauges.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: For an air-cooled engine, explain the function of: cowling ducts and baffles.
concept: Cowling ducts and baffles direct cooling air over the cylinders in the correct pattern. The cowling captures ram air from the front and the baffles channel it around each cylinder head and barrel before exiting at the bottom. Without proper baffling, some cylinders overheat while others run too cool. Damaged or missing baffles are a common cause of engine overheating.
exam_tip: Baffles direct airflow over cylinders -- damaged baffles cause uneven cooling and overheating.
key_facts: Cowling captures ram air; baffles direct it around each cylinder
Air flows over top of cylinders and exits at the bottom/rear
Damaged or missing baffles cause uneven cooling and hot spots
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: For an air-cooled engine, explain the function of: cooling fins.
concept: Cooling fins are raised metal surfaces cast into the cylinder barrel and head that increase the surface area exposed to cooling air. More surface area means more heat transfer. The cylinder head (hottest part) has the largest fins, especially around the exhaust port. Fins must be kept clean and undamaged for effective cooling.
exam_tip: Cooling fins increase surface area for heat transfer -- cylinder heads have the largest fins because they're hottest.
key_facts: Increase surface area for heat dissipation
Largest fins on cylinder heads, especially near exhaust ports
Broken or dirty fins reduce cooling effectiveness
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: In a typical aircraft engine, explain the function of: (c) pilot-operated cowl flaps
concept: Cowl flaps are adjustable openings at the rear of the cowling that the pilot controls to regulate cooling airflow. Open cowl flaps increase cooling air exit area, drawing more air through. Closed cowl flaps restrict exit, reducing cooling but also drag. Open for climb (high power, low speed = hot); close for cruise (lower power, higher speed = adequate cooling with less drag).
exam_tip: Cowl flaps: open for climb (more cooling), close for cruise (less drag). They control cooling airflow exit.
key_facts: Pilot-controlled openings at rear of cowling
Open for climb: maximum cooling at low airspeed/high power
Close for cruise: reduces cooling drag when adequate airflow exists
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: In a typical aircraft engine, explain the function of: (d) oil cooler
concept: The oil cooler removes heat from the engine oil that has absorbed heat from the engine's internal components. It works like a small radiator -- oil flows through finned tubes while air passes over them. A thermostatic bypass valve sends cold oil around the cooler during warm-up. The oil cooler handles about 10-15% of total engine heat rejection.
exam_tip: The oil cooler handles about 10-15% of engine heat rejection. A bypass valve routes cold oil around it during warm-up.
key_facts: Removes heat from engine oil via finned tubes exposed to airflow
Thermostatic bypass valve routes cold oil around cooler during warm-up
Handles approximately 10-15% of total engine cooling
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: In a typical aircraft engine, explain the function of: (e) radiator
concept: Radiators are used in liquid-cooled engines to dissipate heat from the coolant to the air. The heated coolant circulates through the radiator where it flows through thin tubes with fins, transferring heat to the passing airflow. Liquid-cooled engines are less common in light aircraft but offer more even temperature control across cylinders.
exam_tip: Radiators are for liquid-cooled engines only -- they transfer coolant heat to the airstream, like a car radiator.
key_facts: Used in liquid-cooled engines to dissipate coolant heat
Coolant flows through finned tubes exposed to airflow
Less common in light aircraft; more common in larger/older engine types
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: In a typical aircraft engine, explain the function of: (f) intercooler
concept: An intercooler cools the compressed air between the turbocharger/supercharger compressor and the engine intake. Compressing air heats it, which reduces density (counteracting the purpose of supercharging) and increases detonation risk. The intercooler removes this heat, restoring air density and reducing detonation risk.
exam_tip: Intercooler cools compressed intake air to increase its density and reduce detonation risk.
key_facts: Cools air heated by turbo/supercharger compression
Increases compressed air density for better power output
Reduces detonation risk from hot intake air
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Explain the general engine handling techniques for maintaining engine temperatures within the proper range, for aircraft: (a) without CHT (cylinder head temperature) gauge or cowl flaps
concept: Without a CHT gauge or cowl flaps, manage engine temperature by adjusting airspeed and power. In the climb, maintain at least the recommended minimum airspeed to ensure adequate cooling airflow. Avoid prolonged high-power, low-speed operations. If engine temperatures are high, temporarily level off to increase airspeed and reduce power until temperatures stabilise.
exam_tip: No CHT gauge or cowl flaps: maintain minimum climb speed, avoid prolonged high power at low airspeed.
key_facts: Maintain recommended minimum climb speed for adequate cooling
Avoid prolonged high power at low airspeed
If overheating is suspected, level off to increase cooling airflow
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Explain the general engine handling techniques for maintaining engine temperatures within the proper range, for aircraft: (b) fitted with CHT gauge and cowl flaps
concept: With a CHT gauge and cowl flaps, actively monitor CHT and adjust cowl flaps accordingly. Open cowl flaps for climb and when CHT is approaching limits. Close them progressively in cruise to reduce drag once temperatures stabilise. During descent, close cowl flaps to prevent engine shock-cooling (rapid temperature drop that can crack cylinder heads).
exam_tip: Monitor CHT, open cowl flaps for climb, close for cruise. Close during descent to prevent shock cooling.
key_facts: Open cowl flaps during climb to maximise cooling
Close progressively in cruise to reduce drag
Close during descent to prevent shock cooling (rapid temp drop)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirements which the undercarriage/landing skid system must be able to meet.
concept: Helicopter landing gear must absorb the energy of normal and heavy landings, support the aircraft weight during ground operations, and provide stability against dynamic rotor forces and wind loads. Skid-type gear must withstand vertical loads from landing, lateral loads from slope operations, and fore-aft loads during run-on landings. The gear must not induce ground resonance in articulated rotor systems. Design requirements include adequate ground clearance for the tail rotor and fuselage underside, resistance to fatigue from repeated landing cycles, and ability to support the aircraft during maintenance with rotor removed.
exam_tip: Landing gear must handle: vertical landing loads, lateral slope loads, dynamic rotor forces. Must not cause ground resonance. Adequate tail rotor clearance essential.
key_facts: Must absorb normal and heavy landing energy
Must support weight during ground operations
Must resist lateral loads for slope operations
Must not induce ground resonance
Adequate tail rotor ground clearance required
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Describe the features of a simple light helicopter undercarriage/landing skid construction.
concept: Helicopter landing skids are the tubular framework that extends below the fuselage and contacts the ground during landing. They're typically constructed from steel tubes (often with shock absorption built in) that spread the aircraft weight over a large landing area. Unlike aircraft wheels, skids have no rolling friction—they slide across rough terrain, which is why helicopters can land in unprepared areas like hillsides, beaches, and clearings. The skids are attached to the fuselage via elastic or spring elements that absorb landing shock. In an R22, the skids are connected by a cross-bar that helps maintain proper spacing and provides additional structural rigidity. The design allows the helicopter to land on slopes up to about 25 degrees, which is essential for remote operations in NZ's mountainous terrain.
exam_tip: Skids don't have wheels—they slide. This lets helicopters land anywhere with firm ground. Know the shock absorption system and why skids can handle slopes.
key_facts: Tubular steel construction extending below fuselage
Contact ground directly (no wheels)
Shock absorption system for landing impact
Enable landing on slopes up to ~25 degrees
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Explain the procedure for tie down (or picketing) of a light helicopter.
concept: Tying down a light helicopter involves securing the aircraft to prevent movement from wind or rotor sail. Typical procedure: ensure rotor brake is applied, secure main rotor blades (blade tie-downs or blade sock), tie the aircraft down at designated tie-down points on the skids or fuselage using approved ropes or straps to ground anchors. The tail boom should be secured if applicable. Blade covers protect blades from UV and weather damage. In high wind conditions, the helicopter should be faced into wind if possible. Never tie down in a way that could stress the airframe or rotor system. Follow the aircraft flight manual procedures.
exam_tip: Tie-down sequence: rotor brake on, blades secured, aircraft tied to ground anchors at approved points. Face into wind. Never stress the airframe. Follow flight manual.
key_facts: Apply rotor brake first
Secure main rotor blades with tie-downs
Use approved tie-down points on skids/fuselage
Face into wind if possible
Follow aircraft flight manual procedures
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Describe the pilot checks of oleo-pneumatic undercarriage legs.
concept: Before flight you're checking the health of those struts. Get down there with a tyre gauge and check the air pressure—your POH's got the numbers. For a Piper Aztec that's usually around 170 psi, but check your specific aircraft. Press on the strut; it should feel firm with a small amount of give—maybe 2-3 cm compression. If it's too soft, you've either lost air pressure or the metering orifice is blocked. If it's hard as concrete, you might be over-pressurised. Look for oil seepage around the piston rod and at the rod wiper seal. Some light weeping's normal; heavy oil trails mean it's time for maintenance. Check the tyres for tread, wear, and condition.
exam_tip: Air pressure check is critical. Soft struts affect both landing characteristics and braking effectiveness. Know the aircraft's pressure limits.
key_facts: Check nitrogen pressure against AFM/POH specification
Strut should compress 2-3 cm when pressed by hand
Soft strut indicates low pressure or blocked metering orifice
Hard strut indicates over-pressurisation
Inspect for oil seepage around piston rod
Check tyre tread, wear, and condition
Rod wiper seal should show no excessive leakage
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Describe the construction of aircraft wheel assemblies and tyres.
concept: Your aircraft wheel's a two-part assembly. You've got a wheel rim (usually aluminium alloy) with the tyre mounted on it, and it spins on a stub axle. The axle comes out the end of the landing gear leg and's held in place by a castellated nut with a cotter pin—you'll see that during preflight. Inside the wheel cavity you've got bearing races (usually ball or tapered roller bearings) packed with grease. The brake disc bolts to the wheel, and when you press the pedal, the brake assembly clamps that disc. Aircraft tyres are radial ply construction (not bias), rated for specific load and speed limits. They're beefier than car tyres because we land hard and take off quick. Tread depth matters for water drainage on wet strips.
exam_tip: Two-piece assembly: rim and tyre. Bearings are sealed and packed. Radial ply tyres are standard. Know that tyre failure is rare but brake heat can damage it.
key_facts: Wheel assembly consists of rim (aluminium alloy) and tyre
Wheel spins on stub axle secured with castellated nut and cotter pin
Ball or tapered roller bearings packed with grease
Brake disc bolts directly to wheel
Aircraft tyres are radial ply construction
Load and speed ratings specified for each tyre type
Tread depth important for wet runway water drainage
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirements for tyre care and checks.
concept: Your tyres are wearing out every flight—it's just a question of how much. Before flight, visually inspect for tread depth, cracks, separation, and uneven wear. Minimum tread is usually 2/32 inch depth; if you're much below that, you're risking hydroplaning on wet strips. Check for cuts, stones lodged in treads, and any bulges in the sidewall (that's a flat). Look at the tyre for even wear across the width—one-sided wear might mean your wheel alignment's off. After landing, feel the tyres gently (careful, they can be hot). If one's noticeably hotter than the others, something's wrong—maybe brake drag or under-inflation. Check pressure cold (preferably next morning) because hot-pressure readings are misleading. Store tyres indoors, out of sunlight and away from ozone-generating equipment. Ultraviolet light and ozone destroy rubber.
exam_tip: Minimum 2/32 inch tread. Check pressure cold. Uneven wear or hot spots mean investigation needed. UV and ozone are your enemies.
key_facts: Minimum tread depth 2/32 inch (0.6 mm)
Inspect visually before every flight for cracks, separation, and bulges
Check for even wear across tyre width
Feel tyres after landing—excessive heat indicates problems
Measure tyre pressure cold, not after landing
Correct inflation according to AFM/POH specification
Store tyres indoors, protected from UV and ozone
Rotate tyres per maintenance schedule to extend life
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Describe the main components of retractable landing gear.
concept: Retractable landing gear on helicopters (found on types like the AS350 or larger turbine machines) has several key components. The gear legs themselves—usually oleo-pneumatic struts combining oil and nitrogen gas for shock absorption. The retraction mechanism, typically hydraulic actuators (cylinders and pistons) powered by the aircraft's hydraulic system. An uplock holds the gear securely in the retracted position during flight, and a downlock holds it firmly extended for landing—both are mechanical locks backed up by proximity switches or microswitches that tell the cockpit indicators whether the gear is locked. A selector valve controlled by the cockpit gear lever directs hydraulic fluid to extend or retract. Emergency extension is usually a free-fall system using gravity, sometimes assisted by compressed nitrogen, or a hand pump if hydraulics fail. Warning systems include gear-not-down warnings tied to airspeed, altitude, or power settings, and position indicator lights in the cockpit (typically green for down-and-locked, red for in transit, no light for up-and-locked). Wheel wells or fairings house the retracted gear for a clean aerodynamic profile.
exam_tip: Components: oleo struts, hydraulic actuators, uplocks/downlocks, selector valve, emergency extension (gravity/nitrogen), warning systems, position indicators. Know what green/red/no light means.
key_facts: Oleo-pneumatic struts: oil + nitrogen gas for shock absorption. Hydraulic actuators: extend and retract gear. Uplocks: hold gear retracted in flight. Downlocks: hold gear extended for landing. Selector valve: directs hydraulic flow. Emergency extension: gravity free-fall, nitrogen assist, or hand pump. Warning systems: gear-not-down tied to airspeed/altitude/power. Indicators: green = down-and-locked, red = in transit.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of operation of a typical retractable landing gear.
concept: When you move the gear selector to 'UP', the selector valve routes hydraulic pressure to the retract side of the actuator cylinders. The actuators overcome the downlock mechanisms (which release under hydraulic pressure or mechanical linkage) and begin folding the gear legs into the wheel wells. As the gear reaches full retraction, the uplocks engage mechanically, holding the gear in place even if hydraulic pressure is lost. Cockpit indicators transition from green (down-and-locked) through red (in transit) to no light (up-and-locked). For extension, selecting 'DOWN' routes hydraulic pressure to the other side of the actuators. The uplocks release, actuators push the gear down, and the downlocks snap into place. Green lights confirm locked position. If hydraulics fail, the emergency system lets gravity pull the gear down (free-fall), sometimes assisted by a nitrogen bottle or manual hand pump. The free-fall system typically releases the uplocks mechanically (a cable or handle in the cockpit) and lets the gear drop under its own weight. Wind load in flight can actually help push the gear into the locked-down position. Always confirm three greens before landing.
exam_tip: Normal operation: hydraulic actuators, selector valve routes pressure. Emergency: free-fall (gravity), nitrogen assist, or hand pump. Know the indicator light sequence: green → red → off (retraction). Always three greens for landing.
key_facts: Retraction: selector valve routes hydraulic pressure to retract actuators. Downlocks release, gear folds into wells, uplocks engage. Extension: pressure routes to extend side, uplocks release, gear extends, downlocks engage. Emergency: free-fall (gravity), nitrogen assist, or manual hand pump. Uplock release: mechanical cable or cockpit handle. Indicators: green = down-locked, red = transit, off = up-locked. Always confirm three green lights before landing.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Describe typical: (a) emergency gear operation systems
concept: Emergency gear extension systems provide a backup method to lower the landing gear if the normal system fails. Common methods include: a manual hand pump (hydraulic), a free-fall release (gravity drops the gear), a compressed gas (nitrogen) bottle, or a manual crank. The specific system depends on the aircraft type.
exam_tip: Emergency gear methods: hand pump, free-fall/gravity, nitrogen bottle, or manual crank -- know your aircraft's system.
key_facts: Manual hydraulic hand pump is a common backup method
Free-fall (gravity extension) drops gear under its own weight
Compressed nitrogen bottles provide emergency hydraulic pressure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Describe typical: (b) associated emergency gear operation procedures
concept: Emergency gear procedures typically involve: confirm the normal system has failed, check hydraulic fluid level and circuit breakers, reduce speed to below the maximum gear extension speed (VLE), follow the POH emergency gear extension procedure, allow extra time for the gear to lock, and confirm three greens before landing. Always fly a low pass for a visual check if possible.
exam_tip: Follow the POH procedure exactly. Reduce speed below VLE, extend gear, confirm three green lights before landing.
key_facts: Reduce speed below VLE (maximum gear extension speed)
Follow the specific POH emergency extension procedure
Confirm three green gear-down-and-locked indications before landing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Describe typical gear warning and indicating systems.
concept: Your gear indication system uses micro-switches on each landing gear leg. When the leg's fully extended and locked, the switch closes and completes a circuit to your green light (or green needle on an indicator). When the leg's fully retracted, a different switch closes, and the light goes out—that's your 'up and locked' condition. Any intermediate position (partially extended) leaves both switches open, and you get a red light or flashing warning. The warning horn's usually wired to the throttle proximity switch; if you reduce power (like on approach) and the gear's still up, a horn blares in the cockpit. Some systems have redundant indicator lamps. The beauty of micro-switches is they're simple, reliable, and require no pilot input—they just tell you what the gear's actually doing.
exam_tip: Green = down and locked on all three legs. Red or no light = unsafe condition. Warning horn sounds if throttle is reduced with gear up.
key_facts: Micro-switches on each landing gear leg sense position
Green light indicates gear fully extended and locked
Red light indicates unsafe condition (partial extension)
No light indicates gear fully retracted and locked
Warning horn activates when power is reduced with gear in unsafe position
Redundant indication systems on some aircraft types
Indicators are independent of hydraulic system functionality
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of ground retraction protection systems.
concept: You're sitting on the ground at Ardmore after landing, and you don't want someone accidentally raising the gear while you're still on the pavement—you'd break the gear legs and wreck the aircraft. Ground retraction protection systems prevent this. The usual method is a squat switch on one or both main landing gear legs. When the aircraft is on the ground and weight's on the legs, this switch closes. If someone tries to operate the gear selector, the squat switch blocks the 'up' command from reaching the hydraulic system—the selector moves but nothing happens. The system releases once the aircraft's airborne and weight comes off the legs (the switch opens). Some systems also use lock-out solenoids that are energised by ground power or a separate circuit. The key is that the squat switch is purely mechanical with no pilot action required—it's a passive safety system.
exam_tip: Squat switch prevents accidental gear retraction on the ground. Automatically released in flight. Know that on some aircraft you manually reset ground protection after landing.
key_facts: Squat switch mounted on main landing gear leg(s)
Switch closes when aircraft weight is on the landing gear
Closed squat switch blocks 'up' command to hydraulic system
Switch opens and protection releases when aircraft becomes airborne
Prevents inadvertent gear retraction on the ground
System is automatic—no pilot action required
Some aircraft require manual reset of ground-sensing system
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Explain the effects of the application of a load to an airframe structure (stress and strain).
concept: When a load is applied to an airframe structure, the material experiences stress (force per unit area, measured in Pascals or psi) and strain (deformation as a proportion of original dimension). Within the elastic limit, the material returns to its original shape when the load is removed—this is normal flight loading. Beyond the elastic limit, permanent deformation occurs (plastic deformation). Beyond the ultimate stress, the material fails. Helicopter airframes experience complex loading patterns including vibration from the rotor system, landing impacts, and manoeuvre loads. Understanding stress-strain relationships helps pilots recognise why load limits and manoeuvre limits exist in the flight manual.
exam_tip: Stress = force/area. Strain = deformation/original dimension. Elastic limit = returns to shape. Beyond elastic = permanent deformation. Beyond ultimate = failure.
key_facts: Stress = force per unit area
Strain = deformation / original dimension
Elastic limit: material returns to original shape
Plastic deformation: permanent change beyond elastic limit
Ultimate stress: material failure point
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between bending, tensile, compression, torsional and shear loads.
concept: Aircraft structures experience five main load types. Bending loads cause a structure to curve (like a rotor blade bending upward under lift). Tensile loads pull and stretch a member (like control cables in tension). Compression loads push and shorten a member (like landing gear struts during landing). Torsional loads twist a structure around its axis (like torque on the tail boom from main rotor reaction). Shear loads act parallel to a surface trying to slide one part past another (like rivets holding skin panels). Most helicopter components experience combined loading—the main rotor mast simultaneously handles bending, torsion, and shear.
exam_tip: Five load types: bending (curves), tensile (stretches), compression (shortens), torsion (twists), shear (slides). Helicopter components usually experience combined loads.
key_facts: Bending: structure curves under load
Tensile: pulling/stretching force
Compression: pushing/shortening force
Torsion: twisting around axis
Shear: parallel sliding force
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between bending, tensile, compression, torsional and shear loads.
concept: Different loads stress materials in different ways. Tensile loading pulls the material apart—think of hanging a weight from a wire, stretching it lengthwise. Compression loading pushes it together—think of standing on a can, squashing it. Bending combines both: the top surface goes into compression while the bottom goes into tension (both happen simultaneously). Torsional loading twists the material—like wringing out a wet towel—creating shear stress throughout. Shear loading slides one part past another—like cutting paper with scissors, where the blades create opposing forces side-by-side. In an aircraft, wing bending creates tension and compression. The fuselage resists torsion from aileron input. Landing loads create complex combinations of all five. Engineering designs each structural element to resist its dominant loading type.
exam_tip: Know the five types visually: tensile pulls, compression squashes, bending combines both, torsion twists, shear slides. Real structures usually see multiple types.
key_facts: Tensile loading: pulling forces that stretch material
Compression loading: pushing forces that squeeze material
Bending loading: combination of tension and compression through thickness
Torsional loading: twisting forces that create shear throughout
Shear loading: opposing forces that slide material layers past each other
Bending is most common in wings and fuselage
Torsion resisted by fuselage tube and wing structure
Shear develops at attachment points and around cutouts
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the various construction styles of a fuselage.
concept: Fuselages come in three main styles. The oldest is fabric-covered wooden or metal frame—think Aviat Husky or Cessna 180. Light, but labour-intensive and requires regular fabric replacement. Then there's tube-and-fabric: a welded steel-tube frame covered in doped fabric—very damage-tolerant because damage's visible and easily repaired. Most modern light aircraft use stressed-skin construction: an aluminium skin that carries loads like a monocoque (think eggshell), supported by internal stiffeners, ribs, and stringers. The skin itself is structural—it resists bending and torsion. This is heavier than frame-and-fabric but more efficient and durable. Some modern composites use carbon-fibre or glass-fibre in epoxy resin, combining light weight with high strength. Each style has trade-offs: fabric's light and repairable; tube's strong and simple; stressed-skin's efficient and long-lasting; composites are high-tech but tricky to repair.
exam_tip: Most light twins are stressed-skin aluminium. Know that skin damage in stressed-skin aircraft is serious because the skin carries load.
key_facts: Fabric-covered frame: light but requires regular maintenance
Tube-and-fabric: damage-tolerant and visibly inspectable
Stressed-skin (monocoque/semi-monocoque): skin carries primary loads
Aluminium alloy is standard for stressed-skin construction
Internal stiffeners (stringers, ribs) prevent buckling of skin
Composite materials offer high strength-to-weight but complex repairs
Each style has different inspection and repair requirements
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the common types of wing construction.
concept: Wing structure has to resist massive bending forces—lift pushes up, weight pulls down—so the wing's basically a cantilever beam. Most light twins have an aluminium alloy stressed-skin wing with a main spar (the beefy I-beam-shaped box that runs front-to-back) and a rear spar. The space between the spars is where fuel tanks live (integral fuel tanks in the wing itself). The leading edge's a rounded tube that provides shape and some stiffness. The bottom and top skins are stressed-skin—they carry bending loads. Ribs perpendicular to the spars maintain aerodynamic shape and prevent skin buckling. Stringers or stiffeners run spanwise (tip to root) to resist compression in the upper skin during bending. Wing struts (if any) or internal bracing help cantilever-supported wings handle bending. The root attaches to the fuselage at two or four main attachment points, passing all flight loads back into the fuselage structure.
exam_tip: Spar's the key structural element. Main spar = primary bending resistance. Fuel lives between spars. Ribs keep shape; stringers prevent buckling.
key_facts: Main spar is primary bending-resistance structure (I-beam cross-section)
Rear spar provides additional stiffness and carries some shear
Wing skin is stressed-skin, carries bending stresses
Ribs perpendicular to spars maintain aerodynamic shape and prevent buckling
Stringers/stiffeners run spanwise to resist compression buckling
Integral fuel tanks occupy spar cavity
Wing root attachment points transfer flight loads to fuselage
Strut bracing or internal structure supports cantilever bending
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the construction of: (a) the tailplane
concept: The tailplane (horizontal stabiliser) is a small aerofoil at the rear of the aircraft that provides longitudinal (pitch) stability. In most light aircraft, it generates a downward force to balance the nose-down pitching moment of the wings. It typically has a fixed section (stabiliser) and a movable section (elevator). It's constructed with spars, ribs, and stressed skin, similar to the wing.
exam_tip: The tailplane provides pitch stability -- usually generates a downward force to balance the wing's pitching moment.
key_facts: Provides longitudinal (pitch) stability
Usually generates a downward balancing force in flight
Constructed with spars, ribs, and stressed skin similar to the wing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the construction of: (b) the vertical fin
concept: The vertical fin (vertical stabiliser) is the fixed vertical surface at the tail that provides directional (yaw) stability. It works like a weathervane -- if the aircraft yaws, the fin generates a side force that returns the nose to the original heading. It carries the rudder (the movable control surface). Construction is similar to the tailplane with spars, ribs, and stressed skin.
exam_tip: The vertical fin provides yaw stability -- it acts like a weathervane to keep the aircraft tracking straight.
key_facts: Provides directional (yaw) stability
Acts as a weathervane, restoring the aircraft to straight flight
Carries the rudder for pilot-controlled yaw input
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Briefly describe the construction of: (c) control surfaces
concept: Control surfaces (ailerons, elevator, rudder) are hinged panels on the trailing edges of the wings and tail surfaces. They deflect into the airflow to create aerodynamic forces that roll, pitch, or yaw the aircraft. Constructed with a spar at the hinge line, ribs for shape, and skin covering. Some have mass balances to prevent flutter and trim tabs for fine adjustment.
exam_tip: Control surfaces deflect airflow to create rolling, pitching, or yawing forces. Mass balances prevent dangerous flutter.
key_facts: Ailerons: roll; Elevator: pitch; Rudder: yaw
Hinged at the trailing edge of wings and tail surfaces
Mass balances prevent flutter; trim tabs allow fine control adjustment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of a freewheeling unit.
concept: The freewheeling unit (also called an overrunning clutch or sprag clutch) is one of the most critical components in a helicopter's drive system. Its job is simple but vital: it allows the main rotor to turn freely—to 'freewheel'—if the engine stops or slows below rotor RPM. Without it, a dead engine would drag the rotor to a stop. With it, the rotor disconnects automatically and keeps spinning under its own inertia, which is exactly what you need for autorotation. The mechanism uses rollers or sprags wedged between inner and outer races. When the engine drives faster than the rotor (normal flight), the rollers lock and transmit power. When the engine slows below rotor speed (engine failure or throttle chop), the rollers disengage and the rotor spins freely. There's no pilot action required—it's automatic and instantaneous. On the R22, you can feel the freewheeling unit disengage during practice autorotations when you roll the throttle off. The rotor RPM holds while engine RPM drops. That's the freewheeling unit doing its job.
exam_tip: Freewheeling unit = allows rotor to turn independently when engine fails. Automatic, no pilot action. Uses rollers/sprags. Essential for autorotation. Engine drives faster than rotor = locked (power on). Rotor faster than engine = disengaged (autorotation).
key_facts: Freewheeling unit: also called overrunning clutch or sprag clutch. Allows main rotor to freewheel when engine stops. Uses rollers or sprags between inner and outer races. Automatic engagement/disengagement. Engine RPM > rotor RPM = locked (normal flight). Rotor RPM > engine RPM = disengaged (autorotation). Essential for autorotation entry. No pilot action required.
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5 Rev 38; Principles of Helicopter Flight, W.J. Wagtendonk, 2nd Ed.

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of a swash plate assembly.
concept: The swashplate is one of the most clever mechanical systems in a helicopter. It's a complex assembly of plates and bearings that converts the pilot's cyclic stick input into individual blade pitch changes, enabling all helicopter flight control. Here's how it works: the inner swashplate is fixed to the mast and rotates with the rotor. The outer swashplate doesn't rotate but can tilt in any direction and move up and down. When the pilot moves the cyclic stick, control linkages push or pull on the outer swashplate, tilting it. As the rotor blades pass over the tilted swashplate, each blade rides up and down on it, changing its pitch angle. A blade passing over the high point of the tilted swashplate increases its pitch, while a blade passing over the low point decreases pitch. This cyclic pitch change every rotation gives you cyclic control—it's why the helicopter can move forward, backward, or sideways. The vertical movement of the outer swashplate controls collective pitch (all blades together), which controls vertical power and lift.
exam_tip: The swashplate converts stick input to blade pitch changes. Cyclic (tilted swashplate) controls direction. Collective (vertical movement) controls lift. It's complex but essential—understand the basic principle.
key_facts: Converts pilot stick input to blade pitch changes
